explanation: Direct self-address is reported as unspoken thought; the content enacts the disruption of her joy.
evidence: Reporting frame “she said to herself”, Exclamatory inner cry “Oh this horror!”

passage: Yet, after all, how much she owed to him later. Always when she thought of him she thought of their quarrels for some reason--because she wanted his good opinion so much, perhaps. She owed him words: “sentimental,” “civilised”; they started up every day of her life as if he guarded her. A book was sentimental; an attitude to life sentimental. “Sentimental,” perhaps she was to be thinking of the past. What would he think, she wondered, when he came back? That she had grown older? Would he say that, or would she see him thinking when he came back, that she had grown older? It was true. Since her illness she had turned almost white.
explanation: Clarissa’s associative reflections and self-questioning about Peter are rendered in her register without heavy narration.
evidence: Rhetorical self-questions (“What would he think… Would he say…?”), Character-colored diction (“owed him words… sentimental… civilised”)

passage: She was not old yet. She had just broken into her fifty-second year. Months and months of it were still untouched. June, July, August! Each still remained almost whole, and, as if to catch the falling drop, Clarissa (crossing to the dressing-table) plunged into the very heart of the moment, transfixed it, there--the moment of this June morning on which was the pressure of all the other mornings, seeing the glass, the dressing-table, and all the bottles afresh, collecting the whole of her at one point (as she looked into the glass), seeing the delicate pink face of the woman who was that very night to give a party; of Clarissa Dalloway; of herself.
explanation: An enacted present-tense self-collection: Clarissa asserts youthfulness and ‘plunges’ into the moment; imagery and cadence simulate composure and concentration.
evidence: List-like temporal focus (“June, July, August!”), Self-reflexive seeing (“seeing… collecting the whole of her at one point”), Parenthetical stage asides integrated into an interior framing

passage: How many million times she had seen her face, and always with the same imperceptible contraction! She pursed her lips when she looked in the glass. It was to give her face point. That was her self--pointed; dartlike; definite. That was her self when some effort, some call on her to be her self, drew the parts together, she alone knew how different, how incompatible and composed so for the world only into one centre, one diamond, one woman who sat in her drawing-room and made a meeting-point, a radiancy no doubt in some dull lives, a refuge for the lonely to come to, perhaps; she had helped young people, who were grateful to her; had tried to be the same always, never showing a sign of all the other sides of her--faults, jealousies, vanities, suspicions, like this of Lady Bruton not asking her to lunch; which, she thought (combing her hair finally), is utterly base!
explanation: Sustained self-definition in Clarissa’s idiom; metaphor of the ‘diamond’ self and evaluative aside capture her inner consolidation and a flash of pique.
evidence: Metaphorical self-description (“one diamond… a radiancy… a refuge”), Parenthetical insertion with present action (“(combing her hair finally)”), Evaluative outburst “is utterly base!”

passage: Now, where was her dress?
explanation: A deictic, present-tense internal question marking Clarissa’s momentary thought.
evidence: Deictic adverb “Now”, Interrogative phrased as inner question

passage: She would mend it. Her maids had too much to do. She would wear it to-night. She would take her silks, her scissors, her--what was it?--her thimble, of course, down into the drawing-room, for she must also write, and see that things generally were more or less in order.
explanation: A plan-like inner monologue; the broken retrieval “her—what was it?—her thimble” captures the texture of ongoing thought.
evidence: Volitional future statements (“She would…” x3), Parenthetical dash-aside for word retrieval

passage: Strange, she thought, pausing on the landing, and assembling that diamond shape, that single person, strange how a mistress knows the very moment, the very temper of her house!
explanation: Explicit ‘she thought’ frames Clarissa’s inner observation with her recurring ‘diamond’ self-image.
evidence: Reporting verb: “she thought”, Figurative reprise (“diamond shape… that single person”)

passage: All was for the party.
explanation: A compressed inner summation of the house’s activity from Clarissa’s perspective.
evidence: Compression and evaluative summation (“All was…”), Aligns with preceding inner attention to house’s ‘temper’

passage: (And Lucy, coming into the drawing-room with her tray held out, put the giant candlesticks on the mantelpiece, the silver casket in the middle, turned the crystal dolphin towards the clock. They would come; they would stand; they would talk in the mincing tones which she could imitate, ladies and gentlemen. Of all, her mistress was loveliest--mistress of silver, of linen, of china, for the sun, the silver, doors off their hinges, Rumpelmayer’s men, gave her a sense, as she laid the paper-knife on the inlaid table, of something achieved. Behold! Behold! she said, speaking to her old friends in the baker’s shop, where she had first seen service at Caterham, prying into the glass. She was Lady Angela, attending Princess Mary, when in came Mrs. Dalloway.)
explanation: Parentheses mark a switch into Lucy’s daydream: she imagines the coming guests and fantasizes her role (“She was Lady Angela…”), rendered in her own registers and rhythms.
evidence: Conditional/imagined sequence about guests (“They would come; they would stand…”), Self-casting fantasy (“She was Lady Angela…”), Parenthetical isolates the consciousness shift

passage: and thank you, thank you, she went on saying (sitting down on the sofa with her dress over her knees, her scissors, her silks), thank you, thank you, she went on saying in gratitude to her servants generally for helping her to be like this, to be what she wanted, gentle, generous-hearted.
explanation: An unspoken mantra of thanks that continues under the surface; the repetition enacts the affective state of gratitude and calm.
evidence: Repetition of “thank you, thank you” as subvocalized refrain, Affective gloss “in gratitude… to be what she wanted”

passage: And then this dress of hers--where was the tear? and now her needle to be threaded.
explanation: Telegraphic, task-focused inner directives and questions register as Clarissa’s immediate thought stream.
evidence: Elliptical structure (“and now her needle to be threaded”), Interrogative thought (“where was the tear?”)

passage: if ever I have a moment, thought Clarissa (but never would she have a moment any more), I shall go and see her at Ealing.
explanation: Explicitly tagged thought, with a parenthetical counter-thought that undercuts her plan—capturing inner contradiction.
evidence: Reporting tag “thought Clarissa”, Parenthetical negation “(but never would she have a moment any more)”

passage: Quiet descended on her, calm, content, as her needle, drawing the silk smoothly to its gentle pause, collected the green folds together and attached them, very lightly, to the belt.
explanation: Narrator describes Clarissa’s felt calm by synchronizing prose rhythm and tactile imagery with the action of sewing—formally enacting the mood.
evidence: Affective naming + enactment (“Quiet descended… calm, content… drawing the silk smoothly… gentle pause”), Rhythmic, sensuous phrasing simulates composure

passage: So on a summer’s day waves collect, overbalance, and fall; collect and fall; and the whole world seems to be saying “that is all” more and more ponderously, until even the heart in the body which lies in the sun on the beach says too, That is all. Fear no more, says the heart. Fear no more, says the heart, committing its burden to some sea, which sighs collectively for all sorrows, and renews, begins, collects, lets fall. And the body alone listens to the passing bee; the wave breaking; the dog barking, far away barking and barking.
explanation: The passage’s rhythm, repetition, and imagery enact a state of tranquil acceptance; the ‘heart’ speaks and the syntax mimics the sea’s motion.
evidence: Repetitions and cadences (“collect, overbalance, and fall; collect and fall”) mirror calm cycles, Personified inner voice (“Fear no more, says the heart”), Sensory drift to ambient sounds enacting absorption
notes: Though untagged to Clarissa explicitly, it follows directly from her calming and is conventionally read as aligned with her present state.

passage: “Who can--what can,” asked Mrs. Dalloway (thinking it was outrageous to be interrupted at eleven o’clock on the morning of the day she was giving a party), hearing a step on the stairs.
explanation: Parenthetical explicitly renders Clarissa’s inner protest while the outer sentence reports her spoken interjection.
evidence: Parenthetical: “(thinking it was outrageous…)”, Temporal self-reference to her morning and party—her evaluative frame

passage: for a single second she could not remember what he was called! so surprised she was to see him, so glad, so shy, so utterly taken aback to have Peter Walsh come to her unexpectedly in the morning! (She had not read his letter.)
explanation: A rush of interior reactions rendered in exclamatory FID; the parenthesis adds an inner factual aside clarifying her surprise.
evidence: Exclamatory cadence and piling adjectives (“so surprised… so glad, so shy…”), Parenthetical fact-check aside

passage: She’s grown older, he thought, sitting down. I shan’t tell her anything about it, he thought, for she’s grown older. She’s looking at me, he thought, a sudden embarrassment coming over him, though he had kissed her hands.

passage: Exactly the same, thought Clarissa; the same queer look; the same check suit; a little out of the straight his face is, a little thinner, dryer, perhaps, but he looks awfully well, and just the same.
explanation: Tagged thought in Clarissa’s rapid appraisals and corrections renders her inner seeing.
evidence: Reporting tag “thought Clarissa”, Paratactic, subjective appraisals (“awfully well… just the same”)

passage: That’s so like him, she thought.
explanation: A brief, explicitly tagged inner reaction to Peter’s gesture.
evidence: Reporting tag “she thought”, Evaluative idiom “That’s so like him”

passage: He’s very well dressed, thought Clarissa; yet he always criticises _me_.
explanation: Tagged inner judgment, including first-person pronoun within FID and typographic emphasis.

passage: Here she is mending her dress; mending her dress as usual, he thought; here she’s been sitting all the time I’ve been in India; mending her dress; playing about; going to parties; running to the House and back and all that, he thought, growing more and more irritated, more and more agitated, for there’s nothing in the world so bad for some women as marriage, he thought; and politics; and having a Conservative husband, like the admirable Richard. So it is, so it is, he thought, shutting his knife with a snap.
explanation: Sustained tagged inner tirade; repetition and accretive phrasing simulate rising agitation.
evidence: Multiple “he thought” tags anchoring unspoken content, Repetitive cadence (“mending her dress… mending her dress… So it is, so it is”) enacts irritation

passage: Now of course, thought Clarissa, he’s enchanting! perfectly enchanting! Now I remember how impossible it was ever to make up my mind--and why did I make up my mind--not to marry him? she wondered, that awful summer?

passage: Of course I did, thought Peter; it almost broke my heart too, he thought; and was overcome with his own grief, which rose like a moon looked at from a terrace, ghastly beautiful with light from the sunken day. I was more unhappy than I’ve ever been since, he thought.
explanation: Tagged inner recollection intensified by metaphor; the rhythm and image of the ‘moon’ enact an upwelling of grief.
evidence: Repeated “he thought” tags surrounding unspoken content, Extended metaphor (“grief… rose like a moon… ghastly beautiful…”)

passage: For why go back like this to the past? he thought. Why make him think of it again? Why make him suffer, when she had tortured him so infernally? Why?
explanation: Series of rhetorical self-questions in Peter’s mind, tagged and staccato, simulate his avoidance and pain.
evidence: Tag: “he thought”, Rapid-fire interrogatives ending in a clipped “Why?”

passage: For she was a child, throwing bread to the ducks, between her parents, and at the same time a grown woman coming to her parents who stood by the lake, holding her life in her arms which, as she neared them, grew larger and larger in her arms, until it became a whole life, a complete life, which she put down by them and said, “This is what I have made of it! This!” And what had she made of it? What, indeed? sitting there sewing this morning with Peter.
explanation: A vivid inner tableau of self and parents around the lake renders Clarissa’s self-appraisal in figurative, subjective imagery, concluding with self-questions.

passage: Yes,” said Peter. “Yes, yes, yes,” he said, as if she drew up to the surface something which positively hurt him as it rose. Stop! Stop! he wanted to cry. For he was not old; his life was not over; not by any means. He was only just past fifty. Shall I tell her, he thought, or not? He would like to make a clean breast of it all. But she is too cold, he thought; sewing, with her scissors; Daisy would look ordinary beside Clarissa. And she would think me a failure, which I am in their sense, he thought; in the Dalloways’ sense.
explanation: Peter’s interior is rendered with direct imperatives to self (“Stop! Stop!”) and tagged cogitations; the syntax enacts vacillation and self-doubt.
evidence: Untagged inner exclamation (“Stop! Stop!”) folded into tagged thoughts, Alternation of desire and inhibition (“Shall I tell her… But she is too cold…”)

passage: I detest the smugness of the whole affair he thought; Richard’s doing, not Clarissa’s; save that she married him. (Here Lucy came into the room, carrying silver, more silver, but charming, slender, graceful she looked, he thought, as she stooped to put it down.) And this has been going on all the time! he thought; week after week; Clarissa’s life; while I--he thought; and at once everything seemed to radiate from him; journeys; rides; quarrels; adventures; bridge parties; love affairs; work; work, work! and he took out his knife quite openly--his old horn-handled knife which Clarissa could swear he had had these thirty years--and clenched his fist upon it.
explanation: A crescendo of Peter’s inner resentment, with a parenthetical interior aside marking his perception of Lucy; the accretive catalogue and repetitions enact agitation.
evidence: Tagging “he thought” throughout anchors the inner tirade, Parenthetical consciousness-shift inserting his appraisal of Lucy, Repetitions and list-like build (“work; work, work!”; “journeys; rides; quarrels…”)

passage: What an extraordinary habit that was, Clarissa thought; always playing with a knife. Always making one feel, too, frivolous; empty-minded; a mere silly chatterbox, as he used. But I too, she thought, and, taking up her needle, summoned, like a Queen whose guards have fallen asleep and left her unprotected (she had been quite taken aback by this visit--it had upset her) so that any one can stroll in and have a look at her where she lies with the brambles curving over her, summoned to her help the things she did; the things she liked; her husband; Elizabeth; her self, in short, which Peter hardly knew now, all to come about her and beat off the enemy.
explanation: Clarissa’s inner counter-move takes on a rhetorical, self-fortifying arc; martial imagery and parenthetical emotional admission enact a defensive soliloquy-like stance.
evidence: Tagged “Clarissa thought” plus first-person insertion (“But I too, she thought”) as self-assertion, Extended protective metaphor (“like a Queen… guards… beat off the enemy”), Parenthetical admission of being ‘taken aback’
notes: Borderline soliloquy in rhetorical organization, but framed in tagged third-person thought; classified as indirect interior monologue with soliloquy-like texture.

passage: What an extraordinary habit that was, Clarissa thought; always playing with a knife. Always making one feel, too, frivolous; empty-minded; a mere silly chatterbox, as he used. But I too, she thought, and, taking up her needle, summoned, like a Queen whose guards have fallen asleep and left her unprotected (she had been quite taken aback by this visit--it had upset her) so that any one can stroll in and have a look at her where she lies with the brambles curving over her, summoned to her help the things she did; the things she liked; her husband; Elizabeth; her self, in short, which Peter hardly knew now, all to come about her and beat off the enemy.

passage: So before a battle begins, the horses paw the ground; toss their heads; the light shines on their flanks; their necks curve. So Peter Walsh and Clarissa, sitting side by side on the blue sofa, challenged each other. His powers chafed and tossed in him. He assembled from different quarters all sorts of things; praise; his career at Oxford; his marriage, which she knew nothing whatever about; how he had loved; and altogether done his job.
explanation: Narrator uses extended simile and psycho-narration to describe the characters’ mental ‘battle’ and Peter’s inner marshalling of memories.
evidence: Comparative set-piece: "So before a battle begins, the horses...", Psycho-narration: "His powers chafed and tossed in him. He assembled..."
notes: Focus is on Peter’s mental arousal; Clarissa is included only as counterpart in the set-up.

passage: “Millions of things!” he exclaimed, and, urged by the assembly of powers which were now charging this way and that and giving him the feeling at once frightening and extremely exhilarating of being rushed through the air on the shoulders of people he could no longer see, he raised his hands to his forehead.
explanation: Though anchored to Peter’s speech, the sentence primarily narrates his subjective feeling with metaphorical language that enacts his excited inner state.
evidence: Affective psycho-narration: "giving him the feeling... frightening and extremely exhilarating", Metaphoric enactment: "rushed through the air on the shoulders of people he could no longer see"
notes: Dialogue triggers but the SOC content lies in the narrated inner sensation.

passage: “I am in love,” he said, not to her however, but to some one raised up in the dark so that you could not touch her but must lay your garland down on the grass in the dark.
explanation: Narrator frames Peter’s utterance as mentally addressed to an imagined figure, describing his interior orientation rather than external dialogue.
evidence: Narratorial framing: "not to her however, but to some one raised up in the dark", Subjective image: "lay your garland down on the grass in the dark"
notes: Mixed with spoken dialogue; SOC element is the narrated mental addressee.

passage: That he at his age should be sucked under in his little bow-tie by that monster! And there’s no flesh on his neck; his hands are red; and he’s six months older than I am!
explanation: Unframed exclamations and present-tense judgments voice Clarissa’s immediate, unspoken reactions in her idiom.

passage: but in her heart she felt, all the same, he is in love. He has that, she felt; he is in love.

evidence: Reporting tag: "she felt", Repetition of the thought content: "he is in love"

passage: But the indomitable egotism which for ever rides down the hosts opposed to it, the river which says on, on, on; even though, it admits, there may be no goal for us whatever, still on, on; this indomitable egotism charged her cheeks with colour; made her look very young; very pink; very bright-eyed as she sat with her dress upon her knee, and her needle held to the end of green silk, trembling a little.
explanation: Narrator interprets Clarissa’s inner drive through extended metaphor, describing how her egotism alters her affect and appearance.
evidence: Metaphoric psycho-narration: "the river which says on, on, on", Causal link to bodily affect: "charged her cheeks with colour"
notes: Focus is on inward propulsion enacted via rhythmic repetition (simulation).

passage: He was in love! Not with her. With some younger woman, of course.

passage: (All the same, he is in love, thought Clarissa.)

passage: There they are! he thought. Do what you like with them, Clarissa! There they are!

notes: Leans toward soliloquy in performance quality but remains tagged thought.

passage: And second by second it seemed to him that the wife of the Major in the Indian Army (his Daisy) and her two small children became more and more lovely as Clarissa looked at them; as if he had set light to a grey pellet on a plate and there had risen up a lovely tree in the brisk sea-salted air of their intimacy (for in some ways no one understood him, felt with him, as Clarissa did)--their exquisite intimacy.
explanation: Narrator renders Peter’s idealizing perception with sustained metaphor and parenthetical intrusions that gloss his intimacy with Clarissa.
evidence: Perceptual framing: "it seemed to him", Parenthetical self-justification: "(for in some ways no one understood him...)"

passage: She flattered him; she fooled him, thought Clarissa; shaping the woman, the wife of the Major in the Indian Army, with three strokes of a knife. What a waste! What a folly!
explanation: Clarissa’s judgments are given in her idiom with a brief tag; the knife image crystallizes her mental picture of Peter’s self-deception.
evidence: Tag: "thought Clarissa" amid character-diction clauses, Exclamatory verdicts: "What a waste! What a folly!"

passage: All his life long Peter had been fooled like that; first getting sent down from Oxford; next marrying the girl on the boat going out to India; now the wife of a Major in the Indian Army--thank Heaven she had refused to marry him! Still, he was in love; her old friend, her dear Peter, he was in love.
explanation: A rolling catalogue in Clarissa’s evaluative idiom recounts Peter’s life errors and her relief at refusing him, ending in concessive recognition of his love.
evidence: Character idiom: "thank Heaven she had refused to marry him!", No explicit narrator analysis beyond FID cadence

passage: For Heaven’s sake, leave your knife alone! she cried to herself in irrepressible irritation; it was his silly unconventionality, his weakness; his lack of the ghost of a notion what any one else was feeling that annoyed her, had always annoyed her; and now at his age, how silly!
explanation: An internal exclamation addressed to Peter is explicitly marked as self-directed thought; the syntax and exclamations simulate her rising annoyance.
evidence: Tag: "she cried to herself" indicates unspoken self-address, Affective diction and exclamations: "For Heaven’s sake... how silly!"

passage: I know all that, Peter thought; I know what I’m up against, he thought, running his finger along the blade of his knife, Clarissa and Dalloway and all the rest of them; but I’ll show Clarissa--
explanation: Tagged first-person thought, including an unfinished resolve, puts us directly in Peter’s mind as he steels himself against perceived opposition.
evidence: First-person content with tag: "I know all that, Peter thought", Broken-off vow: "but I’ll show Clarissa--"

passage: and then to his utter surprise, suddenly thrown by those uncontrollable forces thrown through the air, he burst into tears; wept; wept without the least shame, sitting on the sofa, the tears running down his cheeks.
explanation: Narrator reports Peter’s sudden emotional collapse, characterizing it as being ‘thrown by uncontrollable forces,’ a metaphorical framing of inner turmoil.
evidence: Psycho-narration: "to his utter surprise... uncontrollable forces", Rhythmic repetition: "wept; wept" enacts the breakdown
notes: Least interior of SOC types but still qualifies under Humphrey for narrated mental experience.

passage: before she could down the brandishing of silver flashing--plumes like pampas grass in a tropic gale in her breast, which, subsiding, left her holding his hand, patting his knee
explanation: Narrator renders Clarissa’s visceral surge and subsidence through kinetic imagery, simulating a wave of feeling in her ‘breast.’
evidence: Metaphor: "plumes like pampas grass in a tropic gale", Somatic focus: "in her breast... subsiding"
notes: Clause within a longer sentence; quoted verbatim as it represents the inner surge.

passage: If I had married him, this gaiety would have been mine all day!
explanation: A sudden, untagged inner exclamation registers as FID; the syntax enacts a flash of counterfactual longing.
evidence: No tag; immediate exclamatory articulation, Conditional self-projection: "If I had married him..."

passage: It was all over for her. The sheet was stretched and the bed narrow. She had gone up into the tower alone and left them blackberrying in the sun. explanation: Narrator externalizes Clarissa’s mood via emblematic images (narrow bed, shut door, tower); the parenthetical memory intrudes mid-flow.
evidence: Extended metaphoric set: "The sheet was stretched and the bed narrow... the tower... the door had shut", Parenthetical memory cue: "(once on Leith Hill, she remembered)"
notes: Transitions directly into an interior cry in the next clause.

passage: Richard, Richard! she cried, as a sleeper in the night starts and stretches a hand in the dark for help.
explanation: An unspoken interior cry addressed to Richard, framed by a simile of startled waking, conveys urgent need.
evidence: Self-contained interior address: "Richard, Richard! she cried", Simile of inner emergency: "as a sleeper... stretches a hand in the dark for help"
notes: Ambiguous whether vocalized or purely inward; functionally an interior outcry.

passage: He has left me; I am alone for ever, she thought, folding her hands upon her knee.
explanation: Tagged interior declaration articulates Clarissa’s absolute loneliness; the parataxis heightens affect.
evidence: Tag: "she thought", Balanced, emphatic clauses: "He has left me; I am alone for ever"

passage: Take me with you, Clarissa thought impulsively, as if he were starting directly upon some great voyage;

passage: and then, next moment, it was as if the five acts of a play that had been very exciting and moving were now over and she had lived a lifetime in them and had run away, had lived with Peter, and it was now over.
explanation: Narrator depicts Clarissa’s sense of compressed life and completion via theatrical metaphor, simulating the ebb of heightened emotion.
evidence: Framing: "it was as if the five acts of a play... were now over", Repetition: "it was now over" marks emotional resolution

passage: And it was awfully strange, he thought, how she still had the power, as she came tinkling, rustling, still had the power as she came across the room, to make the moon, which he detested, rise at Bourton on the terrace in the summer sky.

evidence: Tag: "he thought", Memory-perception fusion: "to make the moon... rise at Bourton"

passage: Remember my party, remember my party, said Peter Walsh as he stepped down the street, speaking to himself rhythmically, in time with the flow of the sound, the direct downright sound of Big Ben striking the half-hour.

evidence: Parenthetical isolation, Formulaic sensory image: "leaden circles dissolved"

passage: Oh these parties, he thought; Clarissa’s parties. Why does she give these parties, he thought.

evidence: Tags: "he thought", Character-voice refrain: "these parties... Clarissa’s parties"

passage: Only one person in the world could be as he was, in love. And there he was, this fortunate man, himself, reflected in the plate-glass window of a motor-car manufacturer in Victoria Street. explanation: Un-tagged but character-idiomatic FID moves associatively from self-recognition to the plate-glass reflection to a sweep of India memories.
evidence: Self-referential phrasing: "he, Peter Walsh", Associative list: "All India lay behind him; plains, mountains; epidemics of cholera..."

passage: Clarissa had grown hard, he thought; and a trifle sentimental into the bargain, he suspected, looking at the great motor-cars capable of doing--how many miles on how many gallons?

evidence: Tags: "he thought" / "he suspected", Rhetorical question reflecting Peter’s own interests

passage: (Still the last tremors of the great booming voice shook the air round him; the half-hour; still early; only half-past eleven still.)

passage: For he understood young people; he liked them.

evidence: Character-centric generalization: "he understood young people", No narratorial hedge; assertion fits Peter’s self-image

passage: There was always something cold in Clarissa, he thought. She had always, even as a girl, a sort of timidity, which in middle age becomes conventionality, and then it’s all up, it’s all up, he thought, looking rather drearily into the glassy depths, and wondering whether by calling at that hour he had annoyed her; overcome with shame suddenly at having been a fool; wept; been emotional; told her everything, as usual, as usual.
explanation: Tagged interior appraisal of Clarissa shifts into Peter’s self-reproach; the piling parataxis enacts his cringing recollection.
evidence: Tags: "he thought", Paratactic self-blame: "a fool; wept; been emotional; told her everything"

evidence: Globalized subject: "silence... falls on the mind", Metaphoric stasis: "Time flaps on the mast... Rigid, the skeleton of habit..."
notes: Though generalized, it tracks Peter’s immediate affective dip.

passage: Where there is nothing, Peter Walsh said to himself; feeling hollowed out, utterly empty within.

evidence: "said to himself" signals soliloquized thought, Affective phrasing: "utterly empty within"
notes: Borders on soliloquy; kept as interior monologue due to narrator tagging and lack of extended rhetorical arc.

passage: Clarissa refused me, he thought. He stood there thinking, Clarissa refused me.

evidence: Tags: "he thought" / "He stood there thinking", Repetition of the same clause

passage: like Clarissa herself, thought Peter Walsh, coming down the stairs on the stroke of the hour in white. It is Clarissa herself, he thought, with a deep emotion, and an extraordinarily clear, yet puzzling, recollection of her, as if this bell had come into the room years ago, where they sat at some moment of great intimacy, and had gone from one to the other and had left, like a bee with honey, laden with the moment.
explanation: Tagged thoughts map the bell’s sound onto a vivid memory of Clarissa, blending present stimulus with recollection.
evidence: Tags: "thought Peter Walsh" / "he thought", Sensory-memory metaphor: "like a bee with honey, laden with the moment"

passage: But what room? What moment? And why had he been so profoundly happy when the clock was striking?
explanation: Unframed rhetorical questions directly express Peter’s probing inner search for a precise memory.
evidence: Series of questions without tags, Immediate, searching tone characteristic of FID

passage: Then, as the sound of St. Margaret’s languished, he thought, She has been ill, and the sound expressed languor and suffering.

evidence: Tag: "he thought", Attribution of affect to sound: "the sound expressed languor and suffering"

passage: It was her heart, he remembered; and the sudden loudness of the final stroke tolled for death that surprised in the midst of life, Clarissa falling where she stood, in her drawing-room.

evidence: Memory tag: "he remembered", Imagined scene: "Clarissa falling where she stood, in her drawing-room"

passage: No! No! he cried. She is not dead! I am not old, he cried, and marched up Whitehall, as if there rolled down to him, vigorous, unending, his future.

evidence: First-person assertion: "I am not old", Performative exclamations: "No! No!... She is not dead!"

passage: He was not old, or set, or dried in the least.

passage: Still the future of civilisation lies, he thought, in the hands of young men like that; of young men such as he was, thirty years ago; with their love of abstract principles; getting books sent out to them all the way from London to a peak in the Himalayas; reading science; reading philosophy. explanation: Tagged interior generalization with enumerative cadence reveals Peter’s idealism and self-projection.
evidence: Tags: "he thought" (twice), Enumerative diction: "reading science; reading philosophy"

passage: It is, thought Peter Walsh, beginning to keep step with them, a very fine training.

evidence: Tag: "thought Peter Walsh", Embodied alignment: "beginning to keep step with them"

passage: I can’t keep up with them, Peter Walsh thought, as they marched up Whitehall, and sure enough, on they marched, past him, past every one, in their steady way, as if one will worked legs and arms uniformly, and life, with its varieties, its irreticences, had been laid under a pavement of monuments and wreaths and drugged into a stiff yet staring corpse by discipline. One had to respect it; one might laugh; but one had to respect it, he thought.
explanation: Tagged inner voice folds into an extended, image-rich judgment that enacts the hypnotic uniformity he observes and feels.
evidence: Tagging at start and end: "Peter Walsh thought" ... "he thought", Cinematic image: "life... drugged into a stiff yet staring corpse by discipline"

passage: There they go, thought Peter Walsh, pausing at the edge of the pavement; and all the exalted statues, Nelson, Gordon, Havelock, the black, the spectacular images of great soldiers stood looking ahead of them, as if they too had made the same renunciation (Peter Walsh felt he too had made it, the great renunciation), trampled under the same temptations, and achieved at length a marble stare.
explanation: Tagged FID ventriloquizes Peter’s projection onto the statues; the parenthetical registers a self-reflective intrusion about ‘the great renunciation.’
evidence: Tag: "thought Peter Walsh", Parenthetical inner aside: "(Peter Walsh felt he too had made it...)"

evidence: Character idiom and evaluative stance: "did not want for himself", Short emphatic follow-up: "He could respect it in boys."

passage: They don’t know the troubles of the flesh yet, he thought, as the marching boys disappeared in the direction of the Strand--all that I’ve been through, he thought, crossing the road, and standing under Gordon’s statue, Gordon whom as a boy he had worshipped; Gordon standing lonely with one leg raised and his arms crossed,--poor Gordon, he thought.
explanation: Successive tagged thoughts move from judgment of youth to self-reference and to a projected empathy with Gordon’s statue.
evidence: Multiple tags: "he thought" (thrice), Associative drift: boys → self → Gordon

passage: What is it? Where am I? And why, after all, does one do it? he thought, the divorce seeming all moonshine.

evidence: Series of questions without initial tag, Postposed tag: "he thought" anchoring the interiority

passage: And down his mind went flat as a marsh, and three great emotions bowled over him; understanding; a vast philanthropy; and finally, as if the result of the others, an irrepressible, exquisite delight; as if inside his brain by another hand strings were pulled, shutters moved, and he, having nothing to do with it, yet stood at the opening of endless avenues, down which if he chose he might wander.
explanation: Narrator renders Peter’s inner swell of emotions through similes and kinetic metaphors that formally enact the lift into delight.
evidence: Affective sequencing: "understanding... philanthropy... irrepressible, exquisite delight", Mechanistic inner images: "strings were pulled, shutters moved"

passage: He had not felt so young for years. He had escaped! was utterly free--as happens in the downfall of habit when the mind, like an unguarded flame, bows and bends and seems about to blow from its holding.

evidence: Exclamatory FID: "He had escaped! was utterly free", Simile of flame mimics buoyant instability
notes: The trailing generalization is narratorial but supports the simulated affect.

evidence: First-person content with tag: "I haven’t felt so young for years! thought Peter", Childlike image: "like a child who runs out of doors"

passage: But she’s extraordinarily attractive, he thought, as, walking across Trafalgar Square in the direction of the Haymarket, came a young woman who, as she passed Gordon’s statue, seemed, Peter Walsh thought (susceptible as he was), to shed veil after veil, until she became the very woman he had always had in mind; young, but stately; merry, but discreet; black, but enchanting.

evidence: Tags: "he thought" / "Peter Walsh thought", Constructive ideal: "until she became the very woman he had always had in mind"

passage: Straightening himself and stealthily fingering his pocket-knife he started after her to follow this woman, this excitement, which seemed even with its back turned to shed on him a light which connected them, which singled him out, as if the random uproar of the traffic had whispered through hollowed hands his name, not Peter, but his private name which he called himself in his own thoughts. “You,” she said, only “you,” saying it with her white gloves and her shoulders.

evidence: Imagined hailing: "whispered... his name, not Peter, but his private name", Projected speech: "“You,” she said, only “you,”"

passage: Then the thin long cloak which the wind stirred as she walked past Dent’s shop in Cockspur Street blew out with an enveloping kindness, a mournful tenderness, as of arms that would open and take the tired--

evidence: Personification in service of desire: "enveloping kindness... as of arms that would open", Trailing dash suggests ongoing fantasy

passage: But she’s not married; she’s young; quite young, thought Peter, the red carnation he had seen her wear as she came across Trafalgar Square burning again in his eyes and making her lips red.

evidence: Tag: "thought Peter", Sensory intensification: "burning again in his eyes... making her lips red"

evidence: Tags: "he wondered" / "he thought", Parenthetical meta-comment: "(for one must invent...)"

evidence: Conditional scenario-building: "Still if she stopped he would say... she would answer", Quoted imagined speech

evidence: Fragmentary directive syntax, Anchored to Peter’s immediate choice of direction
notes: Borderline between narration of movement and internal plan; nearby FID supports interior reading.

passage: His boots on the pavement struck out “no matter”; for it was early, still very early.
explanation: Narrator externalizes an inner refrain by mapping it onto auditory rhythm of footsteps; tells about consciousness via metaphor.
evidence: Quoted refrain “no matter” as inner motif, Metaphoric coupling of bodily rhythm and thought

passage: It was a splendid morning too.
explanation: A brief subjective appraisal aligned with Peter’s present perception without a reporting tag.
evidence: Subjective adjective “splendid”, Context of Peter’s walk and prior FID

passage: Like the pulse of a perfect heart, life struck straight through the streets.
explanation: Lyrical simile casting the city’s motion as a heartbeat is filtered through Peter’s sensibility; reads as his impressionistic thought.
evidence: Sensory-metaphoric image keyed to present perception, No narrator tag; diction departs from neutral reportage
notes: Some may read as narratorial set-piece; surrounding Peter-focused FID tips toward interior impression.

passage: There was no fumbling--no hesitation.
explanation: Telegraphic evaluative sentence aligns with Peter’s inner commentary on the city’s efficiency.
evidence: Elliptical, gnomic structure typical of FID, Continues the impressionistic appraisal sequence

passage: The girl, silk-stockinged, feathered, evanescent, but not to him particularly attractive (for he had had his fling), alighted.
explanation: External action intrudes, but the parenthetical supplies Peter’s evaluative stance; narrator reports his settled attitude.
evidence: Insert “not to him particularly attractive” grounds POV in Peter, Explanatory parenthesis “for he had had his fling” states inner disposition
notes: Mixed external description with psycho-narration; SOC component is in the aside.

passage: Admirable butlers, tawny chow dogs, halls laid in black and white lozenges with white blinds blowing, Peter saw through the opened door and approved of.
explanation: Narrator explicitly reports Peter’s approving reaction to sensory details; conventional psycho-narration of evaluation.
evidence: “Peter … approved of” signals mental appraisal, Dense sensory listing reflects subjective noticing

passage: A splendid achievement in its own way, after all, London; the season; civilisation.
explanation: Paratactic, elliptical commendation is in Peter’s idiom without tags; free indirect summation of attitude.
evidence: Telegraphic noun-phrase series linked by semicolons, Subjective evaluative lexis “splendid … after all”

passage: Coming as he did from a respectable Anglo-Indian family which for at least three generations had administered the affairs of a continent (it’s strange, he thought, what a sentiment I have about that, disliking India, and empire, and army as he did), there were moments when civilisation, even of this sort, seemed dear to him as a personal possession; moments of pride in England; in butlers; chow dogs; girls in their security.
explanation: Primarily FID (Peter’s valuations) intercut with a tagged, first-person parenthetical (“it’s strange … I have”), momentarily dropping the narrator frame inside the same sentence.
evidence: Embedded tag “he thought” with first-person “I have”, Character-colored listing of pride objects; subjective “seemed dear”
notes: Hybrid of indirect IM and a flash of direct-voiced thought within parentheses.

passage: Ridiculous enough, still there it is, he thought.
explanation: Brief tagged thought capturing Peter’s resigned concession.
evidence: Reporting tag “he thought”, Colloquial cadence “still there it is”

passage: And the doctors and men of business and capable women all going about their business, punctual, alert, robust, seemed to him wholly admirable, good fellows, to whom one would entrust one’s life, companions in the art of living, who would see one through.
explanation: Narrator articulates Peter’s generalized esteem; standard psycho-narration of his appraisal.
evidence: “seemed to him” flags interior evaluation, Generality of “to whom one would entrust one’s life” reflects inner judgment

passage: What with one thing and another, the show was really very tolerable; and he would sit down in the shade and smoke.
explanation: FID gives Peter’s summative mood (“very tolerable”) and intention (“he would sit down …”), voiced as his own planning.
evidence: Colloquial frame “What with one thing and another”, Decision phrased from his perspective without tag

passage: There was Regent’s Park. Yes.
explanation: Indexical notice plus self-affirmation typical of inner speech.
evidence: Deictic identification of place from his vantage, One-word assent “Yes.”

passage: As a child he had walked in Regent’s Park--odd, he thought, how the thought of childhood keeps coming back to me--the result of seeing Clarissa, perhaps; for women live much more in the past than we do, he thought.
explanation: Peter’s reflection on memory is rendered via FID and two explicit tags; inner voice surfaces as “me” within the thought.
evidence: Parenthetical “he thought” with first person “me”, Generalization “women live much more in the past” in his idiom
notes: Alternates psycho-narration and FID within one sentence.

passage: They attach themselves to places; and their fathers--a woman’s always proud of her father.
explanation: Untagged generalizations voiced as Peter’s beliefs; free indirect style.
evidence: Second-person plural/generic “They … a woman’s always …” reads as Peter’s maxim, Follows his tagged thought, sustaining POV

passage: Bourton was a nice place, a very nice place, but I could never get on with the old man, he thought.
explanation: Tagged thought contains first-person assertion inside third-person frame; a momentary drop into Peter’s direct voice.
evidence: Reporting tag “he thought”, First-person clause “I could never get on with the old man”

passage: There was quite a scene one night--an argument about something or other, what, he could not remember.
explanation: Narrator reports a lapse in Peter’s memory (“he could not remember”); focuses on mental content rather than action.
evidence: Explicit cognition report “he could not remember”, Recollection frame rather than current event

passage: Politics presumably.
explanation: A one-word inference in Peter’s voice without tag; free indirect conjecture.
evidence: Elliptical fragment of supposition, Continues from memory sentence; clearly Peter’s inference

passage: Yes, he remembered Regent’s Park; the long straight walk; the little house where one bought air-balls to the left; an absurd statue with an inscription somewhere or other.
explanation: Associative list of remembered details in Peter’s idiom; no reporting tag.
evidence: Initial affirmation “Yes” marks self-prompted recall, Enumerative, sensory recall consistent with inner recollection

passage: He did not want to be bothered (feeling a little drowsy as he did) by people asking him the time.
explanation: Narrator states Peter’s desire and bodily state; conventional psycho-narration about consciousness.
evidence: Negative volition “did not want to be bothered”, Parenthetical bodily feeling linked to mental state

passage: An elderly grey nurse, with a baby asleep in its perambulator--that was the best he could do for himself; sit down at the far end of the seat by that nurse.
explanation: Self-directed plan phrased as inner assessment of options; FID.
evidence: Evaluative phrasing “the best he could do for himself”, Imperative-like inner directive “sit down … by that nurse”

passage: She’s a queer-looking girl, he thought, suddenly remembering Elizabeth as she came into the room and stood by her mother.
explanation: Tagged inner remark launches a memory of Elizabeth; unspoken evaluation.
evidence: Reporting tag “he thought”, Direct present-tense evaluative phrasing “She’s a queer-looking girl”

passage: Grown big; quite grown-up, not exactly pretty; handsome rather; and she can’t be more than eighteen.
explanation: Staccato evaluative fragments continue Peter’s inner appraisal without tags; FID.
evidence: Telegraphic, paratactic descriptors, Present-tense certainty “she can’t be more than eighteen”

passage: Probably she doesn’t get on with Clarissa.
explanation: Speculative judgment in Peter’s voice; untagged thought.
evidence: Modal adverb “Probably”, Subjective inference about relationship dynamics

passage: “There’s my Elizabeth”--that sort of thing--why not “Here’s Elizabeth” simply?--trying to make out, like most mothers, that things are what they’re not.
explanation: Peter’s mind quotes Clarissa’s habitual phrase and comments on it; the inserted quotation marks signal a mental echo within FID.
evidence: Interior quotation of another’s words within thought, Rhetorical self-question “why not … simply?”
notes: Orthographic intrusion (quoted phrase) marks a shift within the flow of thought.

passage: She trusts to her charm too much, he thought.
explanation: Tagged inner judgment of Clarissa.
evidence: “he thought” reporting tag, Evaluative diction “trusts … too much”

passage: She overdoes it.
explanation: Untagged, clipped evaluative continuation of Peter’s thought; FID.
evidence: Telegraphic predicate-only sentence, Follows immediately after a tagged thought maintaining POV

passage: I shall try and get a word alone with Elizabeth to-night, he thought
explanation: Explicitly tagged first-person plan renders unspoken intention.
evidence: First-person “I shall …” with “he thought”, Embedded within sensory description as a mental intrusion

passage: odd shapes they take, he thought.
explanation: Tagged observation phrased as inner comment on cigar smoke rings.
evidence: Reporting tag “he thought”, Lowercase, fragmentary clause mirrors spontaneous aside

passage: A great brush swept smooth across his mind, sweeping across it moving branches, children’s voices, the shuffle of feet, and people passing, and humming traffic, rising and falling traffic. Down, down he sank into the plumes and feathers of sleep, sank, and was muffled over.
explanation: Narrative rhythm and imagery formally enact the onset of sleep—sensory residues wash over his mind as syntax slows and repeats (“Down, down … sank”).
evidence: Metaphoric sweep and accumulative sensory list simulate fading consciousness, Rhythmic repetition and soft imagery (“plumes and feathers … muffled”) enact somnolence
notes: Primary function is affective simulation; rendered via omniscient psycho-narration and impressionistic imagery.

passage:
By conviction an atheist perhaps, he is taken by surprise with moments of extraordinary exaltation. Nothing exists outside us except a state of mind, he thinks; a desire for solace, for relief, for something outside these miserable pigmies, these feeble, these ugly, these craven men and women. But if he can conceive of her, then in some sort she exists, he thinks, and advancing down the path with his eyes upon sky and branches he rapidly endows them with womanhood; sees with amazement how grave they become; how majestically, as the breeze stirs them, they dispense with a dark flutter of the leaves charity, comprehension, absolution, and then, flinging themselves suddenly aloft, confound the piety of their aspect with a wild carouse. Such are the visions which proffer great cornucopias full of fruit to the solitary traveller, or murmur in his ear like sirens lolloping away on the green sea waves, or are dashed in his face like bunches of roses, or rise to the surface like pale faces which fishermen flounder through floods to embrace. Such are the visions which ceaselessly float up, pace beside, put their faces in front of, the actual thing; often overpowering the solitary traveller and taking away from him the sense of the earth, the wish to return, and giving him for substitute a general peace, as if (so he thinks as he advances down the forest ride) all this fever of living were simplicity itself; and myriads of things merged in one thing; and this figure, made of sky and branches as it is, had risen from the troubled sea (he is elderly, past fifty now) as a shape might be sucked up out of the waves to shower down from her magnificent hands compassion, comprehension, absolution. So, he thinks, may I never go back to the lamplight; to the sitting-room; never finish my book; never knock out my pipe; never ring for Mrs. Turner to clear away; rather let me walk straight on to this great figure, who will, with a toss of her head, mount me on her streamers and let me blow to nothingness with the rest. Such are the visions. The solitary traveller is soon beyond the wood; and there, coming to the door with shaded eyes, possibly to look for his return, with hands raised, with white apron blowing, is an elderly woman who seems (so powerful is this infirmity) to seek, over a desert, a lost son; to search for a rider destroyed; to be the figure of the mother whose sons have been killed in the battles of the world. So, as the solitary traveller advances down the village street where the women stand knitting and the men dig in the garden, the evening seems ominous; the figures still; as if some august fate, known to them, awaited without fear, were about to sweep them into complete annihilation. Indoors among ordinary things, the cupboard, the table, the window-sill with its geraniums, suddenly the outline of the landlady, bending to remove the cloth, becomes soft with light, an adorable emblem which only the recollection of cold human contacts forbids us to embrace. She takes the marmalade; she shuts it in the cupboard.

“There is nothing more to-night, sir?”

But to whom does the solitary traveller make reply?
explanation: A generalized ‘solitary traveller’ allegory channels Peter’s visionary reverie during sleep; the narrator persistently mediates with “he thinks,” while lavish imagery and rhythmic clauses simulate exalted, peaceful transport.
evidence: Repeated “he thinks” anchors inner rumination despite a generalized subject, Surreal, constructed scenes (woman made of sky/branches) indicate constructive fantasy rather than memory
notes: Though framed as impersonal, this arises from Peter’s sleeping consciousness. Strong narratorial presence; hybrid affective simulation through form.

passage: He woke with extreme suddenness, saying to himself, “The death of the soul.”
explanation: Narration reports a silent self-address in direct quotation with an attribution; unspoken thought rendered within a frame.
evidence: “saying to himself” signals internal speech, Quoted content of thought “The death of the soul.”

passage: “The death of the soul.”
explanation: A bare, untagged quoted thought appears without mediation, reading as free direct thought resounding in his mind.
evidence: No reporting verb or tag in the immediate sentence, Repetition of the same inner phrase emphasizes mental echo
notes: Ambiguous whether spoken aloud; context suggests a resumed inner refrain after an out-loud “Lord, Lord!”. Classified as direct thought with caution.

passage: The words attached themselves to some scene, to some room, to some past he had been dreaming of. It became clearer; the scene, the room, the past he had been dreaming of.
explanation: Narrator describes the associative process by which a verbal fragment connects to a remembered scene; conventional psycho-narration of recall.
evidence: Abstract phrasing “attached themselves” for associative linkage, Gradual clarifying of memory content stated explicitly
notes: Explicitly depicts the mechanics of association rather than content alone.

passage: It was at Bourton that summer, early in the ’nineties, when he was so passionately in love with Clarissa.
explanation: Narrator frames the memory by reporting Peter’s past emotional state.
evidence: Temporal recall frame (“that summer… in the ’nineties”), Reported intensity of feeling “so passionately in love”

passage: It was very uncomfortable.
explanation: Succinct evaluative aside reads as Peter’s remembered discomfort; no reporting tag.
evidence: Subjective predicate “very uncomfortable”, Follows narrated social scene, offering Peter’s inner assessment

passage: He hadn’t blamed her for minding the fact, since in those days a girl brought up as she was, knew nothing, but it was her manner that annoyed him; timid; hard; something arrogant; unimaginative; prudish.
explanation: Recollection of judgments in Peter’s idiom; although third-person, diction and itemized epithets are his.
evidence: Evaluative catalogue “timid; hard; … prudish”, Contrastive structure (“hadn’t blamed … but it was her manner”) mirrors inner reasoning
notes: Narrator presence is felt but voice aligns with Peter’s remembered thought.

passage: He had said that instinctively, ticketing the moment as he used to do--the death of her soul.
explanation: Narrator comments on Peter’s mental habit of labeling moments and specifies the internal label given then.
evidence: Meta-cognitive “ticketing the moment as he used to do”, Attribution of an inner tag “the death of her soul”
notes: Preceded by the quoted phrase “The death of the soul.” which this sentence explains.

passage: It was as if she said to Peter--it was all aimed at him, he knew--“I know you thought me absurd about that woman just now; but see how extraordinarily sympathetic I am; see how I love my Rob!”
explanation: Peter’s interpretive projection of Clarissa’s gesture is rendered as if voicing her imagined words within his thought.
evidence: Parenthetical “he knew” signals inner conviction, Interior quotation dramatizes his inference
notes: Orthographic quotation stages a mental ventriloquy of Clarissa.

passage: They had always this queer power of communicating without words. She knew directly he criticised her. Then she would do something quite obvious to defend herself, like this fuss with the dog--but it never took him in, he always saw through Clarissa. Not that he said anything, of course; just sat looking glum. It was the way their quarrels often began.
explanation: Narrator summarizes Peter’s remembered relational dynamics and inner stance (seeing through her, saying nothing).
evidence: Abstracted summary of mutual cognition (“communicating without words”), Attribution of inner response (“never took him in … saw through”)

passage: At once he became extremely depressed. It all seemed useless--going on being in love; going on quarrelling; going on making it up,
explanation: Narrator reports a sudden mood shift, followed by FID listing of futility in Peter’s cadence.
evidence: “he became extremely depressed” psycho-narration, Paratactic, repetitive structure mirrors inner lament

passage: It was an awful evening! He grew more and more gloomy, not about that only; about everything.
explanation: Exclamatory evaluation and broadening gloom voiced as Peter’s remembered feeling.
evidence: Exclamation indicates inner emphasis, Subjective expansion “about everything”

passage: And he couldn’t see her; couldn’t explain to her; couldn’t have it out.
explanation: Anaphoric fragments render Peter’s frustrated inner refrain; no reporting tag.
evidence: Repetition of “couldn’t …”, Elliptical syntax typical of inner complaint

passage: There were always people about--she’d go on as if nothing had happened.
explanation: FID recounts his grievance in his idiom (“she’d go on …”), not the narrator’s.
evidence: Contracted colloquial “she’d go on”, Evaluative “as if nothing had happened”

passage: That was the devilish part of her--this coldness, this woodenness, something very profound in her, which he had felt again this morning talking to her; an impenetrability.
explanation: Peter’s vehement appraisal with metaphorical descriptors is voiced in FID.
evidence: Evaluative epithet “devilish” and metaphor “woodenness”, Self-reference to present morning links past and present feeling

passage: Yet Heaven knows he loved her.
explanation: Exclamatory self-justification in Peter’s idiom; untagged thought.
evidence: Formulaic oath “Heaven knows” typical of inner emphasis, Counterpoint to prior criticism indicates self-address

passage: She had some queer power of fiddling on one’s nerves, turning one’s nerves to fiddle-strings, yes.
explanation: Metaphoric, rhythmically heightened complaint voiced as Peter’s thought; ends with self-confirming “yes.”
evidence: Striking image “fiddle-strings” enacts felt irritability, Additive “yes” as inner tic

passage: He had gone in to dinner rather late, from some idiotic idea of making himself felt,
explanation: Narrator reports action but embeds Peter’s self-reproach (“idiotic idea”) as inner commentary.
evidence: Self-evaluative phrase within narration, Motivational introspection (“making himself felt”)

passage: He sat down beside her, and couldn’t speak. Everything seemed to race past him; he just sat there, eating.
explanation: Narrator conveys Peter’s inner incapacity and perceptual overwhelm; syntax slows and generalizes to enact stupor.
evidence: Psycho-narration “couldn’t speak”, Perceptual distortion “Everything seemed to race past him”
notes: Mild simulation through pacing and generalization.

passage: He had a sudden revelation.
explanation: Narrator names a discrete inner event (revelation) rather than external action.
evidence: Explicit mental-event labeling, Precedes quoted inner speech

passage: “She will marry that man,” he said to himself.

evidence: Direct quotation of thought content, Tag “he said to himself”

passage: He was a prey to revelations at that time. This one--that she would marry Dalloway--was blinding--overwhelming at the moment.

evidence: Meta-comment on frequency of inner events (“prey to revelations”), Affective adjectives “blinding—overwhelming”

passage: There was a sort of--how could he put it?--a sort of ease in her manner to him; something maternal; something gentle.

passage: Yet even then he admired her for it. He admired her courage; her social instinct; he admired her power of carrying things through.
explanation: Peter’s admiring counter-thought is voiced via FID, with emphatic anaphora.
evidence: Repetition “He admired …” mimics inner emphasis, Evaluative content focused on her qualities

passage: But he meant her to feel it. He would have done anything to hurt her after seeing her with Dalloway.
explanation: Narrator articulates Peter’s intention and vindictive impulse—inner motives rather than actions.

passage: And he had a feeling that they were all gathered together in a conspiracy against him--laughing and talking--behind his back.

passage: Never, never had he suffered so infernally!

passage: He almost cried out that he couldn’t attend because he was in Hell!

passage: He was overcome by her generosity--her goodness.

passage: He had never felt so happy in the whole of his life!

passage: And all the time, he knew perfectly well, Dalloway was falling in love with her; she was falling in love with Dalloway; but it didn’t seem to matter. Nothing mattered.

passage: They went in and out of each other’s minds without any effort.

evidence: Figurative phrasing “went in and out of each other’s minds”, Generalized description of intersubjective state

passage: He said to himself as they were getting into the boat, “She will marry that man,” dully, without any resentment; but it was an obvious thing.

passage: He deserved to have her.
explanation: Brief judgment in Peter’s voice about Dalloway; FID.

passage: For himself, he was absurd. His demands upon Clarissa (he could see it now) were absurd. He asked impossible things. He made terrible scenes. She would have accepted him still, perhaps, if he had been less absurd.

evidence: Repetition of “absurd” enacts self-accusation, Parenthesis “(he could see it now)” signals reflective awareness

passage: That was all; but for him it was precisely as if she had said, “I’m only amusing myself with you; I’ve an understanding with Richard Dalloway.” So he took it.
explanation: Narrator reports Peter’s interpretive construction of Clarissa’s intent, dramatized with interior quotation.

passage: He had not slept for nights.
explanation: Narrator reports Peter’s prolonged insomnia—an interior condition.
evidence: Statement of internal bodily-mental state, Temporal duration indicates ongoing affective disturbance

passage: “It’s got to be finished one way or the other,” he said to himself.

passage: How sights fix themselves upon the mind! For example, the vivid green moss.

evidence: Aphoristic inner exclamation about memory mechanics, Specific sensuous detail as instance of fixation

passage: He felt as if his forehead would burst.

evidence: Psycho-narration “He felt as if …”, Metaphor “forehead would burst” conveys subjective intensity

passage: He felt that he was grinding against something physically hard; she was unyielding.

evidence: Psycho-narrative frame “He felt that …”, Concretizing metaphor for psychological impasse

passage: How sights fix themselves upon the mind! For example, the vivid green moss.
explanation: Third-person narrator comments on the persistence of a visual impression in memory, thematically rendering consciousness from outside Peter’s mind.
evidence: Exclamatory generalization about perception: "How sights fix themselves upon the mind!", No reporting verb for a character, but content is mental impression anchored in the recalled scene (the moss).
notes: Ambiguous between narratorial aside and Peter’s own thought in free indirect style. The lack of explicit anchoring makes it borderline psycho-narration; surrounding context (Peter’s memory) tips it toward omniscient description of consciousness.

passage: He felt as if his forehead would burst.
explanation: Straight psycho-narration reporting Peter’s inner sensation in the scene.
evidence: Reporting verb/psycho-narration: "He felt", Simile conveys inner bodily/mental pressure (pre-speech cognition reported by narrator).

passage: He felt that he was grinding against something physically hard; she was unyielding.
explanation: Narrator reports Peter’s felt impression of resistance during the confrontation, focusing on inner sensation.
evidence: Psycho-narration formula: "He felt that…", Metaphor of grinding against hardness indicates a subjective mental/physical impression.

passage: it was as if she had hit him in the face.
