passage: Still, the sun was hot. Still, one got over things. Still, life had a way of adding day to day. Still, he thought, yawning and beginning to take notice--Regent’s Park had changed very little since he was a boy, except for the squirrels--still, presumably there were compensations--
explanation: Repetition and cadence capture Peter’s private, consoling thought-stream in free indirect style; a light narratorial tag appears but voice and idiom are Peter’s.
evidence: Anaphoric "Still" mimics self-pacifying inner talk; switches into observation mid-flow, Parenthetical dash-embedded aside about Regent’s Park reflects his mental drift.
notes: Mixed with a brief explicit tag ("he thought") but overall the diction and rhythm are Peter’s; classified as indirect interior monologue with affective simulation of calm/recovery.

passage: Peter Walsh laughed out. But Lucrezia Warren Smith was saying to herself,
explanation: Cinematic cut from Peter’s outward reaction to Rezia’s inward speech, shifting minds within the same scene.
evidence: Juxtaposition "But Lucrezia… was saying to herself" immediately after Peter’s action, Signals concurrent interiority from a different character without scene break.
notes: Functions as a structural montage pivot; the following lines continue Rezia’s interior monologue.

passage: But Lucrezia Warren Smith was saying to herself, It’s wicked; why should I suffer? she was asking, as she walked down the broad path. No; I can’t stand it any longer, she was saying, having left Septimus, who wasn’t Septimus any longer, to say hard, cruel, wicked things, to talk to himself, to talk to a dead man, on the seat over there;
explanation: Rezia’s unspoken first-person assertions are embedded via free indirect style with light narratorial framing; the content is raw self-suffering and refusal.
evidence: First-person inner assertions without quotation marks: "It’s wicked; why should I suffer?" / "No; I can’t stand it any longer", Framing verbs (“was saying to herself”) recede as her voice takes over.
notes: Hybrid feel from explicit framing plus unquoted inner speech; classified as indirect interior monologue with strong affective charge.

passage: But for herself she had done nothing wrong; she had loved Septimus; she had been happy; she had had a beautiful home, and there her sisters lived still, making hats. Why should she suffer?
explanation: A self-justifying sequence in Rezia’s idiom culminates in a direct interior question, typical of free indirect discourse rendering her grievance.
evidence: List-like self-accounting (“she had done nothing wrong… she had loved… she had been happy”), Bare rhetorical question "Why should she suffer?" echoes prior first-person complaint in her voice.
notes: Underscored emphasis on later "_she_" intensifies subjective focus (orthographic emphasis appears shortly after).

passage: but why should _she_ be exposed? Why not left in Milan? Why tortured? Why?
explanation: A rapid-fire chain of interior questions in Rezia’s voice; italicized pronoun underscores self-focus; rhythm simulates agitated grievance.
evidence: Series of clipped rhetorical questions without reporting verbs, Italics (underscoring) on “_she_” marks heightened subjective emphasis.
notes: Orthographic emphasis signals a spike in affect inside an ongoing FID passage.

passage: Slightly waved by tears the broad path, the nurse, the man in grey, the perambulator, rose and fell before her eyes. To be rocked by this malignant torturer was her lot. But why? She was like a bird sheltering under the thin hollow of a leaf, who blinks at the sun when the leaf moves; starts at the crack of a dry twig. She was exposed; she was surrounded by the enormous trees, vast clouds of an indifferent world, exposed; tortured; and why should she suffer? Why?
explanation: Syntax and imagery enact Rezia’s vertigo and vulnerability; perceptions undulate, metaphors of exposure multiply, and interior questioning punctuates the sequence.
evidence: Sensory distortion: "rose and fell before her eyes"; metaphor of a bird under a leaf, Iterated "exposed; tortured; … why?" mirrors escalating agitation.
notes: Though third-person, the scene is entirely filtered through Rezia’s subjectivity; interior questions pin it to her mind.

passage: She must go back again to Septimus since it was almost time for them to be going to Sir William Bradshaw. She must go back and tell him, go back to him sitting there on the green chair under the tree, talking to himself, or to that dead man Evans, whom she had only seen once for a moment in the shop.
explanation: Self-command and immediate planning in Rezia’s idiom presented via free indirect discourse.
evidence: Imperative-like self-direction: "She must go back…" repeated, Specific visual anchoring of Septimus (“green chair under the tree”) as her mental picture.

passage: But such things happen to every one. Every one has friends who were killed in the War. Every one gives up something when they marry. She had given up her home. She had come to live here, in this awful city.
explanation: Generalized maxims and self-recollection in Rezia’s register, rendering her internal rationalizations about loss and sacrifice.
evidence: Aphoristic generalizations: "Every one has… Every one gives up…", Evaluative coloring in her idiom: "this awful city."

passage: But Septimus let himself think about horrible things, as she could too, if she tried.
explanation: A judgment phrased in Rezia’s evaluative idiom; the concessive structure marks her interior reflection about Septimus’s mental habit.
evidence: Subjective evaluative phrasing ("horrible things"), Counterfactual self-comparison ("as she could too, if she tried") typical of inner musing.

passage: Yet Dr. Holmes said there was nothing the matter with him.
explanation: A brusque, sarcastic inner retort to the doctor’s verdict, in Rezia’s voice.
evidence: Adversative "Yet" cues rebuttal thought, No reporting verb; voice and stance are clearly Rezia’s.

passage: What then had happened--why had he gone, then, why, when she sat by him, did he start, frown at her, move away, and point at her hand, take her hand, look at it terrified? Was it that she had taken off her wedding ring?
explanation: Rapid chained interior questions reconstructing a puzzling scene; classic FID rendering of her anxious inference-making.
evidence: Series of interior questions without framing tags, Detail focus on gestures (hand, ring) as cognitive clues in her thought.

passage: Their marriage was over, he thought, with agony, with relief. The rope was cut; he mounted; he was free, as it was decreed that he, Septimus, the lord of men, should be free; alone (since his wife had thrown away her wedding ring; since she had left him), he, Septimus, was alone, called forth in advance of the mass of men to hear the truth, to learn the meaning, which now at last, after all the toils of civilisation--Greeks, Romans, Shakespeare, Darwin, and now himself--was to be given whole to....
explanation: Septimus’s exalted self-mythologizing unfolds in free indirect style, with parentheses and enumerations mimicking manic cognition and grandiose destiny.
evidence: Shift into prophetic register ("lord of men"; "called forth in advance of the mass of men"), Parenthetical inserts and culture-name list (Greeks… Darwin…) display associative leaps.
notes: The opening “he thought” provides a light frame; the dominant voice is Septimus’s.

passage: The supreme secret must be told to the Cabinet; first that trees are alive; next there is no crime; next love, universal love, he muttered, gasping, trembling, painfully drawing out these profound truths which needed, so deep were they, so difficult, an immense effort to speak out, but the world was entirely changed by them for ever. No crime; love; he repeated, fumbling for his card and pencil, when a Skye terrier snuffed his trousers and he started in an agony of fear.
explanation: Manic proclamation rendered as inner assertions lists ‘truths’; rhythmic fragments (“No crime; love”) simulate exaltation, abruptly disrupted by a fear spike.
evidence: Declarative litany "first… next… next…" mimics visionary systematizing, Telegraphic repetition: "No crime; love;" indicates inner chant-like thought.

passage: It was turning into a man! He could not watch it happen! It was horrible, terrible to see a dog become a man!
explanation: Exclamatory, unframed interior outcries capture Septimus’s hallucinatory terror in FID.
evidence: Exclamations without reporting verbs, Content (dog becoming a man) clearly belongs to Septimus’s delusion.

passage: At once the dog trotted away. Heaven was divinely merciful, infinitely benignant. It spared him, pardoned his weakness. But what was the scientific explanation (for one must be scientific above all things)? Why could he see through bodies, see into the future, when dogs will become men? It was the heat wave presumably, operating upon a brain made sensitive by eons of evolution. Scientifically speaking, the flesh was melted off the world. His body was macerated until only the nerve fibres were left. It was spread like a veil upon a rock.
explanation: Septimus’s self-rationalizing monologue toggles between theological relief and pseudo-scientific causation; parentheses mark meta-aside; imagery externalizes bodily dissolution.
evidence: Parenthetical meta-rule: "(for one must be scientific above all things)", Subjective metaphors of world/body dissolution (“flesh was melted off the world”).

passage: He lay very high, on the back of the world. The earth thrilled beneath him. Red flowers grew through his flesh; their stiff leaves rustled by his head.
explanation: Subjective spatial exaltation and syncretic body-world imagery render Septimus’s interior elevation.
evidence: Impossible vantage “on the back of the world” as subjective state, Somatic-image fusion: “Red flowers grew through his flesh.”

passage: Music began clanging against the rocks up here. It is a motor horn down in the street, he muttered; but up here it cannoned from rock to rock, divided, met in shocks of sound which rose in smooth columns (that music should be visible was a discovery) and became an anthem, an anthem twined round now by a shepherd boy’s piping (That’s an old man playing a penny whistle by the public-house, he muttered) which, as the boy stood still came bubbling from his pipe, and then, as he climbed higher, made its exquisite plaint while the traffic passed beneath.
explanation: Perception is transmuted into visionary music; parenthetical asides mark corrective inner muttering; dense imagery and rhythmic build enact an ecstatic perceptual state.
evidence: Deictic contrast of "down in the street" vs. "up here" within one mind’s mapping, Parenthetical intrusions register meta-thought (discovery; self-correction).

passage: This boy’s elegy is played among the traffic, thought Septimus. Now he withdraws up into the snows, and roses hang about him--the thick red roses which grow on my bedroom wall, he reminded himself.
explanation: Free indirect narration slips into first-person possession (“my bedroom wall”), stitching memory-image to current hallucination.
evidence: Explicit tag "thought Septimus" then immediate internal mapping, First-person "my bedroom wall" intrudes within third-person frame.
notes: Borderline hybrid since first-person appears; treated as FID with first-person leakage.

passage: The music stopped. He has his penny, he reasoned it out, and has gone on to the next public-house.
explanation: Brief rationalizing inner inference in present perfect/present forms typical of FID.
evidence: Present-tense-like immediacy "He has… and has gone…" in a third-person frame, Cognitive verb in character voice: "he reasoned it out".

passage: I leant over the edge of the boat and fell down, he thought. I went under the sea. I have been dead, and yet am now alive, but let me rest still; he begged (he was talking to himself again--it was awful, awful!);
explanation: First-person interior assertions are embedded within third-person tags; parentheses add a narratorial aside about his self-talk; the texture enacts a liminal state between deathly torpor and return to life.
evidence: Direct first-person sequences followed by tags ("he thought… he begged"), Parenthetical meta-comment on his self-talk and affect ("awful, awful!").
notes: Mixed-mode: first-person directness within a framed third-person context warrants a hybrid label.

passage: so he felt himself drawing towards life, the sun growing hotter, cries sounding louder, something tremendous about to happen.
explanation: Psycho-narration of an inner turning-point with rhythmic escalation to simulate imminence.
evidence: Narratorial phrasing "he felt himself" describes inner shift, Accumulating present participles (“growing… sounding…”) mimic rising intensity.

passage: The trees waved, brandished. We welcome, the world seemed to say; we accept; we create. Beauty, the world seemed to say. And as if to prove it (scientifically) wherever he looked at the houses, at the railings, at the antelopes stretching over the palings, beauty sprang instantly. To watch a leaf quivering in the rush of air was an exquisite joy. Up in the sky swallows swooping, swerving, flinging themselves in and out, round and round, yet always with perfect control as if elastics held them; and the flies rising and falling; and the sun spotting now this leaf, now that, in mockery, dazzling it with soft gold in pure good temper; and now and again some chime (it might be a motor horn) tinkling divinely on the grass stalks--all of this, calm and reasonable as it was, made out of ordinary things as it was, was the truth now; beauty, that was the truth now. Beauty was everywhere.
explanation: Septimus’s animistic projection and evaluative refrain (“the truth now… Beauty was everywhere”) render an ecstatic cognitive frame through subjective perception.
evidence: Personification in his register: "We welcome, the world seemed to say", Mantric repetition of "beauty" asserts inner truth-state; parentheses note self-correction ("scientifically"; “it might be a motor horn”).

passage: The word “time” split its husk; poured its riches over him; and from his lips fell like shells, like shavings from a plane, without his making them, hard, white, imperishable words, and flew to attach themselves to their places in an ode to Time; an immortal ode to Time.
explanation: The prose enacts inspired effusion via cascading metaphors and rhythmic clauses, simulating the felt surge of an ‘ode’ forming unbidden.
evidence: Kinetic imagery of words as tangible objects (‘fell like shells… flew to attach themselves’), Claim of involuntariness (“without his making them”) conveys inspired trance.

passage: He sang. Evans answered from behind the tree. The dead were in Thessaly, Evans sang, among the orchids. There they waited till the War was over, and now the dead, now Evans himself--
explanation: Narrator reports an auditory hallucination as if real; content is entirely an inner experience represented from outside.
evidence: External-seeming report of “Evans answered” though Evans is dead, Mythic geography (“Thessaly… orchids”) frames the hallucinated content.

passage: I must tell the whole world, Septimus cried, raising his hand (as the dead man in the grey suit came nearer), raising his hand like some colossal figure who has lamented the fate of man for ages in the desert alone with his hands pressed to his forehead, furrows of despair on his cheeks, and now sees light on the desert’s edge which broadens and strikes the iron-black figure (and Septimus half rose from his chair), and with legions of men prostrate behind him he, the giant mourner, receives for one moment on his face the whole--
explanation: Highly wrought figurative amplification performs Septimus’s grandiose visionary state; the sentence’s momentum and parentheticals stage the swelling inner tableau.
evidence: Epic-scale metaphor (“colossal figure… legions of men”) enacts inner inflation, Parentheses layer concurrent perceptions into the ongoing vision.
notes: Though it includes spoken cry (“I must tell the whole world”), the extended image is a narratorial enactment of his inner grandiosity.

passage: He was talking, he was starting, this man must notice him. He was looking at them.
explanation: Rezia’s terse inner observations (“this man must notice him”) in FID register her fear of being seen.
evidence: Deictic “this man” and necessity modal “must” without reporting tags, Sequence of short clauses mimics quick anxious noticing.
notes: Attribution inferred from immediate context (Rezia trying to seat Septimus; Peter passing).

passage: And that is being young, Peter Walsh thought as he passed them.
explanation: Perspective cuts from Septimus/Rezia’s interior scene to Peter’s instant evaluative thought as he walks past—another montage splice within the shared setting.
evidence: Explicit thought attribution to Peter immediately after the Septimus/Rezia scene, Shared spatial anchor (“as he passed them”) bridges the minds.
notes: Also inaugurates a long stretch of Peter’s free indirect discourse.

passage: To be having an awful scene--the poor girl looked absolutely desperate--in the middle of the morning. But what was it about, he wondered, what had the young man in the overcoat been saying to her to make her look like that; what awful fix had they got themselves into, both to look so desperate as that on a fine summer morning?
explanation: Peter’s speculative inner questions and asides in his idiom render his private appraisal of the couple; dashes mark his insertions.
evidence: Rhetorical questions keyed to his wondering (“what was it about… what awful fix…”), Dash-framed aside (“--the poor girl looked absolutely desperate--”).

passage: The amusing thing about coming back to England, after five years, was the way it made, anyhow the first days, things stand out as if one had never seen them before; lovers squabbling under a tree; the domestic family life of the parks. Never had he seen London look so enchanting--the softness of the distances; the richness; the greenness; the civilisation, after India, he thought, strolling across the grass.
explanation: Evaluative generalization and perception list in Peter’s register; associative cataloging of impressions renders his inner susceptibility.
evidence: Subjective framing (“The amusing thing about…”) and first-person-like general ‘one’ within his idiom, Dash-linked impression clusters reflect an associative noticing.

passage: This susceptibility to impressions had been his undoing no doubt. Still at his age he had, like a boy or a girl even, these alternations of mood; good days, bad days, for no reason whatever, happiness from a pretty face, downright misery at the sight of a frump. After India of course one fell in love with every woman one met.
explanation: Peter self-diagnoses and generalizes about his moods in an interior, essaying FID cadence; jumps from moods to falling in love post-India mark associative drift.
evidence: Self-evaluative past perfect construction (“had been his undoing”), Aphoristic generalization in his idiom (“one fell in love with every woman one met”).

passage: What did the young people think about? Peter Walsh asked himself. Those five years--1918 to 1923--had been, he suspected, somehow very important. People looked different. Newspapers seemed different. Now for instance there was a man writing quite openly in one of the respectable weeklies about water-closets. That you couldn’t have done ten years ago--written quite openly about water-closets in a respectable weekly.
explanation: A chain of interior queries and observations charts Peter’s mental drift from generational thought to print decorum; FID with light self-attribution.
evidence: Explicit inner question and self-address tag (“asked himself”) opens the sequence, Refrain-like repetition around “water-closets” enacts mental fixation.

passage: And then this taking out a stick of rouge, or a powder-puff and making up in public. On board ship coming home there were lots of young men and girls--Betty and Bertie he remembered in particular--carrying on quite openly; the old mother sitting and watching them with her knitting, cool as a cucumber. The girl would stand still and powder her nose in front of every one. And they weren’t engaged; just having a good time; no feelings hurt on either side. As hard as nails she was--Betty What’shername--; but a thorough good sort. She would make a very good wife at thirty--she would marry when it suited her to marry; marry some rich man and live in a large house near Manchester.
explanation: Peter’s mind cascades from makeup in public to shipboard anecdotes to judgments about a girl’s future; an associative, evaluative inner narrative.
evidence: Parenthetical memory insert (“--Betty and Bertie he remembered in particular--”), Predictive scenario-building in his evaluative voice (“She would make… she would marry… live in a large house…”).

passage: Who was it now who had done that? Peter Walsh asked himself, turning into the Broad Walk,--married a rich man and lived in a large house near Manchester? Somebody who had written him a long, gushing letter quite lately about “blue hydrangeas.” It was seeing blue hydrangeas that made her think of him and the old days--Sally Seton, of course! It was Sally Seton--the last person in the world one would have expected to marry a rich man and live in a large house near Manchester, the wild, the daring, the romantic Sally!
explanation: Peter’s inner question triggers a memory cascade and emphatic self-answer; associative leap from generic type to specific person (Sally).
evidence: Self-questioning and self-answering (“Who was it…? … Sally Seton, of course!”), Exclamatory epithets (“the wild, the daring, the romantic Sally!”) in his voice.

passage: But of all that ancient lot, Clarissa’s friends--Whitbreads, Kinderleys, Cunninghams, Kinloch-Jones’s--Sally was probably the best. She tried to get hold of things by the right end anyhow. She saw through Hugh Whitbread anyhow--the admirable Hugh--when Clarissa and the rest were at his feet.
explanation: Peter’s evaluative summation of Sally and Hugh is voiced in FID, with ironic epithet “the admirable Hugh.”
evidence: Name-cascade and aside dashes in his idiom, Ironic labeling (“the admirable Hugh”) shows interior judgment.

passage: She was almost negligible. Then suddenly she would say something quite unexpected--something sharp. She had the relics of the grand manner perhaps. The steam coal was a little too strong for her--it made the atmosphere thick. And so there they lived, with their linen cupboards and their old masters and their pillow-cases fringed with real lace at the rate of five or ten thousand a year presumably, while he, who was two years older than Hugh, cadged for a job. At fifty-three he had to come and ask them to put him into some secretary’s office, to find him some usher’s job teaching little boys Latin, at the beck and call of some mandarin in an office, something that brought in five hundred a year; for if he married Daisy, even with his pension, they could never do on less. Whitbread could do it presumably; or Dalloway. He didn’t mind what he asked Dalloway. He was a thorough good sort; a bit limited; a bit thick in the head; yes; but a thorough good sort.
explanation: Third-person narration saturated with Peter’s evaluative voice and self-referential context; free indirect discourse that renders his inner appraisals of Lady Bruton/Richard and his own predicament.
evidence: Third-person with character-colored diction: “a bit thick in the head; yes; but a thorough good sort.”, Self-locating reference (“he, who was two years older than Hugh”) signals Peter’s anchoring perspective

passage: Whatever he took up he did in the same matter-of-fact sensible way; without a touch of imagination, without a spark of brilliancy, but with the inexplicable niceness of his type. He ought to have been a country gentleman--he was wasted on politics. He was at his best out of doors, with horses and dogs--how good he was, for instance, when that great shaggy dog of Clarissa’s got caught in a trap and had its paw half torn off, and Clarissa turned faint and Dalloway did the whole thing; bandaged, made splints; told Clarissa not to be a fool. That was what she liked him for perhaps--that was what she needed. “Now, my dear, don’t be a fool. Hold this--fetch that,” all the time talking to the dog as if it were a human being. But how could she swallow all that stuff about poetry? How could she let him hold forth about Shakespeare? Seriously and solemnly Richard Dalloway got on his hind legs and said that no decent man ought to read Shakespeare’s sonnets because it was like listening at keyholes (besides the relationship was not one that he approved). No decent man ought to let his wife visit a deceased wife’s sister. Incredible! The only thing to do was to pelt him with sugared almonds--it was at dinner.
explanation: Peter’s inner commentary and memories are rendered via free indirect discourse, moving associatively from general judgments to a specific memory and rhetorical questions challenging Richard’s views.
evidence: Rhetorical questions (“How could she swallow…? How could she let him…?”) read as Peter’s unspoken critique, Associative jump from dog incident to Shakespeare/dinner signals interior movement rather than orderly narration

passage: But Clarissa sucked it all in; thought it so honest of him; so independent of him; Heaven knows if she didn’t think him the most original mind she’d ever met! That was one of the bonds between Sally and himself. There was a garden where they used to walk, a walled-in place, with rose-bushes and giant cauliflowers--he could remember Sally tearing off a rose, stopping to exclaim at the beauty of the cabbage leaves in the moonlight (it was extraordinary how vividly it all came back to him, things he hadn’t thought of for years,) while she implored him, half laughing of course, to carry off Clarissa, to save her from the Hughs and the Dalloways and all the other “perfect gentlemen” who would “stifle her soul” (she wrote reams of poetry in those days), make a mere hostess of her, encourage her worldliness.
explanation: Peter’s recollection and evaluation flow in FID, with a vivid sensory memory surfacing unexpectedly; parentheses mark intrusive asides that signal interiority.
evidence: Parenthetical aside “(it was extraordinary how vividly it all came back to him…)” foregrounds memory’s suddenness, Sensory-laden recall (“cabbage leaves in the moonlight”) is subjectively filtered

passage: But one must do Clarissa justice. She wasn’t going to marry Hugh anyhow. She had a perfectly clear notion of what she wanted. Her emotions were all on the surface. Beneath, she was very shrewd--a far better judge of character than Sally, for instance, and with it all, purely feminine; with that extraordinary gift, that woman’s gift, of making a world of her own wherever she happened to be. She came into a room; she stood, as he had often seen her, in a doorway with lots of people round her. But it was Clarissa one remembered. Not that she was striking; not beautiful at all; there was nothing picturesque about her; she never said anything specially clever; there she was, however; there she was.
explanation: Peter’s interior assessment of Clarissa’s character and presence framed in his idiom; the iterative, emphatic cadence (“there she was… there she was”) conveys his subjective impression.
evidence: Evaluative voice (“one must do Clarissa justice”) belongs to Peter, not an external narrator, Repetitive emphasis (“there she was…”) mimics inner emphasis more than neutral narration

passage: No, no, no! He was not in love with her any more!
explanation: A self-addressed repudiation (“No, no, no!”) has soliloquy’s rhetorical force, immediately followed by a third-person FID assertion; the modes fuse.
evidence: Exclamatory, imperative cadence typical of self-address (“No, no, no!”), Third-person thought report in Peter’s voice (“He was not in love…”) without narrator commentary
notes: Hybrid moment: direct self-denial (soliloquy) + FID assertion. The shift is intra-sentential.

passage: He only felt, after seeing her that morning, among her scissors and silks, making ready for the party, unable to get away from the thought of her; she kept coming back and back like a sleeper jolting against him in a railway carriage; which was not being in love, of course; it was thinking of her, criticising her, starting again, after thirty years, trying to explain her.
explanation: Peter’s inner rationalization and metaphorical self-explanation appears via FID, reproducing his attempt to define his feelings.
evidence: Self-correction within the thought (“which was not being in love, of course”), Idiosyncratic simile (“like a sleeper jolting…”) aligns with a character’s inner phrasing

passage: The obvious thing to say of her was that she was worldly; cared too much for rank and society and getting on in the world--which was true in a sense; she had admitted it to him. (You could always get her to own up if you took the trouble; she was honest.) What she would say was that she hated frumps, fogies, failures, like himself presumably; thought people had no right to slouch about with their hands in their pockets; must do something, be something; and these great swells, these Duchesses, these hoary old Countesses one met in her drawing-room, unspeakably remote as he felt them to be from anything that mattered a straw, stood for something real to her. Lady Bexborough, she said once, held herself upright (so did Clarissa herself; she never lounged in any sense of the word; she was straight as a dart, a little rigid in fact). She said they had a kind of courage which the older she grew the more she respected. In all this there was a great deal of Dalloway, of course; a great deal of the public-spirited, British Empire, tariff-reform, governing-class spirit, which had grown on her, as it tends to do. With twice his wits, she had to see things through his eyes--one of the tragedies of married life. With a mind of her own, she must always be quoting Richard--as if one couldn’t know to a tittle what Richard thought by reading the _Morning Post_ of a morning!
explanation: Extended FID rendering Peter’s critique of Clarissa’s values; parenthetical asides signal intrusive interior commentary.
evidence: Parentheses with second-person “you” (“You could always get her to own up…”) read as Peter’s aside, Lexical coloring (“these hoary old Countesses…”) reflects Peter’s idiom

passage: These parties for example were all for him, or for her idea of him (to do Richard justice he would have been happier farming in Norfolk). She made her drawing-room a sort of meeting-place; she had a genius for it. Over and over again he had seen her take some raw youth, twist him, turn him, wake him up; set him going. Infinite numbers of dull people conglomerated round her of course. But odd unexpected people turned up; an artist sometimes; sometimes a writer; queer fish in that atmosphere. And behind it all was that network of visiting, leaving cards, being kind to people; running about with bunches of flowers, little presents; So-and-so was going to France--must have an air-cushion; a real drain on her strength; all that interminable traffic that women of her sort keep up; but she did it genuinely, from a natural instinct.
explanation: Peter’s interior inventory of Clarissa’s social world in FID, including a parenthetical corrective and clipped list-like rhythms characteristic of inner cataloguing.
evidence: Parenthetical aside correcting himself about Richard (“to do Richard justice…”), Paratactic listing (“visiting, leaving cards… running about… So-and-so… must have an air-cushion”) mimics thought-catalogue

passage: Oddly enough, she was one of the most thoroughgoing sceptics he had ever met, and possibly (this was a theory he used to make up to account for her, so transparent in some ways, so inscrutable in others), possibly she said to herself, As we are a doomed race, chained to a sinking ship (her favourite reading as a girl was Huxley and Tyndall, and they were fond of these nautical metaphors), as the whole thing is a bad joke, let us, at any rate, do our part; mitigate the sufferings of our fellow-prisoners (Huxley again); decorate the dungeon with flowers and air-cushions; be as decent as we possibly can. Those ruffians, the Gods, shan’t have it all their own way,--her notion being that the Gods, who never lost a chance of hurting, thwarting and spoiling human lives were seriously put out if, all the same, you behaved like a lady. That phase came directly after Sylvia’s death--that horrible affair. To see your own sister killed by a falling tree (all Justin Parry’s fault--all his carelessness) before your very eyes, a girl too on the verge of life, the most gifted of them, Clarissa always said, was enough to turn one bitter. Later she wasn’t so positive perhaps; she thought there were no Gods; no one was to blame; and so she evolved this atheist’s religion of doing good for the sake of goodness.
explanation: Peter hypothesizes Clarissa’s private creed; FID carries his speculative ‘theory’ with multiple parenthetical intrusions and imagined reported thought (“she said to herself”).
evidence: Metacommentary (“this was a theory he used to make up…”) is Peter’s mind at work, Embedded imagined dicta (“she said to herself, As we are a doomed race…”)

passage: And of course she enjoyed life immensely. It was her nature to enjoy (though goodness only knows, she had her reserves; it was a mere sketch, he often felt, that even he, after all these years, could make of Clarissa). Anyhow there was no bitterness in her; none of that sense of moral virtue which is so repulsive in good women. She enjoyed practically everything. If you walked with her in Hyde Park now it was a bed of tulips, now a child in a perambulator, now some absurd little drama she made up on the spur of the moment. (Very likely, she would have talked to those lovers, if she had thought them unhappy.) She had a sense of comedy that was really exquisite, but she needed people, always people, to bring it out, with the inevitable result that she frittered her time away, lunching, dining, giving these incessant parties of hers, talking nonsense, sayings things she didn’t mean, blunting the edge of her mind, losing her discrimination.
explanation: Peter’s FID continues, moving through vivid present-tense imaginative scenes; parentheses mark intrusive conjecture about the lovers, signaling interior drift.
evidence: Parenthetical aside projecting Clarissa’s likely action (“Very likely, she would have talked to those lovers…”), Presentational imagery (“Hyde Park… bed of tulips… absurd little drama”) as subjective tableau

passage: There she would sit at the head of the table taking infinite pains with some old buffer who might be useful to Dalloway--they knew the most appalling bores in Europe--or in came Elizabeth and everything must give way to _her_. She was at a High School, at the inarticulate stage last time he was over, a round-eyed, pale-faced girl, with nothing of her mother in her, a silent stolid creature, who took it all as a matter of course, let her mother make a fuss of her, and then said “May I go now?” like a child of four; going off, Clarissa explained, with that mixture of amusement and pride which Dalloway himself seemed to rouse in her, to play hockey. And now Elizabeth was “out,” presumably; thought him an old fogy, laughed at her mother’s friends.
explanation: Peter’s inward narrative of Clarissa’s domestic-social scenes and Elizabeth’s development is rendered in FID, with evaluative asides and scenically specific details.
evidence: Colloquial labeling (“old buffer,” “the most appalling bores”) reflects Peter’s judgments, Shift to compressed scene with quoted speech (“‘May I go now?’”) embedded in memory

passage: Ah well, so be it.
explanation: A brief, self-addressed resignation in first-person-free but unmediated inner voice; no reporting verbs or narrator frame.
evidence: No “he thought” framing; pure interior exclamation, Registers as a self-consoling closure to the preceding FID reflection

passage: The compensation of growing old, Peter Walsh thought, coming out of Regent’s Park, and holding his hat in hand, was simply this; that the passions remain as strong as ever, but one has gained--at last!--the power which adds the supreme flavour to existence,--the power of taking hold of experience, of turning it round, slowly, in the light. A terrible confession it was (he put his hat on again), but now, at the age of fifty-three one scarcely needed people any more. Life itself, every moment of it, every drop of it, here, this instant, now, in the sun, in Regent’s Park, was enough. Too much indeed. A whole lifetime was too short to bring out, now that one had acquired the power, the full flavour; to extract every ounce of pleasure, every shade of meaning; which both were so much more solid than they used to be, so much less personal.
explanation: Explicitly framed as Peter’s thought, the passage then flows in FID, with rhythmic, sensuous diction simulating elation and present-tense absorption; parentheses and dashes mark interior cadence.
evidence: “Peter Walsh thought” frames interiority, followed by present-immediacy (“here, this instant, now”), Rhythmic repetition and accumulation (“every moment… every drop… full flavour; to extract every ounce… every shade…”) enact pleasure
notes: Affect is enacted by syntax and repetition (Steinberg’s simulation).

passage: (pray God that one might say these things without being overheard!)
explanation: Parenthetical aside gives an unmediated, whispered self-prayer—an intrusive, direct thought inside the flow.
evidence: Parentheses mark a sudden interior intrusion, First-person implicit prayer register without a reporting verb

passage: It was impossible that he should ever suffer again as Clarissa had made him suffer. For hours at a time (pray God that one might say these things without being overheard!), for hours and days he never thought of Daisy. Could it be that he was in love with her then, remembering the misery, the torture, the extraordinary passion of those days? It was a different thing altogether--a much pleasanter thing--the truth being, of course, that now _she_ was in love with _him_. And that perhaps was the reason why, when the ship actually sailed, he felt an extraordinary relief, wanted nothing so much as to be alone; was annoyed to find all her little attentions--cigars, notes, a rug for the voyage--in his cabin. Every one if they were honest would say the same; one doesn’t want people after fifty; one doesn’t want to go on telling women they are pretty; that’s what most men of fifty would say, Peter Walsh thought, if they were honest.
explanation: Sustained FID of Peter’s self-assessment and rationalizations, with a direct parenthetical intrusion; the thought tag reappears at the end.
evidence: Embedded rhetorical questions (“Could it be that he was in love with her then…?”), Closing tag “Peter Walsh thought” confirms the preceding as his inner discourse

passage: But then these astonishing accesses of emotion--bursting into tears this morning, what was all that about? What could Clarissa have thought of him? thought him a fool presumably, not for the first time.
explanation: Peter interrogates his own behavior in FID; the rapid self-questioning and self-deprecation are interior markers.
evidence: Rhetorical questions without reporting verbs simulate inner cross-examination, Elliptical insertion (“thought him a fool presumably”) reflects Peter’s conjecture about Clarissa

passage: It was jealousy that was at the bottom of it--jealousy which survives every other passion of mankind, Peter Walsh thought, holding his pocket-knife at arm’s length. She had been meeting Major Orde, Daisy said in her last letter; said it on purpose he knew; said it to make him jealous; he could see her wrinkling her forehead as she wrote, wondering what she could say to hurt him; and yet it made no difference; he was furious! All this pother of coming to England and seeing lawyers wasn’t to marry her, but to prevent her from marrying anybody else. That was what tortured him, that was what came over him when he saw Clarissa so calm, so cold, so intent on her dress or whatever it was; realising what she might have spared him, what she had reduced him to--a whimpering, snivelling old ass. But women, he thought, shutting his pocket-knife, don’t know what passion is. They don’t know the meaning of it to men. Clarissa was as cold as an icicle. There she would sit on the sofa by his side, let him take her hand, give him one kiss--
explanation: FID tracks Peter’s jealous rumination, including a constructed vignette of Daisy writing (reverie) and a surge of affect rendered through exclamation and emphatic generalization.
evidence: “he could see her wrinkling her forehead as she wrote” imagines a scene not directly witnessed (constructive fantasy), Affective outburst (“he was furious!”) and categorical claims (“women… don’t know what passion is”) show inner agitation

passage:
the voice of no age or sex, the voice of an ancient spring spouting from the earth; which issued, just opposite Regent’s Park Tube station from a tall quivering shape, like a funnel, like a rusty pump, like a wind-beaten tree for ever barren of leaves which lets the wind run up and down its branches singing

    ee um fah um so     foo swee too eem oo

and rocks and creaks and moans in the eternal breeze.
explanation: Immediately after “A sound interrupted him,” the song is transformed into a mythic, metaphor-laden perception likely filtered through Peter’s consciousness; the similes enact a subjective reimagining rather than neutral report.
evidence: Proximal anchoring to Peter’s perception (“A sound interrupted him”), Cascading similes and anthropomorphism suggest a perceiver’s imaginative elaboration rather than objective narration
notes: Ambiguous locus of consciousness: could also be an impersonal narratorial lyricism. Classified as FID anchored to Peter due to local anchoring; alternate reading is non-SOC lyrical set piece.

passage: Oh poor old wretch! Suppose it was a wet night? Suppose one’s father, or somebody who had known one in better days had happened to pass, and saw one standing there in the gutter? And where did she sleep at night?
explanation: Rezia’s unspoken compassion and anxious hypotheticals are rendered in FID: exclamatory pity and second-person-general ‘one’ signal her inner voice.
evidence: Exclamation (“Oh poor old wretch!”) without a speech tag reads as inner exclamation, Series of “Suppose…” questions simulate private worrying

passage: Since she was so unhappy, for weeks and weeks now, Rezia had given meanings to things that happened, almost felt sometimes that she must stop people in the street, if they looked good, kind people, just to say to them “I am unhappy”; and this old woman singing in the street “if some one should see, what matter they?” made her suddenly quite sure that everything was going to be right. They were going to Sir William Bradshaw; she thought his name sounded nice; he would cure Septimus at once. And then there was a brewer’s cart, and the grey horses had upright bristles of straw in their tails; there were newspaper placards. It was a silly, silly dream, being unhappy.
explanation: Mixed psycho-narration and FID: embedded clauses (“she thought his name sounded nice; he would cure Septimus at once”) and the concluding self-assurance capture Rezia’s inner hopeful turn, interleaved with her perceptions.
evidence: Embedded evaluatives in her idiom (“she thought his name sounded nice”), Concluding assertion (“It was a silly, silly dream, being unhappy.”) reads as internal resolution

passage: So they crossed, Mr. and Mrs. Septimus Warren Smith, and was there, after all, anything to draw attention to them, anything to make a passer-by suspect here is a young man who carries in him the greatest message in the world, and is, moreover, the happiest man in the world, and the most miserable?
explanation: A narratorial rhetorical question characterizes Septimus’s contradictory inner condition from outside; the narrator retains control, reporting about consciousness rather than immersing us in it.
evidence: Direct address to “a passer-by” and generalized framing marks narrator’s voice, Paradoxical inner states (“happiest… and the most miserable”) are asserted, not rendered
notes: Could be read as free indirect coloration of Rezia’s/observer’s perception, but the generalizing, expository tone points to omniscient psycho-narration.

passage: Perhaps they walked more slowly than other people, and there was something hesitating, trailing, in the man’s walk, but what more natural for a clerk, who has not been in the West End on a weekday at this hour for years, than to keep looking at the sky, looking at this, that and the other, as if Portland Place were a room he had come into when the family are away, the chandeliers being hung in holland bags, and the caretaker, as she lets in long shafts of dusty light upon deserted, queer-looking arm-chairs, lifting one corner of the long blinds, explains to the visitors what a wonderful place it is; how wonderful, but at the same time, he thinks, as he looks at chairs and tables, how strange.
explanation: Narration slides into Septimus’s present thought (“he thinks… how strange”) amid a richly metaphorical comparison, briefly granting access to his interior perception.
evidence: Explicit insertion “he thinks” flags transition into inner discourse, The metaphorical room/caretaker tableau expresses a subjective way of seeing the city

passage: had left home, a mere boy, because of his mother; she lied; because he came down to tea for the fiftieth time with his hands unwashed; because he could see no future for a poet in Stroud;
explanation: Free-indirect rendering of Septimus’s remembered justifications for leaving home; the paratactic, asyndetic reasons are voiced in his idiom without reporting verbs.
evidence: Third-person frame but content in character’s evaluative voice (“she lied;”), Parataxis with semicolons mimics thought listing and lacks explicit tags like “he thought”
notes: Hybrid with surrounding narrator frame (“he had them”). The clipped clause “she lied;” is especially clear as FID.

passage: But of all this what could the most observant of friends have said except what a gardener says when he opens the conservatory door in the morning and finds a new blossom on his plant:--It has flowered; flowered from vanity, ambition, idealism, passion, loneliness, courage, laziness, the usual seeds, which all muddled up (in a room off the Euston Road), made him shy, and stammering, made him anxious to improve himself, made him fall in love with Miss Isabel Pole, lecturing in the Waterloo Road upon Shakespeare.
explanation: The narrator interprets and summarizes inner development and dispositions as if from outside the mind; conventional psycho-narration about states (shyness, anxiety to improve) framed by an extended metaphor.
evidence: Authorial simile/metaphor (“what a gardener says… It has flowered”), Mental traits reported by narrator voice (“made him anxious to improve himself”)
notes: Parentheses mark an aside situating the inner growth; not character-voiced.

passage: Was he not like Keats? she asked;
explanation: Rhetorical question in Isabel’s evaluative idiom rendered via free indirect style; the tag “she asked” lightly frames a thought voiced in her register.
evidence: Character-colored diction (“like Keats?”) without quotation marks for speech, Minimal narratorial tag (“she asked”) signals interior query rather than narrated dialogue
notes: Ambiguous boundary with psycho-narration; could be read as reported speech. Context favors FID of her inner assessment.

passage: and reflected how she might give him a taste of _Antony and Cleopatra_ and the rest;
explanation: Narrator reports Isabel’s inward planning/reflection in conventional psycho-narration.
evidence: Reporting verb of cognition (“reflected how she might…”), Third-person distance maintained throughout
notes: Italicized title marks external text intruding but not a shift of consciousness mode.

passage: He thought her beautiful, believed her impeccably wise; dreamed of her, wrote poems to her, which, ignoring the subject, she corrected in red ink; he saw her, one summer evening, walking in a green dress in a square.
explanation: Conventional narration of Septimus’s mental states and fantasies (thinking, dreaming, seeing in memory).
evidence: Explicit mental verbs (“thought,” “dreamed,” “saw”), Narrator summarizes inner content rather than simulating its flow

passage: Something was up, Mr. Brewer knew; Mr. Brewer, managing clerk at Sibleys and Arrowsmiths, auctioneers, valuers, land and estate agents; something was up, he thought, and, being paternal with his young men, and thinking very highly of Smith’s abilities, and prophesying that he would, in ten or fifteen years, succeed to the leather arm-chair in the inner room under the skylight with the deed-boxes round him, “if he keeps his health,” said Mr. Brewer, and that was the danger--he looked weakly;
explanation: Narrator relays Brewer’s assessments and projections about Septimus’s future; psycho-narration of Brewer’s consciousness.
evidence: Cognition verbs and epistemic stance (“knew,” “he thought,” “prophesying”), Embedded evaluative aside (“if he keeps his health”) within narrator frame
notes: A conventional interior access; not FID.

passage: He went to France to save an England which consisted almost entirely of Shakespeare’s plays and Miss Isabel Pole in a green dress walking in a square.
explanation: Narrator compresses Septimus’s subjective construction of “England” into literary and personal images.
evidence: Third-person report of internal representation (“consisted almost entirely of…”), Subjective, idiosyncratic content revealing his mental world

passage: Septimus, far from showing any emotion or recognising that here was the end of a friendship, congratulated himself upon feeling very little and very reasonably.
explanation: Conventional narration of Septimus’s self-assessment and emotional numbness.
evidence: Narratorial summary of interior state (“congratulated himself upon…”), Evaluative phrasing (“very reasonably”) framed externally

passage: The War had taught him. It was sublime.
explanation: Staccato, untagged judgments in Septimus’s idiom characteristic of free indirect style; the clipped cadence simulates his stark, exalted appraisal.
evidence: Absent reporting verbs; terse evaluative sentences, Pronounced character coloring (“It was sublime.”) without narrator hedging
notes: Could also be read as concise psycho-narration; rhythm and evaluative register tip toward FID.

passage: He had gone through the whole show, friendship, European War, death, had won promotion, was still under thirty and was bound to survive.
explanation: Self-narrating boast in Septimus’s voice rendered via FID; “bound to survive” reads as his inner conclusion.
evidence: Character-colored idiom (“the whole show”), No explicit thought tag; evaluative conclusion embedded in third person

passage: He watched them explode with indifference.
explanation: Narrator states a mental attitude toward events (indifference), reporting his inner affect without simulating the thought process.
evidence: Conventional third-person report of feeling (“with indifference”), No character idiom; narrator retains control

passage: he became engaged one evening when the panic was on him--that he could not feel.
explanation: The appositive dash presents Septimus’s core idée fixe (“that he could not feel”) in his own phrasing; a hallmark of FID intruding into narration.
evidence: Dash introduces unframed inner clause, Content matches his recurring mantra elsewhere (“he could not feel”)
notes: Sits inside a larger narrated sentence; hybrid with psycho-narration.

passage: For now that it was all over, truce signed, and the dead buried, he had, especially in the evening, these sudden thunder-claps of fear. He could not feel.
explanation: Narrator reports recurring affect (“thunder-claps of fear”) using sensory metaphor and states his numbness; form stresses episodic surges of anxiety.
evidence: Metaphoric rendering of fear (“thunder-claps”), Declarative mantra-like sentence (“He could not feel.”)

passage: As he opened the door of the room where the Italian girls sat making hats, he could see them; could hear them; they were rubbing wires among coloured beads in saucers; they were turning buckram shapes this way and that; the table was all strewn with feathers, spangles, silks, ribbons; scissors were rapping on the table; but something failed him; he could not feel.
explanation: Sensory stream filtered through Septimus’s perception (parataxis; repeated clauses), culminating in his interior refrain of numbness; FID simulating hyper-detailed perception amid affective blankness.
evidence: Paratactic listing with semi-colons mimics unfolding perception (“could see…; could hear…;”), Ends on untagged inner verdict (“he could not feel.”)
notes: The cadence enacts the mismatch between sensory richness and affective nullity.

passage: Still, scissors rapping, girls laughing, hats being made protected him; he was assured of safety; he had a refuge.
explanation: Narrator characterizes the perceived scene as psychologically protective, summarizing his inner assurance.
evidence: Abstracted inner state (“he was assured of safety; he had a refuge.”), External sounds/objects mapped to protection

passage: There were moments of waking in the early morning. The bed was falling; he was falling. Oh for the scissors and the lamplight and the buckram shapes!
explanation: FID renders hypnagogic terror (“he was falling”) and a direct-feeling exclamation wishing for stabilizing stimuli; fragmented syntax simulates panic.
evidence: Short, verbless exclamation (“Oh for the scissors…”) without reporting tag, Repetition of “falling” evokes vertiginous state

passage: But beauty was behind a pane of glass. Even taste (Rezia liked ices, chocolates, sweet things) had no relish to him.
explanation: Septimus’s metaphor for anhedonia is voiced in FID; the parenthetical aside embeds an interior qualification within the sentence.
evidence: Character-colored metaphor (“behind a pane of glass”), Parenthetical aside intrudes on otherwise straight narration

passage: But he could not taste, he could not feel. In the tea-shop among the tables and the chattering waiters the appalling fear came over him--he could not feel.
explanation: Repetitive, mantra-like FID that enacts his mounting fear and numbness without explicit thought tags.
evidence: Repetition of the same clause as inner refrain, Dash foregrounds intrusive inner assertion

passage: He could reason; he could read, Dante for example, quite easily (“Septimus, do put down your book,” said Rezia, gently shutting the _Inferno_), he could add up his bill; his brain was perfect; it must be the fault of the world then--that he could not feel.
explanation: A rational self-inventory culminating in an unframed conclusion; the parenthetical intrusion of quoted speech punctuates an otherwise interior sequence.
evidence: Self-justifying progression leading to inner conclusion (“it must be the fault of the world then—”), Embedded parenthetical speech interrupts interior flow

passage: It might be possible, Septimus thought, looking at England from the train window, as they left Newhaven; it might be possible that the world itself is without meaning.
explanation: Explicitly tagged thought; narrator frames Septimus’s metaphysical speculation rather than immersing the reader in its flow.
evidence: Reporting tag (“Septimus thought”), Third-person syntax maintained

passage: That boy’s business of the intoxication of language--_Antony and Cleopatra_--had shrivelled utterly.
explanation: Septimus’s dismissive judgment in his idiom (“That boy’s business…”) rendered via FID.
evidence: Colloquial evaluative phrase anchored in character voice, No reporting verb; title inset as part of the thought

passage: How Shakespeare loathed humanity--the putting on of clothes, the getting of children, the sordidity of the mouth and the belly!
explanation: Exclamatory FID asserting a sweeping judgment; syntactic energy and list of bodily images enact loathing.
evidence: Exclamatory opening (“How … loathed”) typical of FID, Sensory-laden list mirroring emotional intensity

passage: This was now revealed to Septimus; the message hidden in the beauty of words.
explanation: Narrator glosses Septimus’s interpretive epiphany in expository terms.
evidence: Third-person exposition (“was now revealed to Septimus”), Abstract phrasing rather than mimetic thought

passage: The secret signal which one generation passes, under disguise, to the next is loathing, hatred, despair. Dante the same. Aeschylus (translated) the same.
explanation: Doctrinaire assertions in Septimus’s voice framed as absolute statements; short sentence fragments emphasize categorical mood.
evidence: Aphoristic cadence without tags, Elliptical fragments (“Dante the same. Aeschylus … the same.”)

passage: She looked pale, mysterious, like a lily, drowned, under water, he thought.
explanation: Narrator tags Septimus’s simile explicitly, presenting perception rather than immersive thought.
evidence: Tag “he thought” marks reported perception, Sensory/metaphoric description anchored in his viewpoint

passage: Love between man and woman was repulsive to Shakespeare. The business of copulation was filth to him before the end.
explanation: Categorical assertions in Septimus’s idiom via FID; diction enacts revulsion.
evidence: No tags; declarative universalizing tone from character’s mind, Affective lexical choices (“repulsive,” “filth”)

passage: One cannot bring children into a world like this. One cannot perpetuate suffering, or increase the breed of these lustful animals, who have no lasting emotions, but only whims and vanities, eddying them now this way, now that.
explanation: Polemic in FID using impersonal “One” to vent his inner doctrine; rhythm and intensifiers simulate vehement disgust and despair.
evidence: Impersonal pronoun “One” as FID marker, Emotion-suffused diction (“lustful animals,” “no lasting emotions”)

passage: For the truth is (let her ignore it) that human beings have neither kindness, nor faith, nor charity beyond what serves to increase the pleasure of the moment. They hunt in packs. Their packs scour the desert and vanish screaming into the wilderness. They desert the fallen. They are plastered over with grimaces. There was Brewer at the office, with his waxed moustache, coral tie-pin, white slip, and pleasurable emotions--all coldness and clamminess within,--his geraniums ruined in the War--his cook’s nerves destroyed; or Amelia What’shername, handing round cups of tea punctually at five--a leering, sneering obscene little harpy; and the Toms and Berties in their starched shirt fronts oozing thick drops of vice. They never saw him drawing pictures of them naked at their antics in his notebook. In the street, vans roared past him; brutality blared out on placards; men were trapped in mines; women burnt alive; and once a maimed file of lunatics being exercised or displayed for the diversion of the populace (who laughed aloud), ambled and nodded and grinned past him, in the Tottenham Court Road, each half apologetically, yet triumphantly, inflicting his hopeless woe. And would _he_ go mad?
explanation: Extended FID tirade with parenthetical aside and idiolect (“What’shername”) rendering Septimus’s persecutory worldview; vivid imagery and rhetorical accumulation simulate agitated despair and burgeoning paranoia.
evidence: Parenthetical interior aside (“let her ignore it”) and italics in “_he_”, Character-colored labels and hyperbolic catalog (“oozing thick drops of vice,” “brutality blared”)
notes: Orthography (italics, parentheses) marks intrusions and emphasis typical of Woolf’s interior rendering.

passage: She could not grow old and have no children! She was very lonely, she was very unhappy!
explanation: Exclamatory sentences in Rezia’s voice rendered in FID, conveying her inner lament without quotation.
evidence: Absence of speech tags; exclamatory intensity, Immediate affective self-description (“very lonely,” “very unhappy”)
notes: Could be reported speech; the lack of quotes and interior positioning favor FID.

passage: Far away he heard her sobbing; he heard it accurately, he noticed it distinctly; he compared it to a piston thumping.
explanation: Narrator reports Septimus’s detached, mechanistic perception of Rezia’s sobbing, including his interior simile.
evidence: Perceptual verbs (“heard… noticed… compared”), Metaphoric comparison (“piston thumping”) tagged to his perspective

passage: But he felt nothing. His wife was crying, and he felt nothing; only each time she sobbed in this profound, this silent, this hopeless way, he descended another step into the pit.
explanation: Narrator states emotional numbness and uses descent imagery to simulate progressive despair.
evidence: Explicit statement of affective nullity (“he felt nothing”), Metaphor of descent (“another step into the pit”)

passage: Now he had surrendered; now other people must help him. People must be sent for. He gave in. Nothing could rouse him.
explanation: Self-addressed, mantra-like FID marking capitulation; short declaratives simulate a flattened, resigned state.
evidence: Absence of tags; repeated “now” and imperatives directed at anonymous agents, Paratactic, clipped clauses typical of interior self-narration

passage: Oh, what a relief! What a kind man, what a good man! thought Rezia.
explanation: Rezia’s excited interior exclamations are directly voiced, lightly tagged as thought.
evidence: Exclamatory first-person-like affect without being spoken to an addressee, Explicit thought attribution (“thought Rezia”) with content in her idiom

passage: So there was no excuse; nothing whatever the matter, except the sin for which human nature had condemned him to death; that he did not feel. He had not cared when Evans was killed; that was worst; but all the other crimes raised their heads and shook their fingers and jeered and sneered over the rail of the bed in the early hours of the morning at the prostrate body which lay realising its degradation; how he had married his wife without loving her; had lied to her; seduced her; outraged Miss Isabel Pole, and was so pocked and marked with vice that women shuddered when they saw him in the street. The verdict of human nature on such a wretch was death.
explanation: Nocturnal self-accusation dramatized in FID with personification of “crimes” and tribunal imagery; simulates obsessive guilt spirals.
evidence: Imagistic personification of inner voices (“crimes raised their heads… shook their fingers”), Core mantra repeated (“that he did not feel”) without tags

passage: (Rezia would learn to cook porridge.)
explanation: Parenthetical aside functioning as a brief interior projection or narrator-aside about Rezia’s resolution.
evidence: Isolation in parentheses marks a shift from main narrative flow, Content reads as an internal resolution/projection rather than narrated action
notes: Ambiguous attribution: could be narrator’s ironic aside or Rezia’s inward determination; included due to Woolf’s frequent use of parentheses to cue interior intrusions.

passage: When the damned fool came again, Septimus refused to see him.
explanation: Epithet “the damned fool” reflects Septimus’s inner judgment embedded in third-person narration.
evidence: Character-colored insult without quotation marks, No reporting verb of thought; evaluation fused with narration

passage: Human nature, in short, was on him--the repulsive brute, with the blood-red nostrils. Holmes was on him.
explanation: Personified persecutor imagery and rhythmic repetition simulate Septimus’s sense of being hunted; voiced as FID.
evidence: Metaphoric naming of abstraction as creature (“repulsive brute… blood-red nostrils”), Repetition (“Holmes was on him”) underscores obsessive fixation

passage: Once you stumble, Septimus wrote on the back of a postcard, human nature is on you. Holmes is on you.
explanation: Septimus’s credo-like inner sentences are given verbatim; although framed as writing, the gnomic lines capture his obsessive mental refrain.
evidence: Unquoted aphoristic lines that read as inner mantra, Colon-like apposition via syntax foregrounds the maxim despite attribution (“wrote…”)
notes: Ambiguous because externalized as writing rather than unspoken thought; included as FID of an interiorized maxim.

passage: Their only chance was to escape, without letting Holmes know; to Italy--anywhere, anywhere, away from Dr. Holmes.
explanation: Urgent planning voiced in FID; repetition and broken phrasing enact anxious flight-thinking.
evidence: Repetition of “anywhere, anywhere”, No reporting tag; plan phrased as inner necessity (“Their only chance…”)

passage: But Rezia could not understand him. Dr. Holmes was such a kind man. He was so interested in Septimus. He only wanted to help them, he said. He had four little children and he had asked her to tea, she told Septimus.
explanation: A sequence of Rezia’s reassuring beliefs and paraphrased reasons rendered as FID rather than direct speech.
evidence: Short declaratives in character’s idiom (“such a kind man”), Absence of quotation marks; thought-content paraphrased
notes: Blends paraphrase of what she ‘told’ Septimus with her interior rationalizations.

passage: So he was deserted.
explanation: Compressed inner verdict in FID summarizing his affective isolation.
evidence: Brief, untagged evaluative sentence in character’s perspective, Functions as interior conclusion to preceding sequence

passage: The whole world was clamouring: Kill yourself, kill yourself, for our sakes. But why should he kill himself for their sakes?
explanation: FID dramatizes intrusive imperatives (imagined external voices) and his interior rebuttal via rhetorical question.
evidence: Colon introduces imagined voices within thought, Rhetorical self-questioning without tags

passage: Food was pleasant; the sun hot; and this killing oneself, how does one set about it, with a table knife, uglily, with floods of blood,--by sucking a gaspipe?
explanation: The syntax shifts into an intrusive, horrified question about method; FID renders the morbid ideation’s rhythm.
evidence: Question form embedded in narration without thought tag, Graphic alternatives sequenced with dashes/commas mimicking racing thought

passage: He was too weak; he could scarcely raise his hand.
explanation: Narrative statement of inner debility; conventional psycho-narration that contributes to the affective simulation of despair.
evidence: Third-person report of internal capacity (“too weak”), No character idiom; narrator retains control

passage: Besides, now that he was quite alone, condemned, deserted, as those who are about to die are alone, there was a luxury in it, an isolation full of sublimity; a freedom which the attached can never know.
explanation: Elevated diction and paradoxical self-consolation voiced via FID; the cadence enacts a self-aggrandizing mood of sublime isolation.
evidence: Abstract, evaluative phrasing characteristic of character’s inner rhetoric, No thought tag; inward justification framed as universal claim

passage: Holmes had won of course; the brute with the red nostrils had won. But even Holmes himself could not touch this last relic straying on the edge of the world, this outcast, who gazed back at the inhabited regions, who lay, like a drowned sailor, on the shore of the world.
explanation: FID with grandiose, isolating self-image; metaphors and cadence simulate exalted estrangement and defiance.
evidence: Metaphoric self-casting (“last relic… like a drowned sailor”), Character-colored epithet (“the brute with the red nostrils”)

passage: A voice spoke from behind the screen. Evans was speaking. The dead were with him.
explanation: Narrator reports the content of a hallucination in terse, revelatory sentences, simulating its certainty and strangeness.
evidence: Short, unqualified declaratives (“Evans was speaking. The dead were with him.”), No mediating explanation; inner event presented as fact

passage: So there was a man outside; Evans presumably; and the roses, which Rezia said were half dead, had been picked by him in the fields of Greece.
explanation: FID chaining delusional inferences from immediate stimuli (roses) to imagined origins; associative logic of psychosis.
evidence: Inference markers in character voice (“So there was…; Evans presumably”), Associative leap from roses to “fields of Greece”

passage: “You brute! You brute!” cried Septimus, seeing human nature, that is Dr. Holmes, enter the room.
explanation: While the exclamation is spoken, the participial phrase renders Septimus’s interior identification of Holmes with “human nature,” a recurring personification in his mind.
evidence: Interior mapping embedded in narration (“seeing human nature, that is Dr. Holmes”), Consistent with earlier FID personifications of “Human nature… Holmes was on him”
notes: Dialogue is not SOC, but the interior identification in the clause constitutes FID.

passage: Probably, Rezia thought, that was Sir William Bradshaw’s house with the grey motor car in front of it.
explanation: Explicitly tagged thought presenting Rezia’s tentative inference.
evidence: Reporting phrase (“Rezia thought”), Third-person narration of cognitive content (“Probably… that was…”)

passage: Probably, Rezia thought, that was Sir William Bradshaw’s house with the grey motor car in front of it.
explanation: The narrator explicitly reports Rezia’s present thought about identifying Sir William’s house.
evidence: Reporting verb: "Rezia thought", Third-person psycho-narration about her cognition
notes: Borderline conventional narration, but per Humphrey this counts as omniscient description of consciousness since the content is her thought.

passage: Her ladyship waited with the rugs about her knees an hour or more, leaning back, thinking sometimes of the patient, sometimes, excusably, of the wall of gold, mounting minute by minute while she waited; the wall of gold that was mounting between them and all shifts and anxieties (she had borne them bravely; they had had their struggles) until she felt wedged on a calm ocean, where only spice winds blow; respected, admired, envied, with scarcely anything left to wish for, though she regretted her stoutness; large dinner-parties every Thursday night to the profession; an occasional bazaar to be opened; Royalty greeted; too little time, alas, with her husband, whose work grew and grew; a boy doing well at Eton; she would have liked a daughter too; interests she had, however, in plenty; child welfare; the after-care of the epileptic, and photography, so that if there was a church building, or a church decaying, she bribed the sexton, got the key and took photographs, which were scarcely to be distinguished from the work of professionals, while she waited.
explanation: Lady Bradshaw’s unspoken ruminations are rendered in free indirect style, drifting associatively through comforts, status, and minor regrets.
evidence: Associative listing of satisfactions and duties without reporting verbs ("respected, admired, envied... though she regretted her stoutness"), Subjective metaphor: "felt wedged on a calm ocean, where only spice winds blow"; parenthetical aside indicates mental intrusion
notes: Hybrid texture: mostly FID with a brief narratorial parenthesis ("she had borne them bravely..."). The long, flowing syntax enacts complacent contentment (simulation of state).

passage: He could see the first moment they came into the room (the Warren Smiths they were called); he was certain directly he saw the man; it was a case of extreme gravity.

notes: Conventional psycho-narration, included under Humphrey’s broad category of omniscient description of consciousness.

passage: How long had Dr. Holmes been attending him? Six weeks. Prescribed a little bromide? Said there was nothing the matter? Ah yes (those general practitioners! thought Sir William. It took half his time to undo their blunders. Some were irreparable).

notes: Ambiguous with brief FID of Sir William; could be read as narrator’s summary of his thought.

passage: The European War--that little shindy of schoolboys with gunpowder? Had he served with distinction? He really forgot. In the War itself he had failed.

passage: But if he confessed? If he communicated? Would they let him off then, his torturers?

passage: But what was his crime? He could not remember it.

passage: (But it was growing late.)

passage: Love, trees, there is no crime--what was his message? He could not remember it.

passage: Never, never had Rezia felt such agony in her life! She had asked for help and been deserted! He had failed them! Sir William Bradshaw was not a nice man.

passage: This lady too (Rezia Warren Smith divined it) had her dwelling in Sir William’s heart, though concealed, as she mostly is, under some plausible disguise; some venerable name; love, duty, self sacrifice.

passage: subconsciously one was grateful to Rigby and Lowndes for giving one time ratified by Greenwich; and this gratitude (so Hugh Whitbread ruminated, dallying there in front of the shop window), naturally took the form later of buying off Rigby and Lowndes socks or shoes.

passage: Miss Brush resented this familiarity intensely. She thought him an underbred fellow.

passage: Lady Bruton preferred Richard Dalloway of course. He was made of much finer material. But she wouldn’t let them run down her poor dear Hugh. Anyhow, the difference between one man and another does not amount to much. She had never seen the sense of cutting people up, as Clarissa Dalloway did--cutting them up and sticking them together again; not at any rate when one was sixty-two.
explanation: A sustained stretch of Lady Bruton’s unspoken preferences and maxims in FID, with her characteristic generalizations and age-marked perspective.
evidence: Evaluative judgments ("finer material," "poor dear Hugh"), Maxims and age-indexed generalization: "when one was sixty-two"

passage: She had got them there on false pretences, to help her out of a difficulty--

evidence: Access to unspoken motive ("false pretences"), No thought tag; conventional psycho-narration
notes: Borderline SoC: content is mental but style is conventional narration.

passage: Which was she now, the General’s great-grand-daughter? great-great-grand-daughter? Richard Dalloway asked himself.
explanation: Richard’s internal question appears in interrogative form with a light framing tag.

passage: Which was she now, the General’s great-grand-daughter? great-great-grand-daughter? Richard Dalloway asked himself.

passage: Sir Roderick, Sir Miles, Sir Talbot--that was it. It was remarkable how in that family the likeness persisted in the women. She should have been a general of dragoons herself. And Richard would have served under her, cheerfully; he had the greatest respect for her; he cherished these romantic views about well-set-up old women of pedigree, and would have liked, in his good-humoured way, to bring some young hot-heads of his acquaintance to lunch with her; as if a type like hers could be bred of amiable tea-drinking enthusiasts!

passage: Better wait to put before them the question that bothered her (about making an appeal to the public; if so, in what terms and so on), better wait until they have had their coffee, Lady Bruton thought; and so laid the carnations down beside her plate.

passage: “I met Clarissa in the Park this morning,” said Hugh Whitbread, diving into the casserole, anxious to pay himself this little tribute, for he had only to come to London and he met everybody at once;

passage: but greedy, one of the greediest men she had ever known, Milly Brush thought,

passage: They all smiled. Peter Walsh! And Mr. Dalloway was genuinely glad, Milly Brush thought; and Mr. Whitbread thought only of his chicken. Peter Walsh! All three, Lady Bruton, Hugh Whitbread, and Richard Dalloway, remembered the same thing--how passionately Peter had been in love; been rejected; gone to India; come a cropper; made a mess of things; and Richard Dalloway had a very great liking for the dear old fellow too.

evidence: Group-generalizing verbs: "remembered the same thing"; "was genuinely glad ... thought only of his chicken", Exclamatory anaphora signaling mental recall: "Peter Walsh! ... Peter Walsh!"
notes: Ambiguous whether the exclamations are free indirect (SoC) or narratorial rhetoric; kept as omniscient description with FID flavor.

passage: Milly Brush saw that; saw a depth in the brown of his eyes; saw him hesitate; consider; which interested her, as Mr. Dalloway always interested her, for what was he thinking, she wondered, about Peter Walsh? That Peter Walsh had been in love with Clarissa; that he would go back directly after lunch and find Clarissa; that he would tell her, in so many words, that he loved her. Yes, he would say that.
explanation: Milly’s mind speculates about Richard’s thoughts in a chain of hypotheticals; rhetorical questions and anaphoric that-clauses render her inner construction.
evidence: Rhetorical question anchored to her: "what was he thinking, she wondered", Constructed scenario with modals: "he would go back ... he would tell her ... Yes, he would say that."
notes: Secondary device marked as reverie/fantasy because she imagines a future scene rather than recalling a memory.

passage: It was vaguely flattering to them all. He had come back, battered, unsuccessful, to their secure shores. But to help him, they reflected, was impossible; there was some flaw in his character.

explanation: The narrator summarizes the group’s inward reactions and judgment rather than presenting any single mind directly.
evidence: Generic summation of feeling: "It was vaguely flattering to them all", Explicit mental-process verb in plural: "they reflected"

passage: He wrinkled lugubriously, consequentially, at the thought of the letters he would write to the heads of Government offices about “my old friend, Peter Walsh,” and so on.

evidence: Mental nominalization: "at the thought of the letters he would write", Third-person, narrator-inflected idiom (no direct character voice)

passage: Murmuring London flowed up to her, and her hand, lying on the sofa back, curled upon some imaginary baton such as her grandfathers might have held, holding which she seemed, drowsy and heavy, to be commanding battalions marching to Canada, and those good fellows walking across London, that territory of theirs, that little bit of carpet, Mayfair.

passage: And Richard Dalloway and Hugh Whitbread hesitated at the corner of Conduit Street at the very moment that Millicent Bruton, lying on the sofa, let the thread snap; snored.

passage: In Norfolk, of which Richard Dalloway was half thinking, a soft warm wind blew back the petals; confused the waters; ruffled the flowering grasses. passage: still Richard was torpid; could not think or move.

passage: Goodness knows he didn’t want to go buying necklaces with Hugh. But there are tides in the body. Morning meets afternoon.
