Richard didn’t care a straw what became of Emigration; about that letter, whether the editor put it in or not.

passage: Let him give it to a girl, if he must buy jewels--any girl, any girl in the street. For the worthlessness of this life did strike Richard pretty forcibly--buying necklaces for Evelyn. If he’d had a boy he’d have said, Work, work. But he had his Elizabeth; he adored his Elizabeth.

evidence: Imperative/volitive FID: "Let him give it to a girl...", Shift in affect voiced directly: "worthlessness of this life... he adored his Elizabeth"

evidence: Character-colored qualifier: "awfully odd", Affect reported as felt thought: "It pained him to remember"

passage: so Richard’s mind, recovering from its lethargy, set now on his wife, Clarissa, whom Peter Walsh had loved so passionately; and Richard had had a sudden vision of her there at luncheon; of himself and Clarissa; of their life together;

passage: but doubted his own taste.

passage: And yet Richard couldn’t have said that to save his life! Why these people stood that damned insolence he could not conceive. Hugh was becoming an intolerable ass. Richard Dalloway could not stand more than an hour of his society.

passage: But he wanted to come in holding something. Flowers? Yes, flowers, since he did not trust his taste in gold; any number of flowers, roses, orchids, to celebrate what was, reckoning things as you will, an event; this feeling about her when they spoke of Peter Walsh at luncheon; and they never spoke of it; not for years had they spoken of it; which, he thought, grasping his red and white roses together (a vast bunch in tissue paper), is the greatest mistake in the world.
explanation: A flowing sequence of interior questions/answers and asides captures Richard’s desire and rationale; the parenthetical inserts a sensory-present detail into the thought stream.
evidence: Question-answer cadence: "Flowers? Yes, flowers...", Parenthetical intrusion: "(a vast bunch in tissue paper)" within ongoing thought, Embedded tag "he thought" mid-flow

passage: The time comes when it can’t be said; one’s too shy to say it, he thought, pocketing his sixpence or two of change, setting off with his great bunch held against his body to Westminster to say straight out in so many words (whatever she might think of him), holding out his flowers, “I love you.” Why not?
explanation: Richard’s inward maxim and plan are voiced directly with an embedded tag; the parenthetical aside imagines Clarissa’s response; the closing question seals his resolve.
evidence: Embedded thought tag: "he thought" amidst interior generalization, Parenthetical aside: "(whatever she might think of him)", Direct interior rhetorical: "Why not?"

passage: Here he was walking across London to say to Clarissa in so many words that he loved her. Which one never does say, he thought. Partly one’s lazy; partly one’s shy. explanation: Richard’s inward narration of his act merges with a maxim, tagged thought, and fragmentary phrases that simulate the difficulty of sustained thought about Clarissa.
evidence: Tagged thought: "Which one never does say, he thought.", Fragmentation and self-qualification: "Partly one’s lazy; partly one’s shy... except in starts... their whole life."

passage: The time comes when it can’t be said; one’s too shy to say it, he thought, pocketing his sixpence or two of change, setting off with his great bunch held against his body to Westminster to say straight out in so many words (whatever she might think of him), holding out his flowers, “I love you.” Why not? Really it was a miracle thinking of the war, and thousands of poor chaps, with all their lives before them, shovelled together, already half forgotten; it was a miracle. Here he was walking across London to say to Clarissa in so many words that he loved her. Which one never does say, he thought. Partly one’s lazy; partly one’s shy. And Clarissa--it was difficult to think of her; except in starts, as at luncheon, when he saw her quite distinctly; their whole life.
explanation: A contiguous stretch rendering Richard’s unspoken intentions, evaluations, and self-questions largely in his idiom. Reporting tags are light (“he thought”), and the free-standing rhetorical ‘Why not?’ and generalizing ‘one’ are hallmarks of FID. Parenthetical aside signals an interior qualification.
evidence: Tagging + FID drift: “he thought … to say … ‘I love you.’ Why not?”, Character-colored diction and generalizing ‘one’: “Which one never does say… Partly one’s lazy; partly one’s shy.”, Parenthetical aside marking thought intrusion: “(whatever she might think of him)”
notes: Contains a brief imagined performance of speech (“I love you.”) → constructive projection; hence reverie_fantasy as secondary.

passage: He stopped at the crossing; and repeated--being simple by nature, and undebauched, because he had tramped, and shot; being pertinacious and dogged, having championed the down-trodden and followed his instincts in the House of Commons; being preserved in his simplicity yet at the same time grown rather speechless, rather stiff--he repeated that it was a miracle that he should have married Clarissa; a miracle--his life had been a miracle, he thought; hesitating to cross.
explanation: The narrator supplies biographical appraisal and character-trait framing while reporting Richard’s repeated thought about ‘miracle’; we are told-about consciousness with a brief internal tag.
evidence: Authorial appraisal clauses: “being simple by nature … pertinacious and dogged … preserved in his simplicity…”, Reported thought embedded: “he repeated that it was a miracle… his life had been a miracle, he thought”
notes: Hybrid feel: descriptive psycho-narration framing a reported inner refrain.

passage: But it did make his blood boil to see little creatures of five or six crossing Piccadilly alone. The police ought to have stopped the traffic at once. He had no illusions about the London police. Indeed, he was collecting evidence of their malpractices; and those costermongers, not allowed to stand their barrows in the streets; and prostitutes, good Lord, the fault wasn’t in them, nor in young men either, but in our detestable social system and so forth; all of which he considered, could be seen considering, grey, dogged, dapper, clean, as he walked across the Park to tell his wife that he loved her.
explanation: Richard’s judgments and exclamation (“good Lord”) are rendered in his register (FID), but the sentence resolves into an externalizing narrator (‘all of which he considered, could be seen considering’), blending inner discourse with psycho-narration.
evidence: Character idiom and judgment: “good Lord … our detestable social system”, Narratorial externalization: “all of which he considered, could be seen considering”
notes: Affective tinge of indignation (‘blood boil’) is reported rather than formally simulated.

passage: For he would say it in so many words, when he came into the room.
explanation: Unspoken intention phrased from within Richard’s perspective without a reporting verb in this clause; classic free indirect pledge to act.
evidence: Future-oriented resolve framed as his thought: “he would say it”, Sits within a run of Richard-focused interior rendering

passage: Because it is a thousand pities never to say what one feels, he thought, crossing the Green Park and observing with pleasure how in the shade of the trees whole families, poor families, were sprawling; children kicking up their legs; sucking milk; paper bags thrown about, which could easily be picked up (if people objected) by one of those fat gentlemen in livery; for he was of opinion that every park, and every square, during the summer months should be open to children (the grass of the park flushed and faded, lighting up the poor mothers of Westminster and their crawling babies, as if a yellow lamp were moved beneath).
explanation: Tagged interior rationale and perceptions unfold in Richard’s register. Parentheses flag asides and subjective observations; dense sensory description fuses perception with value-judgment.
evidence: Reporting tag: “he thought” anchors the inner discourse, Parenthetical intrusions: “(if people objected) … (the grass of the park … as if a yellow lamp…)”

passage: But what could be done for female vagrants like that poor creature, stretched on her elbow (as if she had flung herself on the earth, rid of all ties, to observe curiously, to speculate boldly, to consider the whys and the wherefores, impudent, loose-lipped, humorous), he did not know.
explanation: Richard’s inward question and confessed ignorance are rendered with an interior frame (‘he did not know’) and a vivid as-if image in parentheses that reflects his speculative viewing.
evidence: Interior questioning: “But what could be done… he did not know.”, Parenthetical subjective imaging: “(as if she had flung herself on the earth…)”

passage: Bearing his flowers like a weapon, Richard Dalloway approached her; intent he passed her; still there was time for a spark between them--she laughed at the sight of him, he smiled good-humouredly, considering the problem of the female vagrant; not that they would ever speak. But he would tell Clarissa that he loved her, in so many words.
explanation: Externally narrated movement and social glance are overlaid with an explicit mention of his in-mind activity (“considering the problem…”) and reaffirmed resolve in FID (“he would tell Clarissa…”).
evidence: Interior verb within action: “considering the problem of the female vagrant”, FID resolve: “he would tell Clarissa that he loved her”

passage: But she had often said to him that she had been right not to marry Peter Walsh; which, knowing Clarissa, was obviously true; she wanted support. Not that she was weak; but she wanted support.
explanation: Richard’s retrospective judgments of Clarissa’s choice and character are rendered in evaluative FID (‘knowing Clarissa, was obviously true’), aligning diction with his viewpoint.
evidence: Evaluative aside in his idiom: “knowing Clarissa, was obviously true”, Repetitive emphasis reflecting inner conviction: “she wanted support. Not that she was weak; but she wanted support.”

passage: As for Buckingham Palace (like an old prima donna facing the audience all in white) you can’t deny it a certain dignity, he considered, nor despise what does, after all, stand to millions of people (a little crowd was waiting at the gate to see the King drive out) for a symbol, absurd though it is; a child with a box of bricks could have done better, he thought; looking at the memorial to Queen Victoria (whom he could remember in her horn spectacles driving through Kensington), its white mound, its billowing motherliness; but he liked being ruled by the descendant of Horsa; he liked continuity; and the sense of handing on the traditions of the past.
explanation: A sustained FID appraisal of Palace and memorial in Richard’s evaluative idiom; parenthetical insertions mark momentary perceptual and mnemonic asides; similes/imagery convey subjective perception.
evidence: Interior evaluatives: “you can’t deny it a certain dignity … absurd though it is; a child with a box of bricks could have done better”, Parenthetical asides: “(like an old prima donna…) … (a little crowd was waiting…) … (whom he could remember…)”

passage: It was a great age in which to have lived. Indeed, his own life was a miracle; let him make no mistake about it; here he was, in the prime of life, walking to his house in Westminster to tell Clarissa that he loved her.
explanation: Self-affirming generalization and self-addressed caution (‘let him make no mistake about it’) in Richard’s inner voice; no reporting verb needed.
evidence: Self-address form: “let him make no mistake about it”, Continues his inward theme of ‘miracle’ and resolve

passage: Happiness is this he thought. It is this, he said, as he entered Dean’s Yard.
explanation: First clause is tagged interior assertion; the second shifts to self-addressed utterance (‘he said’)—a brief soliloquy. The clipped repetition enacts contented immediacy.
evidence: Tag + inward assertion: “Happiness is this he thought.”, Self-directed speech marker: “It is this, he said” with rhythmic repetition
notes: Borderline between murmured speech and inner voice; treated here as soliloquy layered on interior monologue.

passage: Lunch parties waste the entire afternoon, he thought, approaching his door.
explanation: Tagged inner generalization in Richard’s idiom.
evidence: Reporting tag: “he thought”, Gnomic phrasing of subjective judgment

passage: The sound of Big Ben flooded Clarissa’s drawing-room, where she sat, ever so annoyed, at her writing-table; worried; annoyed.
explanation: Narrator frames Clarissa’s felt mood as the bell’s sound ‘floods’ the room; we are told about her inner state through sensory metaphor rather than immersed in her diction.
evidence: Psycho-narration of affect: “ever so annoyed … worried; annoyed.”, Metaphoric sensory overlay: “The sound of Big Ben flooded…”
notes: Borderline set-up that segues into FID; included here as Humphrey-style omniscient description of consciousness.

passage: It was perfectly true that she had not asked Ellie Henderson to her party; but she had done it on purpose.
explanation: Clarissa’s inward justification appears in her idiom as a firm assertion and concessive defense, with the narrator receded.
evidence: Evaluative self-justification: “It was perfectly true…”, Defensive clause: “but she had done it on purpose.”

passage: But why should she invite all the dull women in London to her parties? Why should Mrs. Marsham interfere? And there was Elizabeth closeted all this time with Doris Kilman. Anything more nauseating she could not conceive. Prayer at this hour with that woman.
explanation: Rhetorical questions and evaluative outbursts in Clarissa’s register represent unspoken annoyance and disgust; the staccato fragment ‘Prayer at this hour…’ heightens affect.
evidence: Unattributed interior questions: “why should…? Why should…?”, Affective evaluative: “Anything more nauseating she could not conceive.”

passage: Who at this hour? Three, good Heavens! Three already!
explanation: Free-standing interior exclamations tied to the striking clock; the repetition and exclamation marks simulate her startled impatience.
evidence: Rhetorical questions/exclamations with no reporting tag, Temporal anchoring to the bell (‘Three… Three already!’) indicates immediate inner reaction

passage: What a surprise!
explanation: An untagged, exclamatory interior reaction to Richard’s entry rendered directly in Clarissa’s idiom.
evidence: Standalone exclamation without attribution, Immediate response to the narrated entrance of Richard

passage: She had failed him, once at Constantinople; and Lady Bruton, whose lunch parties were said to be extraordinarily amusing, had not asked her.
explanation: A rapid, memory-tinted linkage from the sight of Richard-with-flowers to past failure and a social slight; rendered as Clarissa’s inward recollection/evaluation.
evidence: Past-tense recollection in her register: “She had failed him, once at Constantinople”, Associative shift to Lady Bruton without explanatory narration
notes: Could be read as brief psycho-narration; classified as FID given the surrounding interior frame.

passage: (But he could not bring himself to say he loved her; not in so many words.)
explanation: Parenthetical aside supplies knowledge of Richard’s inward inhibition from a narratorial vantage.
evidence: Parentheses signal an aside intruding on the external scene, Telling-about inner reluctance: “could not bring himself to say…”
notes: Narrator supplies content that Clarissa infers; thus not FID.

passage: (But he could not tell her he loved her. He held her hand. Happiness is this, he thought.)
explanation: A parenthetical splice of Richard’s inner admission coupled with a tagged interior assertion of happiness; the form compresses thought into an aside.
evidence: Reporting tag inside parentheses: “he thought”, Juxtaposition of inability-to-say with inward affirmation: “Happiness is this”

passage: He had not said “I love you”; but he held her hand. Happiness is this, is this, he thought.
explanation: Tagged assertion of happiness in Richard’s mind; the echoed “is this, is this” enacts contentment’s rhythm.
evidence: Reporting tag: “he thought”, Rhythmic repetition: “is this, is this”

passage: …it was a very odd thing how much Clarissa minded about her parties, he thought.
explanation: Explicitly tagged thought evaluating Clarissa’s concern for parties.
evidence: Reporting tag: “he thought”, Evaluation phrased as inner observation: “a very odd thing…”
notes: Ellipsis indicates the preceding spoken clause; the thought is the parenthetical comment.

passage: However--what was he going to say? If she worried about these parties he would not let her give them. Did she wish she had married Peter? But he must go.
explanation: A run of inward questions, resolutions, and self-commands in Richard’s idiom without tags—standard FID.
evidence: Unattributed interior questions: “what was he going to say? … Did she wish…?”, Self-directed resolve: “he would not let her give them … he must go.”

passage: …and she wondered what? Why? There were the roses.
explanation: Clarissa’s immediate questions and redirecting perception (‘There were the roses.’) are untagged inner reactions.
evidence: Fragmented interior questions: “what? Why?”, Shift of inner focus to a sensory anchor: “There were the roses.”

passage: And there is a dignity in people; a solitude; even between husband and wife a gulf; and that one must respect, thought Clarissa, watching him open the door; for one would not part with it oneself, or take it, against his will, from one’s husband, without losing one’s independence, one’s self-respect--something, after all, priceless.
explanation: Tagged inner maxim articulated in Clarissa’s generalizing register (‘one’), representing a composed but unspoken thought.
evidence: Reporting tag: “thought Clarissa”, Generalizing diction of inner credo: “there is a dignity… one must respect… one would not…”

passage: How like him! He would go on saying “An hour’s complete rest after luncheon” to the end of time, because a doctor had ordered it once. It was like him to take what doctors said literally; part of his adorable, divine simplicity, which no one had to the same extent; which made him go and do the thing while she and Peter frittered their time away bickering. He was already half-way to the House of Commons, to his Armenians, his Albanians, having settled her on the sofa, looking at his roses. And people would say, “Clarissa Dalloway is spoilt.” She cared much more for her roses than for the Armenians. Hunted out of existence, maimed, frozen, the victims of cruelty and injustice (she had heard Richard say so over and over again)--no, she could feel nothing for the Albanians, or was it the Armenians? but she loved her roses (didn’t that help the Armenians?)--the only flowers she could bear to see cut. But Richard was already at the House of Commons; at his Committee, having settled all her difficulties. But no; alas, that was not true. He did not see the reasons against asking Ellie Henderson. She would do it, of course, as he wished it. Since he had brought the pillows, she would lie down....
explanation: An extended FID stream of Clarissa’s inward appraisal of Richard, imagined movements (House of Commons), and imagined judgments (‘people would say…’). Parentheses mark aside-thoughts that fold in remembered phrases and self-queries.
evidence: Unattributed exclamative: “How like him!” and gnomic judgments in her idiom, Constructed scenarios and imagined voices: “He was already half-way… And people would say, ‘Clarissa Dalloway is spoilt.’”, Parenthetical asides: “(she had heard Richard say…) … (didn’t that help the Armenians?)”
notes: Reverie_fantasy tags the imagined staging of Richard already being at the House/Committee and the hypothetical chorus of ‘people’.

passage: But--but--why did she suddenly feel, for no reason that she could discover, desperately unhappy? As a person who has dropped some grain of pearl or diamond into the grass and parts the tall blades very carefully, this way and that, and searches here and there vainly, and at last spies it there at the roots, so she went through one thing and another;
explanation: Begins with Clarissa’s untagged inner question signaling a sudden mood shift, then moves to a narratorial simile that describes her mental search as a tactile image—enacting her anxious rummaging.
evidence: Interior self-questioning: “why did she suddenly feel … desperately unhappy?”, Extended simile mapping cognition to action: “As a person who has dropped some grain of pearl… so she went through one thing and another”

passage: no, it was not Sally Seton saying that Richard would never be in the Cabinet because he had a second-class brain (it came back to her); no, she did not mind that; nor was it to do with Elizabeth either and Doris Kilman; those were facts. It was a feeling, some unpleasant feeling, earlier in the day perhaps; something that Peter had said, combined with some depression of her own, in her bedroom, taking off her hat; and what Richard had said had added to it, but what had he said? There were his roses.
explanation: A chain of eliminations and associative probes rendered in Clarissa’s idiom (negations, catalogues), with a parenthetical memory tag and an inward question before settling on a sensory anchor (‘There were his roses.’).
evidence: Repetitive negations in inner sifting: “no, it was not… no, she did not mind… nor was it…”, Parenthetical recall: “(it came back to her)” and interior question “but what had he said?”

passage: Her parties! That was it! Her parties! Both of them criticised her very unfairly, laughed at her very unjustly, for her parties. That was it! That was it! Well, how was she going to defend herself? Now that she knew what it was, she felt perfectly happy.
explanation: Staccato exclamations enact an inner epiphany; her inward rhetorical question and immediate mood shift to happiness are rendered in FID.
evidence: Epiphanic repetition: “That was it! That was it!”, Immediate affective reversal stated in her idiom: “she felt perfectly happy.”

passage: They thought, or Peter at any rate thought, that she enjoyed imposing herself; liked to have famous people about her; great names; was simply a snob in short. Well, Peter might think so. Richard merely thought it foolish of her to like excitement when she knew it was bad for her heart. It was childish, he thought. And both were quite wrong. What she liked was simply life.
explanation: Clarissa’s inward rehearsal of others’ judgments and her corrective assertion; the cadence and evaluatives are hers, untagged save for embedded ‘he thought’ reporting Richard’s stance.
evidence: Unattributed inner paraphrase of others: “They thought … Peter … Richard …”, Self-definition in decisive cadence: “What she liked was simply life.”

passage: Since she was lying on the sofa, cloistered, exempt, the presence of this thing which she felt to be so obvious became physically existent; with robes of sound from the street, sunny, with hot breath, whispering, blowing out the blinds.
explanation: The narrator figures Clarissa’s sense of ‘life’ as a quasi-physical presence using synesthetic imagery; form enacts sensuous calm/pleasure rather than advancing thought content.
evidence: Metaphoric embodiment: “the presence … became physically existent”, Sensory personification: “robes of sound … hot breath … blowing out the blinds”

passage: But suppose Peter said to her, “Yes, yes, but your parties--what’s the sense of your parties?” all she could say was (and nobody could be expected to understand): They’re an offering; which sounded horribly vague.
explanation: Clarissa constructs a hypothetical exchange with Peter; her inward response (‘They’re an offering’) is staged, with a parenthetical aside articulating self-consciousness about its vagueness.
evidence: Conditional projection: “suppose Peter said to her…”, Parenthetical meta-comment: “(and nobody could be expected to understand)”

passage: But who was Peter to make out that life was all plain sailing?--Peter always in love, always in love with the wrong woman? What’s your love? she might say to him. And she knew his answer; how it is the most important thing in the world and no woman possibly understood it. Very well. But could any man understand what she meant either? about life?
explanation: Continuation of constructed exchange: she imagines her rejoinder and Peter’s answer; the counter-question keeps the imagined dialogue going internally.
evidence: Imagined rejoinder: “What’s your love? she might say to him.”, Anticipated answer framed as known: “And she knew his answer…”

passage: She could not imagine Peter or Richard taking the trouble to give a party for no reason whatever.
explanation: An untagged inner generalization (negative imagining) in Clarissa’s evaluative idiom.
evidence: Negative modal phrasing: “could not imagine…”, Sits within the ongoing hypothetical exchange sequence

passage: But to go deeper, beneath what people said (and these judgements, how superficial, how fragmentary they are!) in her own mind now, what did it mean to her, this thing she called life? Oh, it was very queer. Here was So-and-so in South Kensington; some one up in Bayswater; and somebody else, say, in Mayfair. And she felt quite continuously a sense of their existence; and she felt what a waste; and she felt what a pity; and she felt if only they could be brought together; so she did it. And it was an offering; to combine, to create; but to whom? An offering for the sake of offering, perhaps. Anyhow, it was her gift. Nothing else had she of the slightest importance; could not think, write, even play the piano. She muddled Armenians and Turks; loved success; hated discomfort; must be liked; talked oceans of nonsense: and to this day, ask her what the Equator was, and she did not know.
explanation: A sustained, inward self-definition and credo in Clarissa’s idiom, complete with a parenthetical meta-aside judging ‘judgements.’ The anaphoric ‘and she felt…’ sequences render her continuous inner sense of others.
evidence: Parenthetical aside voicing inner critique: “(and these judgements, how superficial, how fragmentary they are!)”, Anaphoric interior cadence: “And she felt … and she felt … and she felt …”

passage: All the same, that one day should follow another; Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday; that one should wake up in the morning; see the sky; walk in the park; meet Hugh Whitbread; then suddenly in came Peter; then these roses; it was enough. After that, how unbelievable death was!--that it must end; and no one in the whole world would know how she had loved it all; how, every instant....
explanation: Clarissa’s inner litany of quotidian pleasures culminates in a rapturous assertion; the rhythmic, accumulative syntax formally enacts her joy and awe at life’s texture, with a sudden counter-thought of death.
evidence: Accumulative, paratactic listing: “Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday … see the sky; walk in the park; … then these roses; it was enough.”, Affective exclamation and dash: “how unbelievable death was!--that it must end;”

passage: Yes, Miss Kilman stood on the landing, and wore a mackintosh; but had her reasons. First, it was cheap; second, she was over forty; and did not, after all, dress to please. She was poor, moreover; degradingly poor.
explanation: Miss Kilman’s inward self-justifications (reasons, frugality, age) are voiced through a close third that adopts her perspective.
evidence: Rationalizing sequence: “had her reasons. First … second …”, Value-laden self-assessment: “degradingly poor.”
notes: Could be read as psycho-narration; included as FID given the interior ‘reasons’ list in her register.

passage: Otherwise she would not be taking jobs from people like the Dalloways; from rich people, who liked to be kind. Mr. Dalloway, to do him justice, had been kind. But Mrs. Dalloway had not. She had been merely condescending. She came from the most worthless of all classes--the rich, with a smattering of culture.
explanation: Resentful appraisals of the Dalloways in Miss Kilman’s idiom render her unspoken grievances; no reporting tag needed.
evidence: Pejorative phrasing: “people like the Dalloways … the most worthless of all classes”, Concessive aside: “to do him justice” reflects her evaluative voice

passage: She considered that she had a perfect right to anything that the Dalloways did for her. She had been cheated. Yes, the word was no exaggeration, for surely a girl has a right to some kind of happiness? And she had never been happy, what with being so clumsy and so poor.
explanation: A run of inward justifications and self-pity in Miss Kilman’s voice, including a rhetorical question marking personal grievance.
evidence: Assertive self-justification: “a perfect right … She had been cheated.”, Interior rhetorical question: “surely a girl has a right to some kind of happiness?”

passage: They turned her out because she would not pretend that the Germans were all villains--when she had German friends, when the only happy days of her life had been spent in Germany! And after all, she could read history.
explanation: Inward narrative of grievance in her own justificatory cadence; exclamatory emphasis underscores personal stake.
evidence: First-person-adjacent defensiveness in third: “would not pretend … And after all, she could read history.”, Exclamative justification tied to personal past

passage: Then Our Lord had come to her (and here she always bowed her head).
explanation: Narrator reports the conversion moment and inserts a parenthetical stage-direction-like note about her habitual gesture—an externalized sign of inner piety.
evidence: Reportage of inner turning point: “Our Lord had come to her”, Parenthetical behavioral marker: “(and here she always bowed her head)”

passage: Now she did not envy women like Clarissa Dalloway; she pitied them. She pitied and despised them from the bottom of her heart, as she stood on the soft carpet, looking at the old engraving of a little girl with a muff.
explanation: Her interior attitude—pity and contempt—is presented in her evaluative idiom within a close third frame.
evidence: Asserted inner stance: “she pitied them … pitied and despised them”, Anchored in an immediate perceptual scene (carpet/engraving) but focused on inner judgment

passage: With all this luxury going on, what hope was there for a better state of things? Instead of lying on a sofa--“My mother is resting,” Elizabeth had said--she should have been in a factory; behind a counter; Mrs. Dalloway and all the other fine ladies!
explanation: Rhetorical question and moralizing proposals voiced in Miss Kilman’s inner register; the quoted fragment is a remembered phrase intruding into her thought.
evidence: Rhetorical lament: “what hope was there for a better state of things?”, Inserted memory in quotes within inner discourse

passage: Bitter and burning, Miss Kilman had turned into a church two years three months ago. She had heard the Rev. Edward Whittaker preach; the boys sing; had seen the solemn lights descend, and whether it was the music, or the voices (she herself when alone in the evening found comfort in a violin; but the sound was excruciating; she had no ear), the hot and turbulent feelings which boiled and surged in her had been assuaged as she sat there, and she had wept copiously, and gone to call on Mr. Whittaker at his private house in Kensington. It was the hand of God, he said. The Lord had shown her the way. So now, whenever the hot and painful feelings boiled within her, this hatred of Mrs. Dalloway, this grudge against the world, she thought of God. She thought of Mr. Whittaker. Rage was succeeded by calm. A sweet savour filled her veins, her lips parted, and, standing formidable upon the landing in her mackintosh, she looked with steady and sinister serenity at Mrs. Dalloway, who came out with her daughter.
explanation: The narrator recounts Miss Kilman’s conversion and describes the mechanism by which rage yields to devotional calm, complete with parenthetical note about her poor ear. The rhythm shifts to a serene cadence as calm arrives.
evidence: Causal narration of inner process: “Rage was succeeded by calm. A sweet savour filled her veins…”, Parenthetical self-assessment embedded: “(she herself … found comfort in a violin; … she had no ear)”

passage: Fool! Simpleton! You who have known neither sorrow nor pleasure; who have trifled your life away!
explanation: Direct, untagged exclamations addressing Clarissa enact Miss Kilman’s inner invective without narratorial mediation.
evidence: Second-person address to Clarissa with no reporting verb, Exclamatory, fragmentary syntax mimicking unfiltered thought
notes: Embedded in a sentence that introduces ‘Miss Kilman felt’; the exclamations themselves proceed without mediation.

passage: If only she could make her weep; could ruin her; humiliate her; bring her to her knees crying, You are right!
explanation: A chain of desired actions articulated in Miss Kilman’s inward voice; the asyndetic, repetitive structure enacts obsessive hostility.
evidence: Wish-form construction: “If only she could…”, Asyndetic series of imperatives of harm: “could ruin … humiliate … bring her to her knees”

passage: This a Christian--this woman! This woman had taken her daughter from her! She in touch with invisible presences! Heavy, ugly, commonplace, without kindness or grace, she know the meaning of life!
explanation: Clarissa’s untagged, staccato exclamations render her inward shock and scorn at Miss Kilman; compressed syntax and rhetorical questions simulate indignation.
evidence: Fragmented exclamations without reporting: “This a Christian--this woman! … She in touch with invisible presences!”, Affective epithets in her inner voice: “Heavy, ugly, commonplace, without kindness or grace”
notes: The preceding sentence (‘Clarissa was really shocked.’) frames the affect; the quoted lines are the interior outburst itself.

passage: If only she could make her weep; could ruin her; humiliate her; bring her to her knees crying, You are right! But this was God’s will, not Miss Kilman’s. It was to be a religious victory.
explanation: Third-person rendering of Miss Kilman's unspoken wishes and justification in her own idiom without overt reporting verbs, approximating her inner drive for a 'religious victory.'
evidence: Subjunctive wish construction ('If only she could make her weep; could ruin her; humiliate her') typical of thought rendered in FID, Value-laden diction aligned with Kilman ('God’s will,' 'religious victory') without narrator commentary
notes: Could also be read as omniscient psycho-narration summarizing motive. The absence of 'she thought' and the intensity of diction tilt toward FID.

passage: This a Christian--this woman! This woman had taken her daughter from her! She in touch with invisible presences! Heavy, ugly, commonplace, without kindness or grace, she know the meaning of life!
explanation: Exclamatory fragments in Clarissa’s evaluative idiom, no reporting tag, rendering her immediate indignation toward Miss Kilman.
evidence: Elliptical syntax and exclamations ('This a Christian--this woman!') signal unmediated thought texture, Derisive catalog ('Heavy, ugly, commonplace...') reflects Clarissa’s value-laden perspective
notes: Minor grammatical truncation ('she know') underscores immediacy of thought.

passage: Odd it was, as Miss Kilman stood there (and stand she did, with the power and taciturnity of some prehistoric monster armoured for primeval warfare), how, second by second, the idea of her diminished, how hatred (which was for ideas, not people) crumbled, how she lost her malignity, her size, became second by second merely Miss Kilman, in a mackintosh, whom Heaven knows Clarissa would have liked to help.
explanation: Narrator describes the ebbing of Clarissa’s hatred with interpretive commentary while parenthetical aside and metaphor mirror Clarissa’s perceiving consciousness.
evidence: Explanatory insertions ('hatred (which was for ideas, not people)') belong to a narratorial voice, Parenthetical metaphor of 'prehistoric monster' signals subjective perception framed by narrator
notes: Bridges toward Clarissa’s perspective; could be read as FID-laced psycho-narration. Parentheses function as a consciousness intrusion marker.

passage: Love and religion! thought Clarissa, going back into the drawing-room, tingling all over. How detestable, how detestable they are! For now that the body of Miss Kilman was not before her, it overwhelmed her--the idea. The cruelest things in the world, she thought, seeing them clumsy, hot, domineering, hypocritical, eavesdropping, jealous, infinitely cruel and unscrupulous, dressed in a mackintosh coat, on the landing; love and religion. Had she ever tried to convert any one herself? Did she not wish everybody merely to be themselves?
explanation: Clarissa’s inner exclamations and self-questions are presented with a light 'thought Clarissa' frame, then flow into untagged evaluations and rhetorical questions.
evidence: Explicit but light reporting tag ('thought Clarissa') followed by free, exclamatory judgments, Rhetorical self-questioning characteristic of interior monologue ('Had she ever tried to convert any one herself?')
notes: Imagery personifies abstractions ('dressed in a mackintosh coat') as part of Clarissa’s subjective frame.

passage: And she watched out of the window the old lady opposite climbing upstairs. Let her climb upstairs if she wanted to; let her stop; then let her, as Clarissa had often seen her, gain her bedroom, part her curtains, and disappear again into the background. Somehow one respected that--that old woman looking out of the window, quite unconscious that she was being watched. There was something solemn in it--but love and religion would destroy that, whatever it was, the privacy of the soul. The odious Kilman would destroy it. Yet it was a sight that made her want to cry. Love destroyed too. Everything that was fine, everything that was true went. Take Peter Walsh now. There was a man, charming, clever, with ideas about everything. If you wanted to know about Pope, say, or Addison, or just to talk nonsense, what people were like, what things meant, Peter knew better than any one. It was Peter who had helped her; Peter who had lent her books. But look at the women he loved--vulgar, trivial, commonplace. Think of Peter in love--he came to see her after all these years, and what did he talk about? Himself. Horrible passion! she thought. Degrading passion! she thought, thinking of Kilman and her Elizabeth walking to the Army and Navy Stores.
explanation: Extended FID: Clarissa’s perceptions of the old woman lead associatively to reflections on privacy, love, and Peter Walsh, with evaluative diction and self-commands embedded.
evidence: Imperatives directed at herself/scene ('Let her climb upstairs...'), a hallmark of thought rendering, Associative leap ('Take Peter Walsh now') shifting topics by internal logic rather than narrative causality
notes: The intermittent 'she thought' tags punctuate but do not dominate; the flow remains character-anchored.

passage: How extraordinary it was, strange, yes, touching, to see the old lady (they had been neighbours ever so many years) move away from the window, as if she were attached to that sound, that string. Gigantic as it was, it had something to do with her. Down, down, into the midst of ordinary things the finger fell making the moment solemn. She was forced, so Clarissa imagined, by that sound, to move, to go--but where? Clarissa tried to follow her as she turned and disappeared, and could still just see her white cap moving at the back of the bedroom. She was still there moving about at the other end of the room.
explanation: Clarissa’s subjective perception is rendered in her evaluative exclamation and imagined causality, with the clock’s 'finger' image shaping her interior view.
evidence: Exclamatory evaluative opening ('How extraordinary it was, strange, yes, touching') indicates interior stance, Parenthetical aside about being neighbours and imagined causal link ('so Clarissa imagined')
notes: Blends exterior observation with imagined interiority of the old lady; still anchored in Clarissa’s consciousness.

passage: Why creeds and prayers and mackintoshes? when, thought Clarissa, that’s the miracle, that’s the mystery; that old lady, she meant, whom she could see going from chest of drawers to dressing-table. She could still see her. And the supreme mystery which Kilman might say she had solved, or Peter might say he had solved, but Clarissa didn’t believe either of them had the ghost of an idea of solving, was simply this: here was one room; there another. Did religion solve that, or love?
explanation: Rhetorical questions and the deictic immediacy ('here was one room; there another') render Clarissa’s metaphysical musing in her idiom.
evidence: Embedded 'thought Clarissa' tag followed by free-standing questions, Deictic immediacy and repetition enact present-tense cognitive attention

passage: Love--but here the other clock, the clock which always struck two minutes after Big Ben, came shuffling in with its lap full of odds and ends, which it dumped down as if Big Ben were all very well with his majesty laying down the law, so solemn, so just, but she must remember all sorts of little things besides--Mrs. Marsham, Ellie Henderson, glasses for ices--all sorts of little things came flooding and lapping and dancing in on the wake of that solemn stroke which lay flat like a bar of gold on the sea. Mrs. Marsham, Ellie Henderson, glasses for ices. She must telephone now at once. Volubly, troublously, the late clock sounded, coming in on the wake of Big Ben, with its lap full of trifles.
explanation: Anthropomorphized 'late clock' ushers in a cascade of party errands; list fragments and an imperative self-command enact Clarissa’s bustling thought-stream.
evidence: Associative item-listing ('Mrs. Marsham, Ellie Henderson, glasses for ices') without syntactic integration, Imperative to self ('She must telephone now at once') amid sensory-laden metaphors ('lap full of odds and ends')

passage: Beaten up, broken up by the assault of carriages, the brutality of vans, the eager advance of myriads of angular men, of flaunting women, the domes and spires of offices and hospitals, the last relics of this lap full of odds and ends seemed to break, like the spray of an exhausted wave, upon the body of Miss Kilman standing still in the street for a moment to mutter “It is the flesh.”
explanation: Cinematic cut from Clarissa’s internal 'odds and ends' to Miss Kilman in the street via metaphorical 'spray' breaking over her body, linking minds across shared urban scene.
evidence: Juxtaposition of interior metaphorical residue with external view of Kilman ('seemed to break... upon the body of Miss Kilman'), Immediate entry into Kilman’s muttered self-address
notes: Functions as a structural device to move between consciousnesses.

passage: It was the flesh that she must control. Clarissa Dalloway had insulted her. That she expected. But she had not triumphed; she had not mastered the flesh. Ugly, clumsy, Clarissa Dalloway had laughed at her for being that; and had revived the fleshly desires, for she minded looking as she did beside Clarissa. Nor could she talk as she did. But why wish to resemble her? Why? She despised Mrs. Dalloway from the bottom of her heart. She was not serious. She was not good. Her life was a tissue of vanity and deceit. Yet Doris Kilman had been overcome. She had, as a matter of fact, very nearly burst into tears when Clarissa Dalloway laughed at her.
explanation: Narrated in third person but saturated with Kilman’s evaluative diction and self-accusatory focus; questions and anaphora render her inner struggle.
evidence: Self-directed moral frames ('She was not serious. She was not good.') aligned with Kilman’s values, Rhetorical question ('But why wish to resemble her? Why?') as interior turn
notes: Blends psycho-narration with FID; narrator presence remains but yields diction to Kilman.

passage: “It is the flesh, it is the flesh,” she muttered (it being her habit to talk aloud) trying to subdue this turbulent and painful feeling as she walked down Victoria Street.
explanation: Quoted self-address, rhythmic repetition, and self-command characteristic of a soliloquy, performed aloud but addressed to self.
evidence: Direct quoted self-speech to no interlocutor ('It is the flesh, it is the flesh'), Repetitive phrasing enacts effort to subdue feeling
notes: Spoken aloud, but functionally interior self-address; overlaps with indirect narration in the clause after the quote.

passage: But she would concentrate her mind upon something else until she had reached the pillar-box. At any rate she had got Elizabeth. But she would think of something else; she would think of Russia; until she reached the pillar-box.
explanation: Self-commands and planned distraction in third person convey Kilman’s attempt to manage intrusive emotion.
evidence: Anaphoric self-directives ('she would... she would...') mimicking inner self-coaching, Temporal goal-setting to a concrete marker ('until she had reached the pillar-box')

passage: She prayed to God. She could not help being ugly; she could not afford to buy pretty clothes. Clarissa Dalloway had laughed--but she would concentrate her mind upon something else until she had reached the pillar-box. At any rate she had got Elizabeth. But she would think of something else; she would think of Russia; until she reached the pillar-box. How nice it must be, she said, in the country, struggling, as Mr. Whittaker had told her, with that violent grudge against the world which had scorned her, sneered at her, cast her off, beginning with this indignity--the infliction of her unlovable body which people could not bear to see. Do her hair as she might, her forehead remained like an egg, bald, white. No clothes suited her. She might buy anything. And for a woman, of course, that meant never meeting the opposite sex. Never would she come first with any one. Sometimes lately it had seemed to her that, except for Elizabeth, her food was all that she lived for; her comforts; her dinner, her tea; her hot-water bottle at night. But one must fight; vanquish; have faith in God. Mr. Whittaker had said she was there for a purpose. But no one knew the agony! He said, pointing to the crucifix, that God knew. But why should she have to suffer when other women, like Clarissa Dalloway, escaped? Knowledge comes through suffering, said Mr. Whittaker.
explanation: Sustained FID of Kilman’s self-perception, grievances, and consolations (Mr. Whittaker), with aphoristic self-commands and metaphor for her appearance.
evidence: Self-evaluative catalog in her value frame ('unlovable body,' 'Never would she come first'), Alternation of commands and complaint ('one must fight; vanquish; have faith in God' vs. 'no one knew the agony!')
notes: Overlaps with prior entry; included for completeness of the sustained interior sequence.

passage: “The flesh,” she muttered.
explanation: Brief self-addressed utterance, the distilled refrain of her inner struggle, spoken to herself.
evidence: Quoted self-speech addressed to no one, Repetition of thematic self-accusation ('the flesh')

passage: Elizabeth rather wondered whether Miss Kilman could be hungry. It was her way of eating, eating with intensity, then looking, again and again, at a plate of sugared cakes on the table next them; then, when a lady and a child sat down and the child took the cake, could Miss Kilman really mind it? Yes, Miss Kilman did mind it. She had wanted that cake--the pink one.
explanation: Narrator reports Elizabeth’s wondering and inferred judgments about Kilman’s desire in conventional psycho-narration with intermittent rhetorical question.
evidence: Reporting verb/phrasing ('Elizabeth rather wondered'), Embedded rhetorical question reflecting Elizabeth’s (and narrator’s) inference ('could Miss Kilman really mind it?')
notes: Straddles Elizabeth’s perspective and narratorial inference; less immersive than FID.

passage: Ah, but she must not go! Miss Kilman could not let her go! this youth, that was so beautiful, this girl, whom she genuinely loved!
explanation: Exclamatory self-interdiction and apostrophe-like phrasing render Kilman’s intense, possessive inner desire to keep Elizabeth.
evidence: Exclamations with self-directed modal ('she must not go!'), Vocative phrasing ('this youth... this girl') without a reporting frame

passage: And really she would like to go.
explanation: Concise FID presenting Elizabeth’s inner preference while seated with Kilman.
evidence: Modal statement of private desire ('she would like to go'), No reporting tag; content anchored in Elizabeth’s immediate situation

passage: She was about to split asunder, she felt. The agony was so terrific. If she could grasp her, if she could clasp her, if she could make her hers absolutely and forever and then die; that was all she wanted. But to sit here, unable to think of anything to say; to see Elizabeth turning against her; to be felt repulsive even by her--it was too much; she could not stand it.
explanation: Kilman’s inner extremity is rendered via conditional fantasies and self-evaluations, with 'she felt' lightly framing heightened affect.
evidence: Conditional fantasizing ('If she could grasp her... make her hers... and then die') as interior construction, Self-assessment and threshold language ('it was too much; she could not stand it')

passage: “People don’t ask me to parties”--and she knew as she said it that it was this egotism that was her undoing; Mr. Whittaker had warned her; but she could not help it. She had suffered so horribly. “Why should they ask me?” she said. “I’m plain, I’m unhappy.” She knew it was idiotic. But it was all those people passing--people with parcels who despised her, who made her say it. However, she was Doris Kilman. She had her degree. She was a woman who had made her way in the world. Her knowledge of modern history was more than respectable.
explanation: Within and around spoken lines, the narrator dips into Kilman’s concurrent self-awareness and defensive self-assertion, marking interiority amid dialogue.
evidence: Meta-awareness ('she knew as she said it... egotism... her undoing') reveals unspoken cognition, Shift to affirmations ('However, she was Doris Kilman. She had her degree.') in Kilman’s self-justifying register
notes: The em dash after the quoted clause acts as a typographic hinge for the thought-aside.

passage: “I pity”--she meant to say “your mother” but no, she could not, not to Elizabeth. “I pity other people,” she said, “more.”
explanation: An interior aside intrudes into speech, revealing Kilman’s inhibited intention; the dash marks the thought’s emergence and suppression.
evidence: Parenthetical-like intrusion ('she meant to say “your mother” but no, she could not') amid dialogue, Demonstrates unspoken content that shapes but does not reach utterance

passage: Was Miss Kilman going to say anything more?
explanation: A free indirect question registering Elizabeth’s silent anticipation during a pause.
evidence: Direct question form without attribution reflects character’s immediate wondering, Context situates Elizabeth as silent observer awaiting Kilman’s next move

passage: She had gone. Mrs. Dalloway had triumphed. Elizabeth had gone. Beauty had gone, youth had gone.
explanation: Staccato, anaphoric clauses convey Kilman’s inner lament immediately after Elizabeth’s departure.
evidence: Elliptical, repetitive structure mimicking grief’s blunt certainties, Value-laden substitutions ('Beauty had gone, youth had gone') reflect subjective catastrophizing

passage: But Miss Kilman held her tent before her face. Now she was deserted; now rejoined. New worshippers came in from the street to replace the strollers, and still, as people gazed round and shuffled past the tomb of the Unknown Warrior, still she barred her eyes with her fingers and tried in this double darkness, for the light in the Abbey was bodiless, to aspire above the vanities, the desires, the commodities, to rid herself both of hatred and of love.
explanation: Narrator reports Kilman’s fluctuating felt connection to God and her effortful aspiration, describing interior struggle from the outside.
evidence: Alternation of felt states ('Now she was deserted; now rejoined') presented as reported inner experience, Interpretive framing of intent ('tried... to rid herself both of hatred and of love')
notes: Conventional psycho-narration focused on inner states; less immersive than FID but content is consciousness.

passage: and saw Miss Kilman at the end of the row, praying, praying, and, being still on the threshold of their underworld, thought of her sympathetically as a soul haunting the same territory; a soul cut out of immaterial substance; not a woman, a soul.
character_pov: Mr. Fletcher and Mrs. Gorham (collective viewpoint)
explanation: The narrator recounts other worshippers’ thoughts about Kilman in metaphorical terms, describing their interior response rather than rendering it directly.
evidence: Explicit cognition verb ('thought of her sympathetically') attributed to onlookers, Metaphoric appraisal ('a soul cut out of immaterial substance') belongs to reported consciousness
notes: Edges toward lyrical narrator voice; included as omniscient consciousness description per taxonomy.

passage: It was so nice to be out of doors. She thought perhaps she need not go home just yet. It was so nice to be out in the air. So she would get on to an omnibus.
explanation: Short, affect-laden sentences and a plan render Elizabeth’s immediate feelings and intention in FID.
evidence: Affective evaluatives ('so nice') repeated in simple syntax typical of inner register, Future-oriented self-plan ('So she would get on to an omnibus')

passage: People were beginning to compare her to poplar trees, early dawn, hyacinths, fawns, running water, and garden lilies; and it made her life a burden to her, for she so much preferred being left alone to do what she liked in the country, but they would compare her to lilies, and she had to go to parties, and London was so dreary compared with being alone in the country with her father and the dogs.
explanation: Narrator relays Elizabeth’s felt burden and preferences, embedding social perceptions and her reaction in conventional psycho-narration.
evidence: Causal framing of emotion ('it made her life a burden to her'), Contrast of preferences ('preferred being left alone... London was so dreary') as reported inner stance
notes: Imagery belongs to social gaze; the affective appraisal is Elizabeth’s.

passage: But which should she get on to? She had no preferences. Of course, she would not push her way. She inclined to be passive. It was expression she needed,
explanation: A free indirect question followed by evaluative self-descriptors capture Elizabeth’s indecision and temperament.
evidence: Direct question without tag ('But which should she get on to?') signals inner query, Self-characterization in simple declaratives ('She had no preferences... She inclined to be passive')

passage: Well, thought Clarissa about three o’clock in the morning, reading Baron Marbot for she could not sleep, it proves she has a heart.
explanation: Parenthetical time-shifted insertion of Clarissa’s thought about Elizabeth, explicitly tagged but phrased in her direct evaluative idiom.
evidence: Reporting tag ('thought Clarissa') with immediate subjective judgment ('it proves she has a heart'), Temporal cutaway to a different scene/time embedded within current narration
notes: Temporal montage rather than spatial; included under space_montage for structural juxtaposition per available device set.

passage: She was delighted to be free. The fresh air was so delicious. It had been so stuffy in the Army and Navy Stores. And now it was like riding, to be rushing up Whitehall; and to each movement of the omnibus the beautiful body in the fawn-coloured coat responded freely like a rider, like the figurehead of a ship, for the breeze slightly disarrayed her; the heat gave her cheeks the pallor of white painted wood; and her fine eyes, having no eyes to meet, gazed ahead, blank, bright, with the staring incredible innocence of sculpture.
explanation: Narrator conveys Elizabeth’s felt relief and exhilaration, then extends into sensory-laden figurative description that expresses her subjective state.
evidence: Affective report ('She was delighted to be free. The fresh air was so delicious.'), Figurative extensions ('like a rider... figurehead of a ship') enacting the sensation of movement and freedom
notes: Begins as psycho-narration of feeling, then shifts into impressionistic imagery aligned with Elizabeth’s embodied state.

passage: She was delighted to be free. The fresh air was so delicious. It had been so stuffy in the Army and Navy Stores. And now it was like riding, to be rushing up Whitehall; and to each movement of the omnibus the beautiful body in the fawn-coloured coat responded freely like a rider, like the figurehead of a ship, for the breeze slightly disarrayed her; the heat gave her cheeks the pallor of white painted wood; and her fine eyes, having no eyes to meet, gazed ahead, blank, bright, with the staring incredible innocence of sculpture.
explanation: Elizabeth’s unspoken sensations and elation are rendered in third person with her idiom (“so delicious,” “so stuffy”), sliding into richly figurative description that still tracks her embodied perception.
evidence: Evaluative sensory diction aligned with Elizabeth (“so delicious,” “so stuffy”)., No reporting verb; third-person free rendering of interior content (FID)., Extended similes (“like the figurehead of a ship”) tie external scene to her felt state.
notes: Hybrid texture: Elizabeth’s FID merges with narrator-driven imagery; the latter increases narrator presence but remains anchored in her embodied perception.

passage: It was always talking about her own sufferings that made Miss Kilman so difficult. And was she right? If it was being on committees and giving up hours and hours every day (she hardly ever saw him in London) that helped the poor, her father did that, goodness knows,--if that was what Miss Kilman meant about being a Christian; but it was so difficult to say.
explanation: Elizabeth’s evaluative, questioning thought about Miss Kilman is presented as FID, including a parenthetical aside characteristic of unframed thought.
evidence: Rhetorical question: “And was she right?”, Colloquialism signaling character’s voice: “goodness knows,”, Parenthetical aside “(she hardly ever saw him in London)” marks an intrusive thought.

passage: Oh, she would like to go a little further. Another penny was it to the Strand? Here was another penny then. She would go up the Strand.
explanation: Elizabeth’s immediate, self-directed impulses are rendered in third person with exclamatory cue and a self-question, typical of FID.
evidence: Exclamatory interjection “Oh,” and decision statements without reporting verbs., Self-question: “Another penny was it to the Strand?”

passage: She liked people who were ill. And every profession is open to the women of your generation, said Miss Kilman. So she might be a doctor. She might be a farmer. Animals are often ill. She might own a thousand acres and have people under her. She would go and see them in their cottages.
explanation: Elizabeth extrapolates from Miss Kilman’s dictum to imagine future roles; the chain of possibilities is rendered in FID with character-colored logic.
evidence: Direct carryover from quoted speech into Elizabeth’s unreported projections (“So she might be a doctor.”)., Iterative modality (“might be… might own… would go…”) typical of internal scenario-building.
notes: Borderline with reverie/fantasy; however the scenelets are brief, not a sustained constructed fantasy.

passage: This was Somerset House. One might be a very good farmer--and that, strangely enough though Miss Kilman had her share in it, was almost entirely due to Somerset House. It looked so splendid, so serious, that great grey building. And she liked the feeling of people working. She liked those churches, like shapes of grey paper, breasting the stream of the Strand.
explanation: Perceptions and evaluations of the built environment are given in FID, with metaphorical description expressing Elizabeth’s state of mind.
evidence: Deictic immediacy: “This was Somerset House.”, Character-tinted evaluatives (“so splendid, so serious”) and simile (“like shapes of grey paper”).

passage: It was quite different here from Westminster, she thought, getting off at Chancery Lane.
explanation: The narrator overtly reports Elizabeth’s comparative judgment using a reporting verb, describing her mental content rather than immersing in it.
evidence: Explicit reporting tag: “she thought,”, Third-person narrator stance summarizing her perception.
notes: Occurs within a broader FID flow; a brief reassertion of narrator frame.

passage: In short, she would like to have a profession. She would become a doctor, a farmer, possibly go into Parliament, if she found it necessary, all because of the Strand.
explanation: Condensed summation of Elizabeth’s internal resolve rendered as FID in third person without reporting verbs.
evidence: Modality and intention (“would like,” “would become”)., Causal linkage in her own evaluative frame (“all because of the Strand”).

passage: The feet of those people busy about their activities, hands putting stone to stone, minds eternally occupied not with trivial chatterings (comparing women to poplars--which was rather exciting, of course, but very silly), but with thoughts of ships, of business, of law, of administration, and with it all so stately (she was in the Temple), gay (there was the river), pious (there was the Church), made her quite determined, whatever her mother might say, to become either a farmer or a doctor.
explanation: Elizabeth’s inner reasoning is cast as FID, with parenthetical asides and densely figurative cataloguing of the city that channels her resolve.
evidence: Parentheses for in-thought asides: “(she was in the Temple) … (there was the river) …”, Concluding internal resolution clause: “made her quite determined… to become either a farmer or a doctor.”

passage: But she was, of course, rather lazy. And it was much better to say nothing about it. It seemed so silly.
explanation: Self-assessment and self-censorship in Elizabeth’s idiom presented as third-person untagged thought.
evidence: Evaluative self-judgment (“rather lazy”) without reporting verbs., Colloquial summation (“It seemed so silly”).

passage: It was the sort of thing that did sometimes happen, when one was alone--buildings without architects’ names, crowds of people coming back from the city having more power than single clergymen in Kensington, than any of the books Miss Kilman had lent her, to stimulate what lay slumbrous, clumsy, and shy on the mind’s sandy floor to break surface, as a child suddenly stretches its arms; it was just that, perhaps, a sigh, a stretch of the arms, an impulse, a revelation, which has its effects for ever, and then down again it went to the sandy floor.
explanation: The narrator describes an interior activation of Elizabeth’s mind via extended metaphor (“mind’s sandy floor”), simulating the experiential texture of a sudden revelation.
evidence: Psycho-narration of mental process rather than direct thought., Metaphoric enactment of rising/settling consciousness (“break surface… down again it went”).

passage: She must go home. She must dress for dinner. But what was the time?--where was a clock?
explanation: Immediate self-commands and questions are presented as untagged third-person thought.
evidence: Imperative self-commands in third person (“She must…” as FID)., Direct internal question with dash interrupt (“what was the time?--where was a clock?”).

passage: She walked just a little way towards St. Paul’s, shyly, like some one penetrating on tiptoe, exploring a strange house by night with a candle, on edge lest the owner should suddenly fling wide his bedroom door and ask her business, nor did she dare wander off into queer alleys, tempting bye-streets, any more than in a strange house open doors which might be bedroom doors, or sitting-room doors, or lead straight to the larder.
explanation: Sustained simile renders Elizabeth’s subjective apprehension as if exploring a strange house, blurring perception and inner feeling.
evidence: Extended simile mapping street-walking to anxious house exploration., Subjectivity indexed by “shyly” and imagined consequences (“ask her business”).
notes: Anchored in Elizabeth’s perspective but strongly narrator-shaped; classified via imagery device capturing consciousness.

passage: For no Dalloways came down the Strand daily; she was a pioneer, a stray, venturing, trusting.
explanation: Self-characterization in FID captures Elizabeth’s sense of herself as exploratory and independent.
evidence: Character-colored evaluatives (“a pioneer, a stray”)., No reporting verbs; third-person rendering of inner stance.

passage: In many ways, her mother felt, she was extremely immature, like a child still, attached to dolls, to old slippers; a perfect baby; and that was charming. But then, of course, there was in the Dalloway family the tradition of public service. Abbesses, principals, head mistresses, dignitaries, in the republic of women--without being brilliant, any of them, they were that.
explanation: The narrator summarizes Clarissa’s standing attitude toward Elizabeth, reporting her feelings and family ideology rather than immersing in a present-tense stream.
evidence: Reporting phrase “her mother felt,”, Generalized, summarizing tone about attitudes rather than a single thought-moment.
notes: Temporal generalization blurs momentary consciousness; included per Humphrey’s ‘omniscient description of consciousness.’

passage: She liked the geniality, sisterhood, motherhood, brotherhood of this uproar. It seemed to her good.
explanation: Elizabeth’s evaluative perception of the Strand’s atmosphere is rendered as FID.
evidence: Character-inflected catalog (“geniality, sisterhood… brotherhood”)., Ungrounded judgment “It seemed to her good” without reporting cue beyond deictic phrasing.

passage: But it was later than she thought. Her mother would not like her to be wandering off alone like this.
explanation: Elizabeth’s internal correction and reference to her mother’s disapproval are presented as FID.
evidence: Correction/self-realization phrasing (“later than she thought”)., Projection of mother’s view as part of inner monologue (“would not like her…”).

passage: Going and coming, beckoning, signalling, so the light and shadow which now made the wall grey, now the bananas bright yellow, now made the Strand grey, now made the omnibuses bright yellow, seemed to Septimus Warren Smith lying on the sofa in the sitting-room; watching the watery gold glow and fade with the astonishing sensibility of some live creature on the roses, on the wall-paper.
explanation: A cinematic cut links Elizabeth’s Strand scene with Septimus’s interior, using shared light/color motifs; the sentence effects a rapid perspective shift while anchoring in Septimus’s perception.
evidence: Juxtaposition of Strand/omnibus light with Septimus’s wall/bananas via “seemed to Septimus…”, Cinematic parallelism (“now… now… now… now…”) bridging spaces.
notes: Also initiates a new interior sequence for Septimus rendered with impressionistic imagery.

passage: Outside the trees dragged their leaves like nets through the depths of the air; the sound of water was in the room and through the waves came the voices of birds singing. Every power poured its treasures on his head, and his hand lay there on the back of the sofa, as he had seen his hand lie when he was bathing, floating, on the top of the waves, while far away on shore he heard dogs barking and barking far away.
explanation: Septimus’s hallucinatory, exalted perception is described from outside with lush imagery that enacts his euphoric state.
evidence: Metaphoric sensory flooding (“Every power poured its treasures on his head”)., Echoing distance markers and repetition (“barking and barking far away”) simulate trance.

passage: Fear no more, says the heart in the body; fear no more. He was not afraid.
explanation: Unframed internal utterance appears verbatim (“Fear no more…”), followed by a near-immediate FID affirmation of its effect.
evidence: Absence of reporting verbs; aphoristic, internal voice presented directly., Immediate internal conclusion in character register: “He was not afraid.”
notes: Hybrid edge: the second sentence is FID; grouped for coherence of the reassurance beat.

passage: At every moment Nature signified by some laughing hint like that gold spot which went round the wall--there, there, there--her determination to show, by brandishing her plumes, shaking her tresses, flinging her mantle this way and that, beautifully, always beautifully, and standing close up to breathe through her hollowed hands Shakespeare’s words, her meaning.
explanation: The narrator renders Septimus’s ecstatic anthropomorphizing of Nature; dashes mimic deictic fixation and the syntax enacts rapture.
evidence: Deictic dash-encapsulated repetition (“--there, there, there--”)., Anthropomorphic imagery conveying inner state (“brandishing her plumes… breathing… Shakespeare’s words”).

passage: He was happy then. But she could not bear to see him smiling. It was not marriage; it was not being one’s husband to look strange like that, always to be starting, laughing, sitting hour after hour silent, or clutching her and telling her to write.
explanation: Rezia’s judgments and distress are presented in her idiom via FID; the prescriptive “It was not marriage” articulates her inner standard.
evidence: Character-colored norm assertion (“It was not marriage; it was not being one’s husband…”)., No reporting verb; third-person free rendering of her discomfort.

passage: The table drawer was full of those writings; about war; about Shakespeare; about great discoveries; how there is no death. Lately he had become excited suddenly for no reason (and both Dr. Holmes and Sir William Bradshaw said excitement was the worst thing for him), and waved his hands and cried out that he knew the truth! He knew everything! That man, his friend who was killed, Evans, had come, he said. He was singing behind the screen. She wrote it down just as he spoke it. Some things were very beautiful; others sheer nonsense. And he was always stopping in the middle, changing his mind; wanting to add something; hearing something new; listening with his hand up. But she heard nothing. And once they found the girl who did the room reading one of these papers in fits of laughter. It was a dreadful pity. For that made Septimus cry out about human cruelty--how they tear each other to pieces. The fallen, he said, they tear to pieces.
explanation: The narrator summarizes Septimus’s mental disturbances and Rezia’s perspective on them, reporting content of his beliefs and behaviors rather than immersing in their moment-to-moment flow.
evidence: Summative reporting across time (“Lately he had become… he would say…”)., Insertion of external authorities (Holmes/Bradshaw) emphasizes narrator mediation.
notes: Includes quoted outbursts, which are spoken, not SoC; retained here as part of omniscient summary of his consciousness.

passage: Through his eyelashes he could see her blurred outline; her little black body; her face and hands; her turning movements at the table, as she took up a reel, or looked (she was apt to lose things) for her silk.
explanation: Septimus’s narrowed, hyper-attentive perception of Rezia is described externally; the parenthetical aside reflects a character-tinted observation.
evidence: Sensory focus through partial vision (“Through his eyelashes…”)., Parenthesis with domestic observation (“she was apt to lose things”).

passage: He began, very cautiously, to open his eyes, to see whether a gramophone was really there. But real things--real things were too exciting. He must be cautious. He would not go mad. First he looked at the fashion papers on the lower shelf, then, gradually at the gramophone with the green trumpet. Nothing could be more exact. And so, gathering courage, he looked at the sideboard; the plate of bananas; the engraving of Queen Victoria and the Prince Consort; at the mantelpiece, with the jar of roses. None of these things moved. All were still; all were real.
explanation: Septimus’s self-directed cautions and reality-testing sequence are presented as FID, with listing and repetition enacting anxious vigilance.
evidence: Self-commands and assurances in third person (“He must be cautious. He would not go mad.”)., Repetitions that simulate checking: “real things–real things”; “All were still; all were real.”

passage: “Just now!” She said that with her Italian accent. She said that herself.
explanation: A fragmentary echo of Rezia’s phrasing is registered inside Septimus’s thought; the ensuing sentences are his unframed metacommentary on her speech.
evidence: Isolated exclamatory fragment reflects internal echoing (“Just now!”)., Commentary without reporting verbs (“She said that herself.”) indicates character’s inner note.
notes: The initial quoted fragment could also be read as immediate recall of recent dialogue; classified as FID due to introspective commentary.

passage: But no, there she was, perfectly natural, sewing, with the pursed lips that women have, the set, the melancholy expression, when sewing. But there was nothing terrible about it, he assured himself, looking a second time, a third time at her face, her hands, for what was frightening or disgusting in her as she sat there in broad daylight, sewing? Mrs. Peters had a spiteful tongue. Mr. Peters was in Hull. Why then rage and prophesy? Why fly scourged and outcast? Why be made to tremble and sob by the clouds? Why seek truths and deliver messages when Rezia sat sticking pins into the front of her dress, and Mr. Peters was in Hull?
explanation: Septimus argues himself back toward normalcy; rhetorical questions and repeated reassurance cues mark a self-directed inner discourse.
evidence: Rhetorical question cascade (“Why then… Why fly… Why be made… Why seek…”)., Self-assurance phrasing (“he assured himself”).

passage: Miracles, revelations, agonies, loneliness, falling through the sea, down, down into the flames, all were burnt out, for he had a sense, as he watched Rezia trimming the straw hat for Mrs. Peters, of a coverlet of flowers.
explanation: The narrator condenses Septimus’s previous torments into an image of soothing containment, enacting his momentary calm through metaphor.
evidence: Catalog of inner states replaced by calming metaphor (“coverlet of flowers”)., Repetitive descent phrase (“down, down…”) transitions to extinguishing (“all were burnt out”).

passage: Never had she felt so happy! Never in her life!
explanation: Rezia’s intense joy is rendered in exclamatory FID; the rhythmic doubling enacts heightened affect.
evidence: Exclamatory repetition without reporting verb., Superlative framing of feeling (“Never… in her life!”).

passage: (Nobody ever made her laugh as Septimus did.)
explanation: A parenthetical aside intrudes Rezia’s private thought into the narrative flow, a typographic signal of consciousness shift.
evidence: Parentheses set off an interior aside., Character-evaluative content (“Nobody ever made her laugh as Septimus did”).

passage: What had she got in her work-box? She had ribbons and beads, tassels, artificial flowers.
explanation: A self-posed question and immediate inventory mark Rezia’s inner attention rendered in FID.
evidence: Direct internal question without reporting verbs., Immediate answer sequence reflects thought flow.

passage: When she sewed, he thought, she made a sound like a kettle on the hob; bubbling, murmuring, always busy, her strong little pointed fingers pinching and poking; her needle flashing straight. The sun might go in and out, on the tassels, on the wall-paper, but he would wait, he thought, stretching out his feet, looking at his ringed sock at the end of the sofa; he would wait in this warm place, this pocket of still air, which one comes on at the edge of a wood sometimes in the evening, when, because of a fall in the ground, or some arrangement of the trees (one must be scientific above all, scientific), warmth lingers, and the air buffets the cheek like the wing of a bird.
explanation: Though tagged by “he thought,” the passage dwells in Septimus’s sensuous, pacified perception; the parenthetical aside signals a sudden thought intrusion, and the rhythm enacts repose.
evidence: Explicit tags “he thought” frame psycho-narration of perception., Parenthesis inserts a meta-thought (“one must be scientific above all, scientific”).

passage: Never had he done anything which made him feel so proud. It was so real, it was so substantial, Mrs. Peters’ hat.
explanation: Septimus’s self-evaluation and felt solidity are rendered in third-person FID with emphatic rhythm that enacts pride.
evidence: Superlative, affect-laden phrasing (“Never had he done anything…”)., Elliptical emphasis on object as guarantor of reality (“so substantial, Mrs. Peters’ hat”).

passage: Yes, it would always make her happy to see that hat. He had become himself then, he had laughed then. They had been alone together. Always she would like that hat.
explanation: Rezia’s affirmative inner commentary is presented as FID; the initial “Yes,” and iterative “always” mark internal assent and projection.
evidence: Initial assent particle “Yes,” typical of internal agreement., Future habitual in thought (“would always make her happy,” “Always she would like”).

passage: He was very tired. He was very happy. He would sleep. He shut his eyes.
explanation: A staccato sequence presents Septimus’s internal assessment and decision; the clipped rhythm enacts his settling.
evidence: Short, paratactic sentences with no reporting verbs., Volitional statement of intent (“He would sleep”).

passage: But directly he saw nothing the sounds of the game became fainter and stranger and sounded like the cries of people seeking and not finding, and passing further and further away. They had lost him!
explanation: Narrator renders the auditory transformation accompanying Septimus’s inner withdrawal; the final cry is a direct interior exclamation embedded in the description.
evidence: Metaphoric re-casting of sounds as existential cries (“seeking and not finding”)., Direct exclamatory interior line: “They had lost him!”
notes: Hybrid moment: descriptive lead-in culminates in a direct interior exclamation.

passage: What did he see? The plate of bananas on the sideboard. Nobody was there (Rezia had taken the child to its mother. It was bedtime). That was it: to be alone forever. That was the doom pronounced in Milan when he came into the room and saw them cutting out buckram shapes with their scissors; to be alone forever. He was alone with the sideboard and the bananas.
