notes: Collective consciousness of two characters.

passage: She was at her worst--effusive, insincere. It was a great mistake to have come. He should have stayed at home and read his book, thought Peter Walsh; should have gone to a music hall; he should have stayed at home, for he knew no one.
explanation: Peter’s unspoken judgments and self-reproach are rendered in third person but in his idiom; the narrator tags his thought then recedes.
evidence: Attribution: "thought Peter Walsh", Character-colored diction and repetition: "he should have stayed ... should have gone ... he should have stayed"
notes: The opening sentence (“She was at her worst—effusive, insincere.”) reads as Peter’s FID evaluation sliding from narration.

passage: Oh dear, it was going to be a failure; a complete failure, Clarissa felt it in her bones as dear old Lord Lexham stood there apologising for his wife who had caught cold at the Buckingham Palace garden party. She could see Peter out of the tail of her eye, criticising her, there, in that corner. Why, after all, did she do these things? Why seek pinnacles and stand drenched in fire? Might it consume her anyhow! Burn her to cinders! Better anything, better brandish one’s torch and hurl it to earth than taper and dwindle away like some Ellie Henderson! It was extraordinary how Peter put her into these states just by coming and standing in a corner. He made her see herself; exaggerate. It was idiotic. But why did he come, then, merely to criticise? Why always take, never give? Why not risk one’s one little point of view? There he was wandering off, and she must speak to him. But she would not get the chance. Life was that--humiliation, renunciation.
explanation: Clarissa’s inner stream moves between FID and a rhetorically organized self-address (soliloquy) with vivid metaphoric intensity, enacting her agitated state.
evidence: Rhetorical questions and imperatives ("Why seek pinnacles ... Better anything ...") indicate self-address, Emotional lexicon and metaphors ("drenched in fire," "Burn her to cinders!") simulate heightened affect
notes: Begins with a light narrator tag (“Clarissa felt it in her bones”) then yields to Clarissa’s voice; classification reflects the internal shift.

passage: She did like old Lord Lexham. She did think it mattered, her party, and it made her feel quite sick to know that it was all going wrong, all falling flat. Anything, any explosion, any horror was better than people wandering aimlessly, standing in a bunch at a corner like Ellie Henderson, not even caring to hold themselves upright.
explanation: Clarissa’s judgments and nausea about the party’s failure are rendered in her idiom; the hyperbolic form enacts agitation.
evidence: Character-colored phrasing: "She did like ... She did think it mattered", Hyperbole: "Anything, any explosion, any horror was better ..."

passage: Ellie Henderson thought, watching them go, certain she knew that man’s face. A tall man, middle aged, rather fine eyes, dark, wearing spectacles, with a look of John Burrows. Edith would be sure to know.
explanation: Ellie’s present-tense identification effort is rendered in free indirect discourse following an explicit thought tag.
evidence: Attribution: "Ellie Henderson thought", Fragment-like appositions and private referent: "with a look of John Burrows. Edith would be sure to know."

passage: Was it draughty, Ellie Henderson wondered? She was subject to chills. But it did not matter that she should come down sneezing to-morrow; it was the girls with their naked shoulders she thought of, being trained to think of others by an old father, an invalid, late vicar of Bourton, but he was dead now; and her chills never went to her chest, never. It was the girls she thought of, the young girls with their bare shoulders, she herself having always been a wisp of a creature, with her thin hair and meagre profile; though now, past fifty, there was beginning to shine through some mild beam, something purified into distinction by years of self-abnegation but obscured again, perpetually, by her distressing gentility, her panic fear, which arose from three hundred pounds’ income, and her weaponless state (she could not earn a penny) and it made her timid, and more and more disqualified year by year to meet well-dressed people who did this sort of thing every night of the season, merely telling their maids “I’ll wear so and so,” whereas Ellie Henderson ran out nervously and bought cheap pink flowers, half a dozen, and then threw a shawl over her old black dress. For her invitation to Clarissa’s party had come at the last moment. She was not quite happy about it. She had a sort of feeling that Clarissa had not meant to ask her this year. Why should she? There was no reason really, except that they had always known each other. Indeed, they were cousins. But naturally they had rather drifted apart, Clarissa being so sought after. It was an event to her, going to a party. It was quite a treat just to see the lovely clothes. Wasn’t that Elizabeth, grown up, with her hair done in the fashionable way, in the pink dress? Yet she could not be more than seventeen. She was very, very handsome. But girls when they first came out didn’t seem to wear white as they used. (She must remember everything to tell Edith.) Girls wore straight frocks, perfectly tight, with skirts well above the ankles. It was not becoming, she thought.
explanation: A sustained FID rendering of Ellie’s anxieties, self-assessment, and social unease, with a parenthetical mental note and associative shifts across topics.
evidence: Attributions: "Ellie Henderson wondered"; "it was the girls ... she thought of"; "it was not becoming, she thought", Parenthetical aside: "(She must remember everything to tell Edith.)" marking an intrusion of inner note

passage: But it was Richard himself who felt that he could not let the poor creature go on standing there all the evening by herself.
explanation: Narrator conveys Richard’s felt obligation/resolve toward Ellie, describing his inner impulse.
evidence: "felt that he could not" directly signals interior motivation, No dialogue; state is reported, not spoken
notes: Borders on narratorial inference of ethical impulse; treated as psycho-narration per Humphrey.

passage: And Clarissa saw--she saw Ralph Lyon beat it back, and go on talking. So it wasn’t a failure after all! it was going to be all right now--her party. It had begun. It had started. But it was still touch and go. She must stand there for the present.
explanation: Clarissa’s appraisal of her party’s fortunes is rendered in her idiom with clipped, excited sentences; the curtain image anchors perception.
evidence: Exclamatory evaluation: "So it wasn’t a failure after all!", Short, rhythmic clauses: "It had begun. It had started." enact a lift in mood

passage: And yet for her own part, it was too much of an effort. She was not enjoying it. It was too much like being--just anybody, standing there; anybody could do it; yet this anybody she did a little admire, couldn’t help feeling that she had, anyhow, made this happen, that it marked a stage, this post that she felt herself to have become, for oddly enough she had quite forgotten what she looked like, but felt herself a stake driven in at the top of her stairs. Every time she gave a party she had this feeling of being something not herself, and that every one was unreal in one way; much more real in another. It was, she thought, partly their clothes, partly being taken out of their ordinary ways, partly the background, it was possible to say things you couldn’t say anyhow else, things that needed an effort; possible to go much deeper. But not for her; not yet anyhow.
explanation: An extended FID reflection where Clarissa figures herself as a ‘stake’ at the top of the stairs; the metaphor and cadence enact her strained self-division.
evidence: Self-metaphor: "felt herself a stake driven in at the top of her stairs", Interior generalizations framed by "she had this feeling" and parenthetic tag "it was, she thought"

passage: it was odd how standing there one felt them going on, going on, some quite old, some....
explanation: Clarissa’s generalized sense of other lives continuing is rendered in her idiom; repetition enacts the ongoing flow.
evidence: Generic "one felt" used within Clarissa’s focalization, Repetition: "going on, going on" simulates perception’s drift
notes: Slightly generalized diction (“one”), but anchored by surrounding Clarissa-focused narration.

passage: _What_ name? Lady Rosseter? But who on earth was Lady Rosseter?
explanation: Untagged immediate questions capture Clarissa’s present-tense confusion at the name announced; classic FID fragment.
evidence: Rhetorical questions with immediate deictic trigger (announced name), No reporting verb; pure thought texture
notes: Could be read as direct interior monologue; third-person context and lack of first-person markers suggest FID.

passage: “Clarissa!” That voice! It was Sally Seton! Sally Seton! after all these years! She loomed through a mist. For she hadn’t looked like _that_, Sally Seton, when Clarissa grasped the hot water can, to think of her under this roof, under this roof! Not like that! All on top of each other, embarrassed, laughing, words tumbled out--passing through London; heard from Clara Haydon; what a chance of seeing you! So I thrust myself in--without an invitation.... One might put down the hot water can quite composedly. The lustre had gone out of her. Yet it was extraordinary to see her again, older, happier, less lovely.
explanation: Clarissa’s excited recognitions and the sudden memory of the ‘hot water can’ arrive associatively amidst paraphrased speech; exclamatory fragments simulate her startled joy.
evidence: Exclamations and repetitions: "Sally Seton! Sally Seton!"; "under this roof, under this roof!", Associative leap to a concrete past object: "hot water can"
notes: Mixed with paraphrase of Sally’s speech; the inner recognitions and memory flashes are Clarissa’s FID layered over scene narration.

passage: She had the simplest egotism, the most open desire to be thought first always, and Clarissa loved her for being still like that. “I can’t believe it!” she cried, kindling all over with pleasure at the thought of the past.
explanation: Narrator summarizes Clarissa’s enduring affection for Sally and records her inward pleasure as an affective state accompanying speech.
evidence: "Clarissa loved her ..." reports an inner attitude, "kindling all over with pleasure at the thought of the past" signals interior affect linked to memory
notes: More psycho-narration than immersive SoC, but content is inner affect per Humphrey’s category.

passage: The Prime Minister? Was it really? Ellie Henderson marvelled. What a thing to tell Edith! One couldn’t laugh at him. He looked so ordinary. You might have stood him behind a counter and bought biscuits--poor chap, all rigged up in gold lace. And to be fair, as he went his rounds, first with Clarissa then with Richard escorting him, he did it very well. He tried to look somebody. It was amusing to watch.
explanation: Ellie’s inner commentary and amused appraisal of the Prime Minister are rendered in her idiom, tagged briefly, then left to run in FID.
evidence: Attribution: "Ellie Henderson marvelled", Private aside: "What a thing to tell Edith!" and evaluative comments ("He tried to look somebody.")
notes: Annotation ends before the broadened generalization about ‘they all knew,’ which shifts away from Ellie’s individual consciousness.

passage: Lord, lord, the snobbery of the English! thought Peter Walsh, standing in the corner.
explanation: An unspoken exclamation tagged to Peter; its rhythm and diction enact his spurt of contempt.
evidence: Explicit thought tag: "thought Peter Walsh", Exclamatory form: "Lord, lord, the snobbery ...!"

passage: There! That must be, by Jove it was, Hugh Whitbread, snuffing round the precincts of the great, grown rather fatter, rather whiter, the admirable Hugh! He looked always as if he were on duty, thought Peter, a privileged, but secretive being, hoarding secrets which he would die to defend, though it was only some little piece of tittle-tattle dropped by a court footman, which would be in all the papers to-morrow. Such were his rattles, his baubles, in playing with which he had grown white, come to the verge of old age, enjoying the respect and affection of all who had the privilege of knowing this type of the English public school man. Inevitably one made up things like that about Hugh; that was his style; the style of those admirable letters which Peter had read thousands of miles across the sea in the _Times_, and had thanked God he was out of that pernicious hubble-bubble if it were only to hear baboons chatter and coolies beat their wives. An olive-skinned youth from one of the Universities stood obsequiously by. Him he would patronise, initiate, teach how to get on. For he liked nothing better than doing kindnesses, making the hearts of old ladies palpitate with the joy of being thought of in their age, their affliction, thinking themselves quite forgotten, yet here was dear Hugh driving up and spending an hour talking of the past, remembering trifles, praising the home-made cake, though Hugh might eat cake with a Duchess any day of his life, and, to look at him, probably did spend a good deal of time in that agreeable occupation. The All-judging, the All-merciful, might excuse. Peter Walsh had no mercy. Villains there must be, and God knows the rascals who get hanged for battering the brains of a girl out in a train do less harm on the whole than Hugh Whitbread and his kindness. Look at him now, on tiptoe, dancing forward, bowing and scraping, as the Prime Minister and Lady Bruton emerged, intimating for all the world to see that he was privileged to say something, something private, to Lady Bruton as she passed. She stopped. She wagged her fine old head. She was thanking him presumably for some piece of servility. She had her toadies, minor officials in Government offices who ran about putting through little jobs on her behalf, in return for which she gave them luncheon. But she derived from the eighteenth century. She was all right.
explanation: A sustained FID passage where Peter’s contemptuous, rhetorically charged appraisal of Hugh unfolds in associative amplification and invective.
evidence: Character-tag within flow: "thought Peter" early, then narrator recedes, Rhetorical invective and apostrophe: "Villains there must be ... Look at him now"
notes: Some sentences summarize social types; overall voice and earlier tag secure Peter’s consciousness as anchor.

passage: And now Clarissa escorted her Prime Minister down the room, prancing, sparkling, with the stateliness of her grey hair. She wore ear-rings, and a silver-green mermaid’s dress. Lolloping on the waves and braiding her tresses she seemed, having that gift still; to be; to exist; to sum it all up in the moment as she passed; turned, caught her scarf in some other woman’s dress, unhitched it, laughed, all with the most perfect ease and air of a creature floating in its element. But age had brushed her; even as a mermaid might behold in her glass the setting sun on some very clear evening over the waves. There was a breath of tenderness; her severity, her prudery, her woodenness were all warmed through now, and she had about her as she said good-bye to the thick gold-laced man who was doing his best, and good luck to him, to look important, an inexpressible dignity; an exquisite cordiality; as if she wished the whole world well, and must now, being on the very verge and rim of things, take her leave. So she made him think. (But he was not in love.)
explanation: Peter’s perception is rendered through an extended mermaid conceit culminating in an explicit anchoring of his thought and a parenthetical denial of romantic feeling.
evidence: Anchor: "So she made him think. (But he was not in love.)", Sustained metaphorical lens: mermaid imagery organizing perception
notes: The metaphoric texture could be read as narratorial lyricism; closing sentence secures Peter’s consciousness.

passage: Indeed, Clarissa felt, the Prime Minister had been good to come. And, walking down the room with him, with Sally there and Peter there and Richard very pleased, with all those people rather inclined, perhaps, to envy, she had felt that intoxication of the moment, that dilatation of the nerves of the heart itself till it seemed to quiver, steeped, upright;--yes, but after all it was what other people felt, that; for, though she loved it and felt it tingle and sting, still these semblances, these triumphs (dear old Peter, for example, thinking her so brilliant), had a hollowness; at arm’s length they were, not in the heart; and it might be that she was growing old but they satisfied her no longer as they used; and suddenly, as she saw the Prime Minister go down the stairs, the gilt rim of the Sir Joshua picture of the little girl with a muff brought back Kilman with a rush; Kilman her enemy. That was satisfying; that was real. Ah, how she hated her--hot, hypocritical, corrupt; with all that power; Elizabeth’s seducer; the woman who had crept in to steal and defile (Richard would say, What nonsense!). She hated her: she loved her. It was enemies one wanted, not friends--not Mrs. Durrant and Clara, Sir William and Lady Bradshaw, Miss Truelock and Eleanor Gibson (whom she saw coming upstairs). They must find her if they wanted her. She was for the party! There was her old friend Sir Harry.
explanation: Clarissa’s sensory and affective surge (‘intoxication’) gives way to disenchantment and an associative leap to Miss Kilman, including a parenthetical intrusion of Richard’s imagined objection.
evidence: Affective simulation: "that dilatation of the nerves of the heart itself"; "felt it tingle and sting", Associative trigger: "the gilt rim ... brought back Kilman with a rush" and parenthesis: "(Richard would say, What nonsense!)"

passage: But no. Sir Harry could not tell Clarissa Dalloway (much though he liked her; of her type he thought her perfect, and threatened to paint her) his stories of the music hall stage. He chaffed her about her party. He missed his brandy. These circles, he said, were above him. But he liked her; respected her, in spite of her damnable, difficult upper-class refinement, which made it impossible to ask Clarissa Dalloway to sit on his knee.
explanation: Narrator relates Sir Harry’s private affections, discomforts, and impulses; interior states are reported rather than dramatized.
evidence: "He missed his brandy." states a private want, "he liked her; respected her" reports inner attitude

passage: And up came that wandering will-o’-the-wisp, that vagulous phosphorescence, old Mrs. Hilbery, stretching her hands to the blaze of his laughter (about the Duke and the Lady), which, as she heard it across the room, seemed to reassure her on a point which sometimes bothered her if she woke early in the morning and did not like to call her maid for a cup of tea; how it is certain we must die.
explanation: Narration describes a recurring private anxiety about mortality and the momentary reassurance provided by laughter; classic psycho-narration.
evidence: "seemed to reassure her on a point which sometimes bothered her", Content of the ‘point’: "how it is certain we must die"
notes: Parenthesis identifies the joke’s topic; not itself a consciousness marker, but the sentence renders her inner reassurance.

passage: She looked to-night, she said, so like her mother as she first saw her walking in a garden in a grey hat. And really Clarissa’s eyes filled with tears. Her mother, walking in a garden!
explanation: Clarissa’s sudden tenderness is rendered via a free indirect exclamation that echoes her mental picture.
evidence: Affective cue: "Clarissa’s eyes filled with tears", Exclamatory fragment: "Her mother, walking in a garden!"

passage: For Professor Brierly was a very queer fish. With all those degrees, honours, lectureships between him and the scribblers he suspected instantly an atmosphere not favourable to his queer compound; his prodigious learning and timidity; his wintry charm without cordiality; his innocence blent with snobbery; he quivered if made conscious by a lady’s unkempt hair, a youth’s boots, of an underworld, very creditable doubtless, of rebels, of ardent young people; of would-be geniuses, and intimated with a little toss of the head, with a sniff--Humph!--the value of moderation; of some slight training in the classics in order to appreciate Milton.
explanation: Narrator summarizes Brierly’s immediate mental reactions and habitual sensitivities—suspicions, quivering, and evaluative signals—toward others.
evidence: "he suspected instantly" indicates inner appraisal, "he quivered if made conscious ..." describes reflexive interior reaction
notes: Leans toward general characterization, but includes moment-to-moment interior reactions in the party context.

passage: That was the bond between them, and Hutton (a very bad poet) always felt that Mrs. Dalloway was far the best of the great ladies who took an interest in art.
explanation: Narrator reports Hutton’s enduring inner estimation of Clarissa; a stated mental attitude rather than dramatized thought.
evidence: "always felt that ...", No dialogue; interior stance presented summarically
notes: Habitual feeling (gnomic) rather than in-the-moment thought; included under Humphrey’s broad psycho-narration.

passage: With all those degrees, honours, lectureships between him and the scribblers he suspected instantly an atmosphere not favourable to his queer compound; his prodigious learning and timidity; his wintry charm without cordiality; his innocence blent with snobbery; he quivered if made conscious by a lady’s unkempt hair, a youth’s boots, of an underworld, very creditable doubtless, of rebels, of ardent young people; of would-be geniuses, and intimated with a little toss of the head, with a sniff--Humph!--the value of moderation; of some slight training in the classics in order to appreciate Milton.
explanation: Conventional third-person narration reports Brierly’s inner reactions and attitudes (suspecting an atmosphere; quivering; intimating moderation). The narrator interprets his consciousness rather than ceding the voice to him.
evidence: “he suspected instantly an atmosphere not favourable” → inner evaluation reported by narrator, “he quivered if made conscious by a lady’s unkempt hair” → narrated sensitivity/state, “intimated… with a sniff—Humph!” → narrator rendering mental stance via gesture
notes: Least-internal core type per Humphrey: about consciousness but fully narrator-controlled.

passage: and Hutton (a very bad poet) always felt that Mrs. Dalloway was far the best of the great ladies who took an interest in art.
explanation: Narrator states Hutton’s standing sentiment toward Clarissa. It reports an inner, ongoing evaluation without adopting his idiom.
evidence: “always felt that Mrs. Dalloway was far the best…” → stable attitude reported, Parenthetical “(a very bad poet)” signals narratorial judgment, not Hutton’s voice
notes: Borderline between psycho-narration and omniscient description; included as the latter per Humphrey.

passage: It was odd how strict she was. About music she was purely impersonal. She was rather a prig. But how charming to look at! She made her house so nice if it weren’t for her Professors.
explanation: Third-person rendering closely echoes Hutton’s evaluative, colloquial idiom (prig; exclamation). Reporting tags vanish and the diction feels character-owned.
evidence: Character-colored judgment words: “strict,” “purely impersonal,” “prig”, Exclamatory cadence: “But how charming to look at!” signals internal exclamation in Hutton’s register
notes: If read strictly as narrator-commentary it could be psycho-narration, but the idiom and evaluative staccato favor FID.

passage: Clarissa had half a mind to snatch him off and set him down at the piano in the back room. For he played divinely.
explanation: Clarissa’s impulse (“had half a mind”) is presented with minimal mediation; the following sentence adopts her appreciative tone about his playing.
evidence: “had half a mind to snatch him off” → spontaneous inner intention, “For he played divinely.” → evaluative intensifier characteristic of Clarissa’s taste
notes: Ambiguous with psycho-narration; the immediacy of impulse and evaluative diction suggest FID.

passage: They looked; that was all. That was enough.
explanation: Aphoristic, clipped assessments in Clarissa’s mental idiom about Lord Gayton and Nancy, without reporting tags.
evidence: Elliptical structure: parataxis and repetition (“that was all / That was enough”), Third-person reference to the couple but in character-valuative phrasing

passage: He had his honours, ancestral monuments, banners hanging in the church at home. He had his duties; his tenants; a mother and sisters; had been all day at Lords, and that was what they were talking about--cricket, cousins, the movies--when Mrs. Dalloway came up.
explanation: A mental inventory characteristic of Clarissa, inferring Lord Gayton’s background and what they are discussing; reads as her internal listing rather than neutral narration.
evidence: List-like enumeration of social markers (“honours… tenants… banners…”), Inference of conversation topics (“that was what they were talking about”)
notes: Could be read as omniscient summary; the inferential phrasing and social-catalogue rhythm favor FID.

passage: Lord Gayton liked her most awfully. So did Miss Blow. She had such charming manners.
explanation: Narrator states the pair’s feelings toward Clarissa; interior stance is reported rather than voiced.
evidence: “liked her most awfully. So did Miss Blow.” → reported attitudes, Absence of character-colored idiom; balanced, reportorial syntax

passage: She loved Lords; she loved youth,
explanation: Clarissa’s general preferences are summarized from outside; this is about her dispositions rather than their immediate enactment.
evidence: “She loved Lords; she loved youth” → generalized mental attitude, No character-voice texture; gnomic statement of taste

passage: For the young people could not talk. And why should they? Shout, embrace, swing, be up at dawn; carry sugar to ponies; kiss and caress the snouts of adorable chows; and then all tingling and streaming, plunge and swim.
explanation: Clarissa’s inner commentary on youth rendered in her idiom and punctuated by an imperative-energetic list that enacts the exuberance she imagines.
evidence: Rhetorical question from within: “And why should they?”, Associative, breathless listing (“Shout, embrace, swing… all tingling and streaming”) simulates vitality
notes: Form enacts the affect (exuberance) per Steinberg’s simulation.

passage: But the enormous resources of the English language, the power it bestows, after all, of communicating feelings (at their age, she and Peter would have been arguing all the evening), was not for them. They would solidify young. They would be good beyond measure to the people on the estate, but alone, perhaps, rather dull.
explanation: Third-person free indirect rendering of Clarissa’s judgment about the young; the parenthetical aside is a classic Woolfian thought intrusion.
evidence: Parenthetical self-reference to past (“at their age, she and Peter…”), Character-colored verdicts: “solidify young… rather dull.”

passage: Where had Peter got to? They used to be such friends.
explanation: Rhetorical question and nostalgic assertion in Clarissa’s internal voice, lacking reporting tags.
evidence: Direct question without attribution (“Where had Peter got to?”), Shift to evaluative memory (“They used to be such friends.”)

passage: For at the mention of India, or even Ceylon, her eyes (only one was glass) slowly deepened, became blue, beheld, not human beings--she had no tender memories, no proud illusions about Viceroys, Generals, Mutinies--it was orchids she saw, and mountain passes and herself carried on the backs of coolies in the ’sixties over solitary peaks; or descending to uproot orchids (startling blossoms, never beheld before) which she painted in water-colour; an indomitable Englishwoman, fretful if disturbed by the War, say, which dropped a bomb at her very door, from her deep meditation over orchids and her own figure journeying in the ’sixties in India--but here was Peter.
explanation: Narrator renders Aunt Helena’s inner visual field and reverie triggered by India; subjectivity is described from without, saturated with sensory imagery.
evidence: “it was orchids she saw… and herself carried on the backs of coolies” → interior vision reported, Trigger-based drift (“at the mention of India… her eyes… beheld… orchids”)
notes: Free association through topical trigger (India → orchids → journeys).

passage: “Peter Walsh,” said Clarissa. That meant nothing. Clarissa had asked her. It was tiring; it was noisy; but Clarissa had asked her. So she had come. It was a pity that they lived in London--Richard and Clarissa. If only for Clarissa’s health it would have been better to live in the country. But Clarissa had always been fond of society.
explanation: Aunt Helena’s inward reactions, judgments, and reasons are given in clipped, evaluative sentences characteristic of FID, with no overt reporting verb sequence beyond the initial tag.
evidence: Short evaluative clauses: “That meant nothing. … It was tiring; it was noisy.”, Value judgments and prescriptions (“better to live in the country”)

passage: “He has been in Burma,” said Clarissa. Ah. She could not resist recalling what Charles Darwin had said about her little book on the orchids of Burma.
explanation: The interjection “Ah.” and the immediate drift into recollection reproduce Aunt Helena’s inward response in her momentary voice.
evidence: Interjection “Ah.” marks spontaneous thought, “could not resist recalling…” signals inward turn to memory

passage: (Clarissa must speak to Lady Bruton.)
explanation: Parenthetical aside presents Clarissa’s sudden task-oriented thought; parentheses mark an intrusion of consciousness.
evidence: Parentheses enclose a discrete inner imperative-like plan, Shift in focus mid-conversation to a private intention

passage: She remembered him now. He had been at Bourton (and he had left her, Peter Walsh remembered, without a word in the drawing-room that night when Clarissa had asked him to come boating).
character_pov: Miss Helena Parry; Peter Walsh (parenthetical aside)
explanation: Primary sentence renders Aunt Helena’s recollection (FID/psycho-narration), while the parenthetical interrupts with Peter’s memory. The mode briefly cuts from one mind to another via an orthographic aside.
evidence: “She remembered him now.” → interior recall reported, Parenthetical “Peter Walsh remembered” switches POV mid-sentence
notes: Space-montage-like micro-cut between consciousnesses via parentheses.

passage: (Lady Bruton detested illness in the wives of politicians.)
explanation: A succinct parenthetical reports Lady Bruton’s standing attitude; parentheses flag a quick mental aside layered onto dialogue.
evidence: Parentheses signal aside to main scene, Verbalization of a preference: “detested illness…”

passage: (for she could never think of anything to say to Clarissa; though she liked her. She had lots of fine qualities; but they had nothing in common--she and Clarissa. It might have been better if Richard had married a woman with less charm, who would have helped him more in his work. He had lost his chance of the Cabinet).
explanation: A long parenthetical captures Lady Bruton’s private judgments about Clarissa and Richard in her own evaluative cadence; the parentheses mark stream-like intrusion.
evidence: Evaluative attributions (“never think of anything to say… liked her… nothing in common”), Speculative regret about Richard’s marriage and career

passage: shaking hands with that agreeable sinner, that very able fellow who should have made a name for himself but hadn’t
explanation: Narrative dips into Lady Bruton’s character-colored epithets for Peter Walsh, voicing her inward appraisal in free indirect style.
evidence: Epithets (“that agreeable sinner… that very able fellow”) in character idiom, Counterfactual/critique (“should have made a name… but hadn’t”)
notes: Directly follows the parenthetical; same evaluative stream continues outside parentheses.

passage: (always in difficulties with women)
explanation: A clipped, parenthetical aside voicing Lady Bruton’s habitual verdict on Peter’s private life.
evidence: Parenthetical encapsulates a stock judgment, Telegraphic phrasing without finite verb subject echoes inner aside

passage: but what a tragedy it was--the state of India! The Prime Minister had just been telling her (old Miss Parry huddled up in her shawl, did not care what the Prime Minister had just been telling her), and Lady Bruton would like to have Peter Walsh’s opinion, he being fresh from the centre, and she would get Sir Sampson to meet him, for really it prevented her from sleeping at night, the folly of it, the wickedness she might say, being a soldier’s daughter.
explanation: Lady Bruton’s inward concerns about India, with the parenthetical aside momentarily noting Miss Parry’s indifference; the diction is her urgent, imperial frame of mind.
evidence: Exclamatory valuation: “what a tragedy… the state of India!”, Self-locating rationale: “being a soldier’s daughter… prevented her from sleeping at night”
notes: Parenthetical about Miss Parry is an orthographic intrusion of another’s stance mid-flow.

passage: For she never spoke of England, but this isle of men, this dear, dear land, was in her blood (without reading Shakespeare), and if ever a woman could have worn the helmet and shot the arrow, could have led troops to attack, ruled with indomitable justice barbarian hordes and lain under a shield noseless in a church, or made a green grass mound on some primeval hillside, that woman was Millicent Bruton.
explanation: Her inward mythic self-construction in FID; the parenthetical underscores an instinctive nationalism, and the martial imagery externalizes her self-concept.
evidence: Elevated, self-mythologizing modality (“if ever a woman could have worn the helmet…”), Parenthetical aside: “(without reading Shakespeare)”

passage: To be not English even among the dead--no, no! Impossible!
explanation: An emphatic inner credo in exclamatory form; third-person scene but the utterance belongs to Lady Bruton’s patriotic mind.
evidence: Exclamations (“no, no! Impossible!”) without attribution, Maxim-like formulation of a private belief
notes: Transitional sentence just before shift to Sally; could be read as narratorial summation, but tone and placement suggest LB’s inner voice.

passage: But was it Lady Bruton (whom she used to know)? Was it Peter Walsh grown grey? Lady Rosseter asked herself (who had been Sally Seton).
explanation: Sally’s internal questions recognize old acquaintances; the parenthetical clarifies her prior connection as part of the mental recognition.
evidence: Direct questions untagged (“Was it…?”) mimic interior recognition, Parenthetical “(whom she used to know)” functions as a thought-aside
notes: The trailing identification parenthesis “(who had been Sally Seton)” is narratorial, not SoC.

passage: Never should she forget running along the passage naked, and being sent for by Miss Parry! And Clarissa! oh Clarissa!
explanation: Exclamatory recollection in Sally’s idiom; the breathless vocative enacts the excitement of memory.
evidence: “Never should she forget…” → inward vow-like recollection, Exclamations/vocative: “And Clarissa! oh Clarissa!”

passage: But her voice was wrung of its old ravishing richness; her eyes not aglow as they used to be, when she smoked cigars, when she ran down the passage to fetch her sponge bag, without a stitch of clothing on her, and Ellen Atkins asked, What if the gentlemen had met her? But everybody forgave her (except perhaps Papa).
explanation: Clarissa’s inward gauging of Sally then and now; sensuous recollection shifts into an aside about Papa in parentheses—a thought intrusion.
evidence: Comparative memory framing (“as they used to be… when she smoked cigars…”), Parenthetical aside “(except perhaps Papa)” indicates private qualification

passage: it was bound, Clarissa used to think, to end in some awful tragedy; her death; her martyrdom; instead of which she had married, quite unexpectedly, a bald man with a large buttonhole who owned, it was said, cotton mills at Manchester. And she had five boys!
explanation: Clarissa’s remembered forebodings about Sally (then) contrasted with the surprising actuality; the abrupt “And she had five boys!” mimics internal surprise.
evidence: “Clarissa used to think” signals remembered inner stance, Paratactic fragments (“her death; her martyrdom; … And she had five boys!”)

passage: They were talking: it seemed so familiar--that they should be talking. They would discuss the past. With the two of them (more even than with Richard) she shared her past; the garden; the trees; old Joseph Breitkopf singing Brahms without any voice; the drawing-room wall-paper; the smell of the mats.
explanation: Clarissa’s inward recognition of intimacy with Peter and Sally flows into a sensory catalogue of shared past; parentheses mark intensification of the thought.
evidence: Evaluative presentness (“it seemed so familiar”), Associative list of sense-laden memories (smells, songs, décor)

passage: There were the Bradshaws, whom she disliked. She must go up to Lady Bradshaw (in grey and silver, balancing like a sea-lion at the edge of its tank, barking for invitations, Duchesses, the typical successful man’s wife), she must go up to Lady Bradshaw and say....
explanation: Clarissa’s inward resolve and dislike break into a vivid, satiric parenthetical simile; the form enacts her flash of social irritation.
evidence: Self-commands via FID: “She must go up…”, Parenthetical animal simile (“balancing like a sea-lion… barking for invitations”)
notes: Self-commands verge on soliloquy in FID form.

passage: Why did the sight of him, talking to Richard, curl her up? He looked what he was, a great doctor. A man absolutely at the head of his profession, very powerful, rather worn. For think what cases came before him--people in the uttermost depths of misery; people on the verge of insanity; husbands and wives. He had to decide questions of appalling difficulty. Yet--what she felt was, one wouldn’t like Sir William to see one unhappy. No; not that man.
explanation: Clarissa queries her aversion, then rationalizes Sir William’s authority before returning to her core feeling; rhetorical question and inward verdicts mark FID.
evidence: Opening question from within: “Why did the sight of him… curl her up?”, Private verdicts: “one wouldn’t like Sir William to see one unhappy. No; not that man.”

passage: Clarissa looked at Sir William, talking to Richard. He did not look like a boy--not in the least like a boy. She had once gone with some one to ask his advice. He had been perfectly right; extremely sensible. But Heavens--what a relief to get out to the street again! There was some poor wretch sobbing, she remembered, in the waiting-room. But she did not know what it was--about Sir William; what exactly she disliked. Only Richard agreed with her, “didn’t like his taste, didn’t like his smell.”
explanation: Clarissa’s remembered consultation, visceral relief, and uncertain antipathy are voiced in FID, including a recalled quoted phrase from Richard.
evidence: Interjection: “But Heavens—what a relief…”, Embedded memory-quote: “didn’t like his taste, didn’t like his smell.”

passage: Oh! thought Clarissa, in the middle of my party, here’s death, she thought.
explanation: Narrator tags frame Clarissa’s immediate inward exclamation and formulation (“here’s death”); the exclamation enacts the jolt.
evidence: “Oh! … here’s death” registers as her inner utterance, Double “she thought” framing locates the thought in real time
notes: Though tagged, the central phrase is her unmediated idiom.

passage: What business had the Bradshaws to talk of death at her party? A young man had killed himself. And they talked of it at her party--the Bradshaws talked of death. He had killed himself--but how? Always her body went through it first, when she was told, suddenly, of an accident; her dress flamed, her body burnt. He had thrown himself from a window. Up had flashed the ground; through him, blundering, bruising, went the rusty spikes. There he lay with a thud, thud, thud in his brain, and then a suffocation of blackness. So she saw it.
explanation: Clarissa’s interior protests and visualization of the suicide are rendered in her voice; sensory metaphors and rhythmic repetition simulate shock and horror.
evidence: Rhetorical question: “What business had the Bradshaws…?”, Visceral imagery + onomatopoeia: “thud, thud, thud… suffocation of blackness.”

passage: They went on living (she would have to go back; the rooms were still crowded; people kept on coming). They (all day she had been thinking of Bourton, of Peter, of Sally), they would grow old.
explanation: Clarissa’s thought splits to insert two parenthetical asides—one about her immediate duty, one about the day’s mental preoccupations—before returning to the reflection on aging.
evidence: Dual parentheses marking intrusions of concurrent thoughts, Deictic “They… They…” echoing inner emphasis

passage: A thing there was that mattered; a thing, wreathed about with chatter, defaced, obscured in her own life, let drop every day in corruption, lies, chatter. This he had preserved. Death was defiance. Death was an attempt to communicate; people feeling the impossibility of reaching the centre which, mystically, evaded them; closeness drew apart; rapture faded, one was alone. There was an embrace in death.
explanation: Philosophic sentences articulate Clarissa’s inward formulation about death’s meaning; rhythmic parallelism enacts meditative intensity.
evidence: Character-colored metaphysics (“an attempt to communicate… reaching the centre…”), Anaphoric/repetitive structure (“Death was… Death was…”)

passage: But this young man who had killed himself--had he plunged holding his treasure? “If it were now to die, ’twere now to be most happy,” she had said to herself once, coming down in white. Or there were the poets and thinkers.
explanation: Clarissa’s inner speculation blends to a remembered quotation and invokes poets and thinkers, linking by associative echo rather than logic.
evidence: Hypothetical question from within (“had he plunged holding his treasure?”), Insertion of recalled line without external prompting

passage: Suppose he had had that passion, and had gone to Sir William Bradshaw, a great doctor yet to her obscurely evil, without sex or lust, extremely polite to women, but capable of some indescribable outrage--forcing your soul, that was it--if this young man had gone to him, and Sir William had impressed him, like that, with his power, might he not then have said (indeed she felt it now), Life is made intolerable; they make life intolerable, men like that?
explanation: Extended hypothetical in Clarissa’s mind, culminating in an imagined outcry; the parenthetical heightens immediacy of feeling.
evidence: Counterfactual frame: “Suppose he had had… might he not then have said…”, Parenthetical “(indeed she felt it now)” marks an affective surge

passage: Then (she had felt it only this morning) there was the terror; the overwhelming incapacity, one’s parents giving it into one’s hands, this life, to be lived to the end, to be walked with serenely; there was in the depths of her heart an awful fear. Even now, quite often if Richard had not been there reading the Times, so that she could crouch like a bird and gradually revive, send roaring up that immeasurable delight, rubbing stick to stick, one thing with another, she must have perished.
explanation: Clarissa recounts her periodic terror; parenthetical grounds it temporally and imagery enacts recovery. Syntax and metaphors simulate anxiety and relief.
evidence: Parenthetical time-anchor: “(she had felt it only this morning)”, Somatic/metaphoric enactment: “crouch like a bird… send roaring up… rubbing stick to stick”

passage: But that young man had killed himself. Somehow it was her disaster--her disgrace. It was her punishment to see sink and disappear here a man, there a woman, in this profound darkness, and she forced to stand here in her evening dress. She had schemed; she had pilfered. She was never wholly admirable. She had wanted success. Lady Bexborough and the rest of it.
explanation: Clarissa internalizes the death as her own disgrace and catalogs her moral failings; clipped self-indictments enact the affect of shame.
evidence: Self-accusatory declarations (“her disaster—her disgrace… She had schemed; she had pilfered.”), Deictic immediacy (“here… here in her evening dress”)

passage: Nothing could be slow enough; nothing last too long. No pleasure could equal, she thought, straightening the chairs, pushing in one book on the shelf, this having done with the triumphs of youth, lost herself in the process of living, to find it, with a shock of delight, as the sun rose, as the day sank.
explanation: Clarissa’s inner articulation of mature contentment; syntax and sensory-scene details formally enact serenity and quiet joy.
evidence: Rhythmic anaphora (“Nothing could be… nothing… No pleasure could equal…”), Present-tense sensory frame (“as the sun rose, as the day sank”)

passage: Oh, but how surprising!--in the room opposite the old lady stared straight at her! She was going to bed. And the sky. It will be a solemn sky, she had thought, it will be a dusky sky, turning away its cheek in beauty. But there it was--ashen pale, raced over quickly by tapering vast clouds. It was new to her. The wind must have risen. She was going to bed, in the room opposite. It was fascinating to watch her, moving about, that old lady, crossing the room, coming to the window. Could she see her?
explanation: Clarissa’s inward perception of the sky and the opposite-room woman; the oscillation between sky and figure, with exclamations and questions, renders a moment of fascinated, contemplative awareness.
evidence: Exclamatory and interrogative inner cues (“Oh, but how surprising!… Could she see her?”), Subjective sky imagery (“ashen pale… tapering vast clouds”)

passage: The clock began striking. The young man had killed himself; but she did not pity him; with the clock striking the hour, one, two, three, she did not pity him, with all this going on.
explanation: Clarissa sets her response to the cadence of the clock; the numbered beats and anaphoric phrasing enact a ritualized steadiness in her thought.
evidence: Temporal-rhythmic insertion: “one, two, three”, Anaphora: “she did not pity him… she did not pity him”

passage: There! the old lady had put out her light! the whole house was dark now with this going on, she repeated, and the words came to her, Fear no more the heat of the sun.
explanation: Clarissa’s noticing fuses with a remembered line that surfaces unbidden; the Shakespeare snippet arrives as part of her internal flow.
evidence: Deictic exclamation: “There!” signalling inner pointing, “the words came to her, Fear no more…” → spontaneous memory-quotation

passage: She felt somehow very like him--the young man who had killed himself. She felt glad that he had done it; thrown it away.
explanation: Concise inward identifications register Clarissa’s paradoxical gladness and kinship; the clipped second clause enacts the sensation of release (“thrown it away”).
evidence: Repetition: “She felt… She felt…” foregrounds inner state, Parataxis: “glad that he had done it; thrown it away.”

passage: She must assemble. She must find Sally and Peter.
explanation: Self-directives in FID render Clarissa’s interior resolve to re-enter the party; the rhythmic repetition simulates mustered composure.
evidence: Imperative self-commands expressed via FID (“She must… She must…”), Short, purposeful clauses enact resolution
notes: Gray area with soliloquy (self-address). In FID form (third person), so classified as indirect interior monologue.

passage: Lord, Lord, what a change had come over her! the softness of motherhood; its egotism too.
explanation: Peter’s inward exclamation and evaluative noun-phrases register his startled appraisal of Sally; diction and interjection are character-colored.
evidence: Exclamatory interjection: “Lord, Lord”, Nominal appraisals (“softness of motherhood; its egotism”) without reporting tag

passage: (After all these years he really could not call her “Lady Rosseter.”)
explanation: Parenthetical aside renders Peter's private scruple in his own idiom within third-person narration.
evidence: Parentheses mark an intrusion of thought, Deictic, evaluative phrasing aligned with Peter's perspective (‘really could not’)
notes: Orthographic parenthesis signals consciousness shift; classic Woolf device.

passage: He hadn’t been a success, Sally supposed?
explanation: Rhetorical question framed as Sally's evaluation; third-person frame but Sally's voice dominates.
evidence: Reporting cue ‘Sally supposed’ light but present, Interrogative form enacts her inner musing

passage: For herself, she scarcely ever read the papers. She sometimes saw his name mentioned.
explanation: Third-person but diction and scope clearly limited to Sally's self-assessment; reads as her inward justification.
evidence: Self-referential ‘For herself’ foregrounds her viewpoint, Plain, unadorned register typical of FID in Woolf
notes: Could be read as omniscient psycho-narration; the surrounding FID context supports SoC reading.

passage: But then--well, she lived a very solitary life, in the wilds, Clarissa would say, among great merchants, great manufacturers, men, after all, who did things. She had done things too!
explanation: Associative, self-interrupting cadence (‘But then--well’) and self-assertion render Sally’s inner justification in her idiom.
evidence: Discourse markers (‘But then--well’) mimic thinking, Self-assertive exclamation ‘She had done things too!’

passage: Lord, Lord, what a change had come over her! the softness of motherhood; its egotism too.
explanation: Exclamatory evaluation voiced in Peter’s register; the clipped appositions enact his quick, appraising inner reaction.
evidence: Exclamation ‘Lord, Lord’ signals unspoken reaction, Paratactic fragments ‘the softness of motherhood; its egotism too’

passage: Heavens, he had wept!
explanation: Peter’s idiomatic exclamation recalls an emotional memory; the interjection simulates his felt mortification.
evidence: Interjection ‘Heavens’, Exclamatory brevity typical of inner recoil

passage: That was his old trick, opening a pocket-knife, thought Sally, always opening and shutting a knife when he got excited.
explanation: Narrated thought with explicit tag conveys Sally’s private observation about Peter’s nervous habit.
evidence: Reporting verb ‘thought Sally’, Content is evaluative and unspoken (‘old trick’)

passage: Why not call Richard “Wickham”?
explanation: Rhetorical self-justification in Sally’s voice, free of narratorial explanation.
evidence: Direct rhetorical question with character-specific reference, Follows Sally-focused recollection

passage: and he was to write, surely? In those days he was to write.
explanation: FID renders Sally’s half-questioning recollection of Peter’s old ambitions.
evidence: Tagless interrogative ‘surely?’ expresses inner doubt, Repetitive phrasing mimics recollective emphasis
notes: Leading conjunction ‘and’ shows continuation of Sally’s inward chain.

passage: But who was this Rosseter?
explanation: Peter’s inward question about Sally’s husband presented without reporting verb.
evidence: Bare interrogative in third person context, Anchored to Peter by immediately surrounding sentences

passage: Oh yes, Sally remembered; she had it still, a ruby ring which Marie Antoinette had given her great-grandfather.
explanation: Narrator reports Sally’s memory content rather than immersing us in it directly.
evidence: Reporting of memory state ‘Sally remembered’, Conventional past-tense narration of mental content

passage: But going to Bourton had meant so much to her--had kept her sane, she believed, so unhappy had she been at home.
explanation: Narrator summarizes Sally’s belief about the past; interior content reported from outside.
evidence: Attribution ‘she believed’ signals psycho-narration, Balanced syntax, not mimicking raw thought

passage: And the marriage had been, Sally supposed, a success?
explanation: Sally’s inner speculation framed as a question with light narratorial tag.
evidence: Interrogative form, Light reporting cue ‘Sally supposed’

passage: (She was like a poplar, she was like a river, she was like a hyacinth, Willie Titcomb was thinking. Oh how much nicer to be in the country and do what she liked! She could hear her poor dog howling, Elizabeth was certain.)
explanation: Parenthetical cuts into two minds during the party, giving Willie’s simile-laden perception and Elizabeth’s private wish/concern.
evidence: Parentheses signal sudden interior asides, Explicit mental-state cues ‘was thinking’ / ‘was certain’, Rapid shift between two POVs (montage)
notes: Single aside juxtaposes two consciousnesses; treated as one montage passage.

passage: But--did Peter understand?--she lacked something. Lacked what was it?
explanation: Interrupted syntax and direct questions render Sally’s inward evaluation of Clarissa.
evidence: Em-dash interruptions enact thought process, Bare interrogative ‘Lacked what was it?’

passage: (and she felt that Peter was an old friend, a real friend--did absence matter? did distance matter? She had often wanted to write to him, but torn it up, yet felt he understood, for people understand without things being said, as one realises growing old, and old she was, had been that afternoon to see her sons at Eton, where they had the mumps)
explanation: A long parenthetical stream that drifts associatively through self-questions and memories; clearly Sally’s inward flow.
evidence: Parentheses mark deviation into interior, Chain of rhetorical questions and associative leaps (old friend → letters → aging → Eton visit)

passage: to be quite frank then, how could Clarissa have done it?--married Richard Dalloway? a sportsman, a man who cared only for dogs.
explanation: Sally’s inward incredulity voiced as questions and appositive judgments.
evidence: Series of interrogatives, Appositive, judgmental phrases ‘a sportsman… cared only for dogs’

passage: Literally, when he came into the room he smelt of the stables.
explanation: Sensory judgment in Sally’s idiom; hyperbolic ‘Literally’ flags her subjective perception.
evidence: Evaluator ‘Literally’ marks character’s emphasis, Concrete sensory image tied to disdain

passage: And then all this?
explanation: Elliptical, deictic complaint capturing Sally’s unspoken gesture of dismissal.
evidence: Deictic ‘all this’ relies on Sally’s situational viewpoint, Elliptical interrogative form

passage: and really she hadn’t the courage--so that was Hugh! the admirable Hugh!
explanation: FID registers Sally’s reluctance and ironic appraisal of Hugh’s reputation.
evidence: Self-assessing clause ‘really she hadn’t the courage’, Exclamatory taglines ‘the admirable Hugh!’ in character idiom

passage: He looked younger, she thought, than any of them.
explanation: Narration flags Sally’s evaluative perception explicitly with ‘she thought’.
evidence: Reporting of thought with explicit tag, Conventional syntax (psycho-narration)

passage: but how could that be? Sally wondered; what did he mean? and how odd it was to know him and yet not know a single thing that had happened to him. And did he say it out of pride? Very likely,
explanation: A string of rhetorical questions and quick self-answers presents Sally’s ongoing inner inquiry.
evidence: Multiple interrogatives without quotation, Self-supplied answer ‘Very likely,’ shows inward dialectic

passage: (And so she would go on, Peter felt, hour after hour; the miner’s son; people thought she had married beneath her; her five sons; and what was the other thing--plants, hydrangeas, syringas, very, very rare hibiscus lilies that never grow north of the Suez Canal, but she, with one gardener in a suburb near Manchester, had beds of them, positively beds! Now all that Clarissa had escaped, unmaternal as she was.)
explanation: Parenthetical aside gives Peter’s inner, slightly mocking list-like stream about Sally’s themes.
evidence: Parentheses mark interior aside, List-like associative catalog of topics and plants

passage: A snob was she? Yes, in many ways.
explanation: Compressed evaluative question-and-answer in Peter’s voice.
evidence: Elliptical inversion ‘A snob was she?’, Immediate self-answer ‘Yes, in many ways.’
notes: Could be narratorial summary of consensus; context and idiom suggest Peter’s interior verdict.

passage: Where was she, all this time? It was getting late.
explanation: Rhetorical questions mark Peter’s inward anxious watch for Clarissa.
evidence: Time-awareness ‘It was getting late’ tied to desire, Unquoted interrogative addressed to no one

passage: but who was that? that lady standing by the curtain all the evening, without speaking? He knew her face; connected her with Bourton. Surely she used to cut up underclothes at the large table in the window? Davidson, was that her name?
explanation: A sequence of inward questions and memory-groping in Peter’s idiom.
evidence: Series of short interrogatives without reporting tags, Associative recall ‘connected her with Bourton’→specific memory

passage: Peter would think her sentimental. So she was. For she had come to feel that it was the only thing worth saying--what one felt. Cleverness was silly. One must say simply what one felt.
explanation: FID articulates Sally’s inward credo in simple, aphoristic sentences that perform her sincerity.
evidence: Prediction of another’s judgment (‘Peter would think…’) is inner anticipation, Short, declarative maxims enact felt simplicity

passage: Poor Peter, thought Sally. Why did not Clarissa come and talk to them? That was what he was longing for. She knew it. All the time he was thinking only of Clarissa, and was fidgeting with his knife.
explanation: Tagged thought opens into Sally’s inward reading of Peter’s desire and behavior.
evidence: ‘thought Sally’ marks entrance to her mind, Attribution of Peter’s inner state (‘what he was longing for’)
notes: Borders on psycho-narration of Peter via Sally’s perspective.

passage: (They had been so intimate--he and Sally Seton, it was absurd not to say it.)
explanation: Parenthetical self-correction registers Sally’s inward acknowledgement of their intimacy.
evidence: Parentheses indicate aside, Evaluative remark ‘absurd not to say it’

passage: Still, it is better to have loved (but he would think her sentimental--he used to be so sharp).
explanation: Sally’s inward maxim interrupted by a parenthetical anticipation of Peter’s critique.
evidence: Gnome-like statement in FID (‘it is better to have loved’), Parenthetical anticipates another’s response (inner dialogue)

passage: Sally was positive of that.
explanation: Narrator states Sally’s conviction about Clarissa’s feelings; reports mental attitude succinctly.
evidence: Attribution of certainty ‘was positive’, No mimetic features of thought rhythm
notes: Minimalist psycho-narration; included for exhaustiveness.

passage: (Sally should not have said that--she went too far).
explanation: Parenthetical captures Peter’s immediate, silent censure of Sally’s remark.
evidence: Parentheses denote aside, Second-person reference to Sally as subject of judgment

passage: That good fellow--there he was at the end of the room, holding forth, the same as ever, dear old Richard.
explanation: Endearments and deictic pointing render Peter’s inward, affectionate appraisal of Richard.
evidence: Vocatives ‘That good fellow… dear old Richard’, Deictic ‘there he was’ rooted in Peter’s gaze

passage: How unlike Clarissa at her age! Could he make anything of her? She would not open her lips.
explanation: Unquoted evaluative questions and statements in Sally’s register about Elizabeth.
evidence: Rhetorical questions without reporting tag, Comparative judgment ‘How unlike Clarissa’
notes: Ambiguity with spoken conversation; tone and form favor FID.

passage: (and really she must go, if Clarissa did not come soon)
explanation: Parenthetical inner resolve about leaving; clearly not spoken.
evidence: Parentheses mark inner aside, Conditional thought about immediate action

passage: (Sally was fifty-five, in body, she said, but her heart was like a girl’s of twenty)
explanation: Parenthetical self-description voiced in Sally’s idiom, with simile expressing subjective age.
evidence: Parentheses indicate consciousness aside, Simile ‘like a girl’s of twenty’ expresses inner feeling

passage: For her father had been looking at her, as he stood talking to the Bradshaws, and he had thought to himself, Who is that lovely girl? And suddenly he realised that it was his Elizabeth, and he had not recognised her, she looked so lovely in her pink frock!
explanation: Narrator recounts Richard’s moment of inward recognition, including his unspoken question.
evidence: ‘he had thought to himself’ explicitly reports thought, Embedded question ‘Who is that lovely girl?’

passage: Elizabeth had felt him looking at her as she talked to Willie Titcomb.
explanation: Narrator states Elizabeth’s internal perception of being looked at.
evidence: ‘had felt’ reports inner sensation, No direct mimetic rendering of thought

passage: That did make her happy. But her poor dog was howling.
explanation: Narrator summarizes Elizabeth’s feeling and concurrent intrusive concern about her dog.
evidence: Emotion reported (‘make her happy’), Topic shift to ‘her poor dog’ signals inner preoccupation
notes: Ambiguous whether the second sentence is FID or psycho-narration; content clearly interior.

passage: What is this terror? what is this ecstasy? he thought to himself. What is it that fills me with extraordinary excitement? It is Clarissa, he said.
explanation: Peter’s inward questions and answer rendered almost directly, with brief tags; rhythm and repetition enact his heightened state.
evidence: Series of unquoted rhetorical questions, Light reporting tags ‘he thought to himself… he said’ amid predominantly interior phrasing
notes: Borders on direct interior monologue; tags keep it in indirect mode while form simulates rapture.
